title: Electrical Appliances — BCG
prompt: Size the market for ACs in Delhi.
solution:
What do you mean by size the market, do you want it in units or rupee value?
Rupee value.
What do you mean by ACs, commercials or personal?
Both.
What type of AC, window or split?
All types. What do you mean by Delhi? Does it include NCR?
Include NCR.
What prices range? Do you want to include everything that counts as AC including centralized AC?
All.
To calculate the market, I will begin by dividing the consumption into two parts - Household and Commercial. For households, starting with the population of Delhi, which is about 30 million. AC is a household thing. Divide the population by 4, since the average
family size is 4. So, number of households = 7.5 million. Guesstimate - Since all do not own ACs, I’ll do an income split. 30% poor, 50% middle class, 20% rich. Further, the middle class can be divided into lower and upper
middle class.
Probability that poor households will own ACs - 0%
Probability that lower middle class households will own at least one AC - 50-60%
Probability that upper middle class households will own at least one AC - 90%
Probability that rich households will own ACs - 100%
From this, we get the average number of ACs in each income type household -
Poor - Lower Middle Class - Upper Middle Class - 1-2 (so taking average 1.5)
Rich - Multiplying this, and approximately total number of ACs. = 9 million. We have the number of household ACs. To calculate the value, I will simply
multiply the number of ACs with the average value of each AC.
Take the average to be 20000.
So, multiplying, this is the market size of ACs if people don’t have ACs already. But since people already have ACs, I need to further calculate. I will now calculate how many are such who probably don’t have an AC or want to replace an AC, they are the actual market. Guesstimate - Taking the average lifetime of an AC to be 9 years. I will take the total number of ACs and divide it by the average lifetime, 9 million/9 = 1 million
Every year there will be some people who will enter the market. This is the year on year growth rate. I will take it to be 10%.
Adding this 10% of 9 million to the 1 million obtained earlier, I get the actual size of the AC market (in units) to be 1.9 million. Lastly, I can multiply this
number with the value of each AC to get the rupee value.
Now, to calculate the market size of commercial ACs, this is the approach I will take - First calculate office use by using number of people working in
offices. Most offices have centralized ACs so I will calculate the office size, space occupied by one person and so on.
Alright. We can conclude the guesstimate here.

title: Beverages — Accenture
prompt: Estimate the number of cups of tea consumed in Delhi NCR in a month.
solution:
Can I assume the population of Delhi NCR to be around 71 million?
Fair assumption, you may proceed.
I’ll split the population into urban and rural: 60% urban, 40% rural.
That gives us roughly 47 million urban and 24 million rural.
Next, I’d like to segment the population by age group, since tea consumption varies with age.
I’ll use:
0–15 years: 30%
15–30 years: 30%
30–60 years: 30%
60+ years: 10%
Yes, looks good to me.
Applying this to the urban population:
0–15: ~14 million
15–30: ~14 million
30–60: ~14 million Guesstimate - 60+: ~5 million And to the rural population:
0–15: ~7 million
15–30: ~7 million
30–60: ~7 million
60+: ~3 million
Now I’ll assume average daily tea consumption by age group:
0–15: 0 cups/day
15–30: 1.5 cups/day
30–60: 2.5 cups/day
60+: 1 cup/day
You may go ahead.
So for urban areas (monthly consumption over 30 days):
15–30: 14 * 1.5 * 30 = 630 million cups
30–60: 14 * 2.5 * 30 = 1050 million cups
60+: 5 * 1 * 30 = 150 million cups
→
Total: 1830 million cups Guesstimate - And for rural areas: 15–30: 7 * 1.5 * 30 = 315 million cups
30–60: 7 * 2.5 * 30 = 525 million cups
60+: 3 * 1 * 30 = 90 million cups
→
Total: 930 million cups
I’d now like to adjust for non-tea drinkers—assume 20% don’t drink tea for personal or health reasons.
That’s reasonable, go ahead.
So:
Urban: 1830 * 0.8 = 1464 million cups
Rural: 930 * 0.8 = 744 million cups
Final estimate:
Total = 1464 + 744 = 2208 million cups/month
→
Roughly 2.2 billion cups of tea consumed monthly in Delhi NCR.
Great, I appreciate your efforts. Thank you!

title: Beverages — Praxis Global Alliance
prompt: Estimate the number of Coca-Cola bottles sold during a cricket match.
solution:
To proceed, I’ll make some assumptions.
Let’s assume:
Type of match: T20 (shortest format, high crowd turnout)
Location: India
Venue: Narendra Modi Stadium, Ahmedabad (largest in the country)
Match type: International match (e.g., India vs Australia)
Seating capacity: ~132,
→
Occupancy for high-profile T20: ~90% ~118,000 attendees
Now, not everyone will purchase a Coca-Cola. Based on stadium crowd behavior, let’s break it down:
→
20% of attendees are children/teens, more likely to buy ~23,
→
50% are adults likely to buy due to heat, excitement, or habit ~59,
→
Let’s assume 40% of total attendees purchase a Coca-Cola bottle
→
118,000 × 40% = 47,200 bottles
Reasonable. Any further breakdown?
Sure. Some people may purchase more than one. Let’s assume:
90% buy 1 bottle = 42,
10% buy 2 bottles = 4,720 * 2 = 9,
Total bottles sold = 42,480 + 9,440 = ~51,920 bottles

title: QSR — Accenture
prompt: Estimate the monthly sales of Domino's outlets in North Delhi
solution:
Alright. I’d like to understand the problem a little better. How many Domino's outlets are we assuming in North Delhi?
Sure, 20 to 22.
Should I arrive at the sales in INR or number of pizzas?
For this case, let’s estimate the number of pizzas sold.
Great. Should I assume standard working hours?
Standard working hours would be 12 hours.
Sure, should I consider seasonality in the variation of demand?
Ignore it for the time being.
I will first estimate the daily sales. In order to do so, I am dividing the 12 hour day into 5 peak hours and 7 non-peak hours. Each order requires minutes to receive and 15 minutes to process. I’m also assuming that 6 orders can be processed at once.
Sure. That seems reasonable.
Alright. Then, in the 5 peak hours, numbers of orders per hour is 18 (60*6/20). Can I go ahead and assume that in the non-peak hours, the number of
orders per hour is 60% of peak hours?
Alright. Guesstimate - Then, total number of orders in a day comes out to be approximately 166 (18*5 +18*0.6*7). Estimating the number of pizzas per order to be 1.5, the
sales in terms of number of pizzas equals 250 per day.
Total monthly sales from all outlets = 1,65,000 (250*30*22)
I have also taken some externalities into account:
If working hours are around 14-16 hours, revenue could be boosted by 10-15%. Midnight snacking is a legitimate consumer behaviour and could
lead to such boosts.
Seasonality: During festival season, families come together, and there might be discounts and vouchers, which means more people coming in,
leading to more sales.
Competition: Presence of a competitive firm like Pizza Hut or La Pinoz, unless customers have a very strong preference for Domino's.
Alright, thank you.

title: Capability — Bain Capability Network
prompt: Estimate the total number of tissues used in a day in Delhi.
solution:
To begin with, I would like to clarify two things: What kind of a day - whether it is a weekend/weekday/any festival in particular?
Secondly, for what all uses- in homes/for personal use or in corporate setting or in public places?
It is a regular weekday and you have to estimate for all kinds of uses. Specific numbers aren’t required but how would you approach the problem?
We can primarily look at personal use vs non-personal use.
Personal use covers:
At-home use: tissues used in kitchens, bathrooms, and for general hygiene
On-the-go use: tissues people carry with them during commutes or day-to-day activities
The population of Delhi is roughly 2 crores. Using a weighted average of income groups we can arrive at roughly 2 tissues per person, that would make
for around 4 crore tissues daily.
INCOME GROUP % OF POPULATION Avg Tissue Use/Day
Low Income 45% 0.
Middle Income 45% High income 10% Guesstimate - For non personal use, it would primarily include office spaces, eateries and hospitals.
Total Tissues per Day = Σ [No. of Locations x Avg Footfall per Day x Avg Tissues per Person]
We can use a weighted average to find the average footfall per day.
Alright, sounds good.

title: Electronics — Bain Capability Network
prompt: How many models of Samsung phone are sold on Amazon every year?
solution:
To estimate the annual sales for a Samsung phone on Amazon, we can use the following approach:
First, we will calculate the Sales which is:
Number of people who use mobile phones * 0.25 (considering the durability on average is 4 years hence, 1/4) * 0.33 (Since, Samsung is among the
top brands so every third person uses Samsung, thus 1/3=0.33) * 0.2 (As, there are five models among Samsung, so 1/5=0.2) * Percentage of people
who buy from Amazon.
Now, we will calculate the number of people who use mobile phones which can be estimated as follows: We will consider the population of India (140cr), and divide it further on the basis of people living in Urban (30% of 140cr) and Rural (70% of 140cr)
areas. We will further divide Urban and Rural into 4 categories which is: Upper, Upper Middle, Lower Middle and Lower.
For people living in Urban areas - Upper Middle, 60% of people will fall in the age group that uses mobile phones and we assume a 100% internet
penetration rate here, for other income categories in urban areas we may take a 70% penetration rate
For people living in Rural areas, it can be divided as Rural-Upper & Upper Middle , Here 40% of people lie in the age group that uses mobile phones, Since, people living in Rural areas are not very well equipped with the concept of internet and technology, we assume a 50% internet penetration
rate.
We can make the calculations as follows: Guesstimate - Estimation of Mobile users:
Urban Population: (30% of 140 crore = 42 crore)
For, Upper & Upper Middle Class (30% of Urban) = 12.6 crore; 60% are in the mobile-using age group = 7.56 crore. Since, Internet penetration rate is
100% , therefore number of users = 7.56 crore users
For, Lower Middle & Lower Class (70% of Urban) = 29.4 crore; 40% are in the mobile-using age group = 11.76 crore, Since, Internet penetration rate is
70% , therefore number of users= 8.23 crore users
Hence, Total Urban Mobile Users = 7.56 + 8.23 = 15.79 crore
Rural Population (70% of 140 crore = 98 crore)
We consider only Upper & Upper Middle Class (20% of Rural) = 19.6 crore (since lower classes may not frequently buy smartphones online).
40% are in the mobile-using age group = 7.84 crore, since Internet penetration rate is 50% , hence number of users are 3.92 crore users Hence, Total Rural Mobile Users = 3.92 crore
Therefore, Total Mobile Users in India = 15.79 (Urban) + 3.92 (Rural) = 19.71 crore
Estimation of Sales:
Annual smartphone buyers = 19.71 crore * 0.25 = 4.93 crore; Samsung buyers per year = 4.93 crore * 0.33 = 1.63 crore;
Per-model sales = 1.63 crore / 5 = 32.6 lakh units/year; Samsung model sales on Amazon = 32.6 lakh * 0.20 = 6.52 lakh units/year per model Therefore, annually around 6.5 lakh Samsung phones per model are sold on Amazon
Alright, thank you!

title: QSR — BCG
prompt: If you are a regular Starbucks consumer, what is your lifetime consumer value? Take assumptions for this.
solution:
I have taken the following assumptions:
I am a working individual with a five-day work week, and would have at least three times a week.
I work from the ages of 20 to 50.
Taking a regular order worth 300 Rs., ordering 3 times a week translates to 12 times a month, leading to about 144 times a year. So :
Lifetime number of years = Total expenditure= 300 * 30 * 144= 1296 There are certain variations
I may not necessarily order three times a week, every single week. I may order twice a week or maybe even twice in a single day.
There is a certain discounted value of the customer lifetime expenditure to be accounted for, as there is a certain loss of money as time
progresses.
Also, to get the customer lifetime value, we should use the formula for the Present value of the annuity Alright, thank you

title: Capability — Bain Capability Network
prompt: Estimate the monthly revenue of a college café.
solution:
Are there any other food outlets on campus?
No, the café is the sole food outlet on campus.
Is this estimation for a specific month, such as during a festival or holiday season?
No, consider a regular month.
Understood. How many days a week is the café operational?
It operates six days a week. Thank you.
To estimate the café’s monthly revenue, I’ll use the following approach:
Monthly Revenue = Average Spend per Visit × Total Number of Visits per Month.
To estimate the total number of visits, I will begin by analyzing campus demographics. Assume there are 400 students per batch, resulting in a total of 1,200 students across three years. Of these:
25% (300 students) are hostellers
75% (900 students) are day scholars or live in nearby paying guest accommodations Guesstimate - Due to access to a hostel mess, hostellers are likely to visit the café less frequently. I will assume they visit the café twice a week. In contrast, day
scholars are more dependent on the café and are assumed to visit four times a week.
Estimated visits per month:
Hostellers:
300 students × 2 visits/week × 4 weeks = 2,400 visits
Day scholars:
900 students × 4 visits/week × 4 weeks = 14,400 visits
Additionally, let’s account for faculty and non-teaching staff.
Assume there are approximately 200 individuals in this group, each visiting the café once a week:
200 × 1 visit/week × 4 weeks = 800 visits
Total estimated visits per month = 2,400 (hostellers) + 14,400 (day scholars) + 800 (faculty/staff) = 17,600 visits
Next, I will estimate the average spend per person per visit. Assume:
Average cost of a beverage = ₹
Average cost of a food item = ₹
If we assume that each visitor purchases one beverage and one food item, the:
Average spend per visit = ₹20 + ₹40 = ₹
Using the previously calculated 17,600 visits per month, the: Estimated monthly revenue = ₹60 × 17,600 = ₹1,056,
Therefore, the estimated monthly revenue of the college café is ₹1,056,000 during a regular month.
Thank you, that will be all.

title: Aviation — Trinity Lifesciences
prompt: Estimate the quantity of paint required to paint an aircraft.
solution:
What type of aircraft are we considering—commercial, private, or military?
It's a commercial Boeing 737.
How many coats of paint are required? Should we focus solely on the exterior, or include the interior as well?
Assume two coats, only the exterior.
What is the coverage area of the paint per litre ? Do we need to account for areas like windows and other small parts?
Use the standard coverage rate similar to household paints. You can ignore those for this estimation.
Understood. I’ll start by estimating the surface area of the exterior. A Boeing 737's main body resembles a cylinder with conical sections at the front
and rear, plus wings:
Main Body (Cylindrical Section): Surface Area: 2πrh
To calculate the length: There are around 30 rows with a seating + leg space of around 3 feet + 10% margin for washrooms = 30*3 + 10%*30*3 =
100 feet
Diameter: 6 columns( 6*1.5 ft) + aisle(3 ft) = 12 ft
Radius (r): 12/2 = 6 feet
Surface Area: 2πrh = 2 * π * 6 * 100 ≈ 3800 sq ft Guesstimate - Nose and Tail (Conical Sections): Length: 1/4th of main body = 25 ft appx .
Average Slant Height (l): root r2 + h2 = root (625+36) ≈ 25 ft
Surface Area: πrl = π * 6 * 25 ≈ 470 sq ft per cone.
Total for Both Cones: 2 * 470 = 940 sq ft
Wings:
We can assume them to be two triangular structures
Let’s skip the calculation for wings.
Okay. Total surface area (2 coats): (3800 + 940) * 2 ≈ 9500 sq ft
Alright
Now, I’ll estimate the paint coverage per litre using the standard rate for household paints.
Based on personal observation, a 20-litre paint bucket covers two small rooms with a double coat.
Each room has 4 walls + ceiling = 5 surfaces. Assuming wall height and width as 10 ft:
→
Area = 5 * 10 * 10 = 500 sq ft per room 2 rooms = 1000 sq ft
→
Double coat = 2000 sq ft per 20 litres Coverage = 100 sq ft/litre
Using this for our plane gives the total paint to be 9500/100 = 95 litres of paint excluding wings, wastage, overlapping, etc
Your approach is logical and well-structured, but real-world paint usage may be higher due to surface texture and application methods.
Thank you for the insight. I'll consider such factors in future estimations.

title: Cab Service — Praxis Global Alliance
prompt: The client wants to start an EV taxi service from Bangalore Airport to Bangalore City. What would you suggest as the initial fleet size?
solution:
This requires two estimates—
1.The number of people arriving at Bangalore Airport daily
2.The number likely to opt for EV taxis
I’ll begin with estimating airport footfall. Can I assume that approximately 50,000 people arrive daily?
How did you arrive at that number?
Let's start with flight capacity and frequency.
→
Assume 1 flight lands every 10 minutes per runway 6 flights/hour
Round it to 5 flights/hour for simplicity
→
Assume 4 runways 20 flights/hour total
Average aircraft capacity: 200 passengers
Now, let’s break the day into three segments:
Peak (8 hours): 4 runways 5 flights * 200 passengers * 8 hours = 32,
Average (8 hours): 4 * 3 * 200 * 8 = 19,
Non-peak (8 hours): 4 * 2 * 200 ×=* 8 = 12,
Total arrivals/day = 32,000 + 19,200 + 12,800 = ~64,
Assuming 14,000 are transit passengers, net arriving passengers = ~50, Guesstimate - That works. What next?

Let's now estimate the number of passengers choosing EV taxis. Options available include:
Buses
Metro
Private vehicles
Normal and electric cabs
Considering cost, convenience, and awareness, I assume ~1,000 people/day may opt for EV taxis.
To serve that demand efficiently, I recommend:
Fleet size: 500 EVs, assuming 2 trips/day per vehicle with buffer for downtime.
Thank you!

title: Unknown — BCG
prompt: I would like you to estimate the number of electric vehicles (EVs) sold in India in a year. You can take any approach you find suitable.
solution:
Good morning. Before I begin, I would like to ask a few clarifying questions to narrow down the scope of the problem.
Sure, go ahead.
Are we estimating the number of EVs across all vehicle types, or should I focus on a particular segment like cars, two-wheelers, or commercial
vehicles?
Good question. Let's limit our scope to electric cars only.
Understood. I’ll approach the problem by starting with the total population of India and narrowing it down to the potential customer base. Does that
sounIdN reDasUonaSbTle?RY
Yes, please go ahead.
The population of India is around 1.4 billion. I'll now split this population into rural and urban segments. Assuming approximately 70% of the
population resides in rural areas and 30% in urban areas, I would exclude the rural population given that electric cars are relatively less accessible
and affordable in rural India. Would you agree with this approach? Yes, that seems reasonable.
So, the urban population would be roughly 30% of 1.4 billion, which is around 420 million people. Now, I'll segment this population based on income
levels: poor, lower middle class, middle class, upper middle class, and upper class. Since the average electric car costs around INR 25 lakh, I would
exclude all groups below the upper class as they might not be able to afford such vehicles. Does that sound like a fair assumption? Guesstimate - I agree. However, would you consider including a small percentage of the upper middleI NclaDsUs sSinTcRe Ysome of themD mIFayF IhCavUe LthTeY financial
capacity or aspiration to buy an EV?
That’s a valid point. To simplify, I will primarily focus on the upper class but acknowledge that a small percentage of upper middle-class households
may also contribute. For now, I'll proceed with the upper class.
Sure, go ahead.
Assuming the upper class forms around 10% of the urban population, that would be 10% of 420 million, which is around 42 million people. Now, I'll
convert this population into households. Assuming an average household size of 4 members, we would have approximately 10.5 million upper-class
households.
Agreed. What's next?
Next, I would estimate the average number of cars per household. For upper-class families, I would assume they own around 2 cars on average. This
would imply around 21 million cars owned by the upper class.
Sounds reasonable. Please continue.
Now, I’ll estimate the proportion of households willing to switch to electric vehicles. Considering factors like awareness, infrastructure challenges, and
accessibility, I would assume that around 40% of households would consider switching to an electric vehicle.
How did you arrive at 40%?
I made the assumption based on a few factors:
Awareness and Accessibility: Some people may not yet be aware of the benefits of electric cars or may not have the access
Infrastructure: Charging stations may not be readily available in many urban locations Guesstimate - Fair enough. Please proceed. So, 40% of 21 million cars would be approximately 8.4 million cars. However, not everyone who considers switching will actually do so. Factoring in
constraints like higher upfront costs, uncertainty, or lack of charging infrastructure, I would assume only around 20% of these potential customers
would finally purchase an electric car.
What’s your logic behind 20%?
Some people may delay their purchase decision until infrastructure improves, some may be hesitant about resale value, and others may still prefer
traditional internal combustion engine vehicles. Hence, I’m being conservative with a 20% conversion rate.
Fair point. What’s your final calculation?
20% of 8.4 million would be approximately 1.7 million cars. So, my final estimate for the number of electric vehicles sold in India annually would be
around 1.7 million units.
Excellent work. Thank you.

title: Capability — Bain Capability Network
prompt: Your task is to estimate the weekly revenue of a petrol pump in South Delhi.
solution:
Great, thanks. To begin, can I clarify — is this a typical urban petrol pump?
Yes, it's a standard urban petrol pump located in South Delhi.
Understood. Does the pump sell both petrol and diesel?
Yes, you can assume it sells both.
Got it. Are we considering only fuel sales, or should I include auxiliary services like a convenience store and air filling?
Include auxiliary services like store sales, air filling, and pollution check if relevant. Perfect. I’ll break the estimation into a few steps:
1.Estimate total weekly vehicle traffic, segmented into weekdays and weekends
2.Break that down by vehicle type
3.Estimate the average fuel purchase per vehicle type
4.Calculate fuel volume and revenue 5.Add auxiliary revenue streams
Sounds like a solid structure. Start with step 1.
Based on traffic patterns in South Delhi, I’ll assume the petrol pump serves: Guesstimate - Weekdays: ~1,200 vehicles per day Weekends: ~800 vehicles per day
So weekly traffic = (1,200 * 5) + (800 * 2) = 8,000 vehicles/week
Okay. How will you segment the vehicle types?
Since you mentioned a typical urban location, I’ll assume:
→
2-Wheelers: 60% 4,800/week
→
4-Wheelers: 30% 2,400/week
→
Trucks/LCVs: 10% 800/week
How will you estimate the fuel volume sold?
I’ll do this by estimating the average litres purchased per visit by each vehicle type, then multiplying by the number of vehicles:
For 2-wheelers, the average is low — maybe ₹400 worth per refill, which is roughly 4 litres.
4-wheelers might buy around ₹2,000 worth per visit — I’ll assume 20 litres.
Trucks, being diesel-heavy and often refilling fully, can go up to ₹7,000–₹8,000 per visit. I’ll estimate 80 litres.
Alright. Apply that.
2W: 4,800 * 4 = 19,200 litres
4W: 2,400 * 20 = 48,000 litres
Trucks: 800 * 80 = 64,000 litres
Total fuel volume = 131,200 litres/week Guesstimate - Assuming the average fuel price is ₹96/litre: Fuel Revenue = 131,200 * 96 = ₹1.26 crore/week
That gives us the weekly fuel revenue. Now how would you factor in auxiliary income?
I’ll break it down into three key streams:
1.Convenience store sales
2.Air filling and pollution checks
3.Lubricants and engine oil top-ups
Alright. Let’s start with the convenience store.
Sure. For an average petrol pump in a busy area like South Delhi, the convenience store might see decent footfall from people waiting for fuel or
stopping briefly. I’ll estimate around ₹15,000/day in sales.
That’s ₹15,000 * 7 = ₹1.05 lakh/week
Okay. What about air filling and PUC checks?
These are low-ticket but high-frequency services. I’ll estimate:
Air and water: ₹3,000/day
PUC (Pollution Under Control): ₹2,000/day
Combined: ₹5,000/day × 7 = ₹35,000/week
And lubricants? Guesstimate - Sales of engine oil, coolant, etc. could be around ₹5,000/day, especially from frequent drivINerDs liUkeS tTruRckYers or cab drDiveIrFsF. ICULTY
So that’s ₹35,000/week as well.
The auxiliary revenue comes to roughly ₹1.75 lakh per week. Added to the fuel sales — which were ₹1.26 crore — the total weekly revenue comes to
around ₹1.28 crore.
That’s well-structured. Any final comments?
Yes — this is a top-line estimate. For more accuracy, I’d look at the petrol-to-diesel ratio, split by vehicle type, and seasonal fluctuations. Also,
margins on fuel are quite slim, so if we were assessing profitability, the auxiliary services would play a much bigger role.
Makes sense. Good job.

title: Capability — Bain Capability Network
prompt: Can you estimate how many breakfast meals are served in a day at Indira Gandhi International Airport?
solution:
Sure. First, should I consider both domestic and international flights?
Yes, include both.
Great. I’ll focus on all the terminals then. Also, I’ll assume we are looking at the flights that offer pre-booked in-flight breakfast meals, not buy-on-
board options. For timing, I’ll define breakfast hours as 6 AM to 11 AM—so a 5-hour window.
A busy international airport typically has:
3–4 runways (but often 2-3 are operational most of the time),
Departure slot every ~2 minutes in peak hours. Let’s say 1 runway handles 30 departures per hour × 16 operating hours = 480 departures/day per active runway.
With 3 runways = ~1440 departures/day (rounded down to adjust for downtime, bad weather, night restrictions, etc.).
Assuming the airport has around 1,200 total flights/day (departures and arrivals included), that gives 600 departures/day (half of total).
Let’s assume:
DDoImFeFstiIcC = U450L dTeYpartures/day
International = 150 departures/day
Flights are evenly spread over 24 hours. So per hour:
Domestic = 450 ÷ 24 ≈ 18. Guesstimate - International = 150 ÷ 24 ≈ 6.25 In 5 hours (6–11 AM):
Domestic flights = 18.75 × 5 ≈ International flights = 6.25 × 5 ≈ Total flights = 94 + 31 = Now estimating passengers:
I am assuming that domestic flights are handled by smaller aircrafts (like A320) which on average have 180 seats, whereas international flights,
using bigger aircrafts, on an average have 300 seats.
Domestic: 94 × 180 seats = 16,
International: 31 × 300 seats = 9,
Total passengers ≈ 26,
Considering only pre-booked meal services:
There are low-cost carriers like Indigo in domestic flights that do not serve pre-booked breakfast meals. Assuming that only 30% of domestic flights
are operated by full-service airlines, I get the following calculations:
Assume 30% of domestic passengers get meals: 0.3 × 16,920 ≈ 5,
Assume 100% of international passengers get meals: 9,
Total meals = 5,076 + 9,300 = ~14,
Alright, sounds good!

title: & Banking — McKinsey & Company
prompt: Guestimate the total number of cash withdrawals in Delhi on an average day?
solution:
Sure. I’d start with the population of Delhi—roughly around 20 million.
Alright, how would you segment that?
I’d segment by income group—something like 30% Below Poverty Line, 30% Lower Middle Class, 20% Upper Middle Class, and 20% Upper Class.
I think the lower middle class proportion should be higher. What’s your take?
That’s a fair point. Maybe we adjust it to 25% BPL, 40% lower middle class, 20% upper middle, and 15% upper class. That may better reflect the actual
spread. What’s your reasoning for using income segments here?
Income influences both access to banking and cash usage. Lower-income groups rely more on cash, while higher-income groups tend to use digital
modes. This impacts withdrawal behavior directly.
You study economics, right? How would you relate this income segmentation to real-world spending behavior?
There’s a clear economic rationale. Lower-income households operate more on a cash basis due to both access and trust issues with digital systems. So, they withdraw more frequently. Higher-income users, being more digitally active, use ATMs less often.
Go ahead with your approach then.
From there, I’d apply filters to estimate what percentage within each income group actually uses cash, and then how frequently they withdraw—
maybe weekly, bi-weekly, monthly. I’d apply a weighted average across these groups to get the final daily estimate. Guesstimate - I am not looking for the final number—this approach seems sound. Let’s move on. Assume you’re the CEO of an ATM business. How would you
increase the number of cash withdrawals?
We could improve ATM uptime, optimize location placement, or introduce incentives like cashback on withdrawals.
Let’s change the question, what if you had to increase footfall using non-financial products?
To clarify—are we trying to generate revenue from those products or simply attract more users to the ATMs?
I’m running out of time. I want the strategies directly—no more clarifying questions.
Understood. I’ll list three ideas.
Digital Literacy for Tech-Illiterate Users: We can use the ATM area to offer basic digital education—simple explainers on UPI, online payments, etc.,
either via screens or printed leaflets. It helps users get more comfortable with digital tools, increasing footfall and utility.
Basic Health Facilities for Elderly Users: We can install basic tools like BP or sugar level monitors near ATMs. Elderly people who prefer cash can make use of these, giving them a reason to visit more frequently.
Financial Literacy for Lower-Middle Class Users: Displaying financial information in regional languages—like savings tips, details of government
schemes, or bank offerings—can help build trust and empower users to explore more services.
If someone is tech-illiterate, how do you expect them to absorb digital literacy material from an ATM?
The aim isn’t full transformation in one visit. It’s about repeated exposure—short, local-language messages, videos, or printed material that sparks interest. Over time, that builds familiarity. They might ask family or friends to help, which furthers engagement.
Okay.
The broader idea behind these strategies is targeting user groups who are still dependent on cash—tech-illiterate individuals, the elderly, and lower-
middle income groups. These are the segments likely to visit ATMs anyway, so offering relevant non-financial value adds can keep them coming back.

title: Agriculture — Kearney
prompt: The client is experiencing a drop in EBITDA. We’d like you to identify the root cause and provide recommendations.
solution:
Just to confirm, EBITDA refers to Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization, right?
Yes, that’s correct.
Great. Who’s the client?
It’s a fertilizer . Their EBITDA is currently at 3%, while the average is around 6%.
Understood. Has this been an ongoing issue, or is it more recent?
It’s a recent trend—only started in the last few months. And what types of products do they deal in?
They sell both urea and non-urea fertilizers.
Got it. Is the problem localized to a particular region or geography?
No, it’s impacting all their locations.
Alright. Since EBITDA is essentially revenue minus various costs, is the issue more related to revenue or costs? It’s definitely on the cost side.
Understood. Which part of the cost structure is driving this?
Mainly manufacturing and packaging costs. Profitability - Let's start with manufacturing. That usually involves machines, labor, and facilities. Is there any increase in wastage or defects?
No, those have remained stable.
Then is it being driven by labor costs?
Yes, labor costs have gone up.
Typically, labor costs = Number of employees × Wages per packet × Number of packets. Has the number of employees increased?
There hasn’t been a significant increase in headcount, but labor costs per packet, particularly in packaging, have gone up.
Interesting. So, the rise is concentrated in the packaging part. Packaging costs can be broken into:
Packaging Cost = (Number of packets per labor)*(Cost of packaging materials).
Have there been any changes in packaging method, size, or material?
Yes—the packaging material has changed recently. That would explain the spike. A different material could be more expensive or less efficient to work with, slowing down productivity and increasing
cost per unit.
To tackle this, I’d recommend using a 2×2 matrix—Impact vs. Time to Implement—to identify potential solutions. For example - Profitability - Quick to Implement Takes Time
Revert to old material if feasible Explore automation in packaging
High Impact
Retrain packaging staff for new material Negotiate bulk pricing for new material
Redesign packaging format for long-term
Low Impact Minor workflow tweaks
savings This structured approach helps prioritize efforts that deliver the most value quickly, while planning for more complex changes in the background.
That’s a really helpful way to break it down—thanks a lot!

title: Fashion — Bain Capability Network
prompt:
You are the CFO at Van Heusen, and the revenue is reducing, but the profit is increasing. Is it sustainable? And how do we turn it around if it's not

solution:

regarding which segment is decreasing?

overcome this problem? In order to overcome this problem, we can focus on the following key-aspects:
Marketing and Promotion of our products
Implementing Employee Training programs to increase efficiency across different areas

Apart from these strategies, if we wanted to make some changes in the existing product lines, what would you suggest? We can explore diverse product lines, which would help our customers with variety in the options provided and increase the revenues. Switching to
fast-fashion products can be one of the ways since it would be relevant to current fashion trends, however we will have to ensure a strategic
implementation since these trends change very often and may not be a sustainable for long run

title: Food — Kearney
prompt: A ’s ingredient cost per packet was 20% higher than our competitors. We had to find out why.
solution:
So, who’s the client in this case?
It’s an airline —like Indigo—that serves food during flights.
Got it. What’s the specific food item we’re looking at?
It’s a rice bowl. We noticed that the quantity of rice per packet had gone up.
Interesting. Is this something other companies in the are also seeing?
Nope, it seems to be just us.
AlrigIhNt. HDavUe thSeTre RbeYen any recent changes—maybe a new supplier or a different quality of rice?
No, nothing’s changed on that front. Same supplier, same grade of rice.
Okay, to break it down clearly, I came up with a basic formula to figure out what’s driving the cost:
Ingredient cost per packet = (Cost per unit of ingredient × Total quantity used) / (Number of packets made - Wastage).
This way, we cover all the possible angles—how much we’re using, how much it costs, and how much is getting wasted. That makes sense. After looking at the numbers, we found that the issue is with wastage.
Alright. To figure out where exactly the wastage is happening, we mapped the whole process—starting from procurement, then inbound logistics,
manufacturing, packaging, outbound logistics, and finally, what happens when it reaches the client.
Yes, and we found that the problem is coming from the storage stage Profitability - Hmm. Storage issues usually fall into two categories—either something accidental, like a one-time incident, or something more routine and
incidental. Would you say this is incidental?
Yes, it’s been happening consistently, so definitely incidental.
In that case, we should look at a few things—like the type of inventory model you're using, where the warehouse is located, or maybe even the
temperature conditions.
We checked, and the problem seems to be with the inventory management.
Alright, which inventory model are you using—FIFO or LIFO?
We’re currently using LIFO.
Ah, that explains it. With LIFO, the newer stock gets used first, and the older rice just sits there. Over time, that older rice can spoil and mix in with the
good batches, which leads to wastage. I’d recommend switching to FIFO, where the older stock gets used up first. It should help reduce spoilage and bring down the cost per packet.
That’s a really helpful suggestion. Thanks a lot!

The operates all across India and they have been facing this issue all across India.

title: & Automobiles — McKinsey & Company

Your client is a scooter manufacturing in India. Over the past year, they have experienced a 20% decline in revenue. The CEO has

The decline is specific to the client.

and distribution?
They handle manufacturing, sales, and distribution through their own stores. Noted. Since this is a revenue problem, I would like to break revenue down into its components:

I see. I will now further break down the total volume of scooters sold into two factors: demand-side and supply-side issues. On the demand side, potential causes could include:
Decreased customer need
Lower awareness or brand perception Profitability - Affordability concerns
Poor customer experience
On the supply side, potential bottlenecks could be in:
Raw material procurement
Manufacturing and production
Warehousing
Distribution
Sales and marketing

Focus on manufacturing and production.
Alright. Within manufacturing and production, I will examine three key factors:
Process inefficiencies – Increased downtime or misalignment in production steps
Output inefficiencies – Defects leading to high rejection rates
Capacity constraints – Insufficient production capacity
HasD thIeF cFlieInCt obUseLrvTeYd issues in any particular area out of these?

title: & Consumer Goods — McKinsey & Company
prompt:

solution:
To diagnose the issue, I would approach this through an internal and external analysis framework. Does that sound reasonable?
Yes, go ahead.
Let’s begin with the internal analysis, which focuses on the in-store experience. A few key aspects to evaluate include:
Store Layout & Navigation: Are customers able to navigate the store easily? Are key products displayed prominently?
Customer Experience: How do customers perceive service quality? Is there a lack of staff assistance? Payment & Exit: Is the payment process seamless, or do long wait times deter customers? Is the exit experience frictionless?
For the external analysis, we need to consider demand-side factors, such as:
Need Awareness: Are customers aware of the store’s offerings and promotions?
Accessibility: Are store locations well-connected? Are parking facilities adequate?
Affordability & Competition: Are price points competitive with other retailers where the brand also sells? Would you like me to focus on any specific aspect in more detail?
That was a good breakdown. Now, let’s move forward. One way to solve this problem is by hosting special events in our stores to increase foot
traffic. Can you suggest some ideas. Profitability - Absolutely. I would categorize event ideas into online and offline strategies to maximize reach and engagement.
Online Strategies:
1.Collaborate with Influencers: Partner with food bloggers or YouTubers to showcase products.
2. Create Social Content: Share kitchen hacks and demos on Instagram and YouTube.
3.Subtle Placement: Feature products naturally in influencer content and events.
Offline Strategies:
1.Experience Centers: Turn stores into interactive kitchens where customers can try products.
2.Live Shows: Host in-store demos with chefs or local celebs.
3.Tech Engagement: Use AI/VR tools for kitchen visualization.
4.Workshops: Offer themed classes like baking or pottery at select locations
Would you like me to focus on any particular idea?
Good ideas. Now, let’s apply this to a specific case. Kitchen Delights is facing challenges in a city that is interested in sports. They have decided to
implement an event where customers receive discount coupons for purchases above a certain amount. What are your thoughts on this
strategy?
ThaDt sIoFunFdIsC likeU aL loTcYalized approach. To assess its effectiveness, I would analyze key metrics, including expected customer turnout and projected
financials. Could you provide some data points? Profitability - Sure. The decided to offer discount coupons for purchases above $20. They analyzed stadium attendance data to estimate how many
people might visit the store post-event. Based on this, they projected revenue of $650K. Fixed costs for renting the booth at the sports event were
$100K, and variable costs per event were $20K. The planned to hold 25 events in total. How would you evaluate profitability?
To calculate total costs:
Fixed Costs: $100K
Variable Costs: $20K × 25 events = $500K
Total Costs: $600K
Profit Calculation:
Revenue: $650K
Total Costs: $600K
Net Profit: $50K
Given this, the event appears profitable. The should consider ways to optimize costs or increase customer conversion per event.
That’s a solid approach. Thank you!

title: & Pharmaceuticals — McKinsey & Company
prompt:

title: & Insurance — McKinsey & Company
prompt: Let’s start with a case. Your client is a leading health insurance in India, they want to increase their revenue. Suggest solutions.
solution:
Understood. Before diving into potential solutions, I’d like to ask a few questions to understand the business model better.

Let’s focus on increasing the number of customers
Understood. For that, I’d analyze, current operational structure, existing distribution channels and opportunities for new customer segments,
geographies and revenue streams. Profitability - The primarily uses agents for distribution. Let’s focus on that. Okay, so now we’re narrowing in on the distribution piece—specifically the agents. To assess their impact on revenue, we can look at:
Total number of agents
Productivity per agent i.e number of plans sold per agent annually
This gives us a formula:
Revenue = No. of agents*Plans sold per agent*Premium per plan
Do we have any idea on current productivity?
Yes, on average, an agent sells around 10 plans annually.
That seems relatively low, especially given the market leadership. This suggests a productivity issue. Let’s explore solutions in two buckets,
firstly increasing the number of agents and improving agent productivity.
Yes, go on.
Firstly to attract more individuals to become insurance agents, we should launch widespread awareness and marketing campaigns that
highlight the opportunity and benefits of the role. Offer attractive incentives both monetary (such as commissions and bonuses) and non-
monetary (like recognition programs and career growth opportunities) to motivate new recruits. Then to improve Productivity of Existing
Agents we can enhance ease of business by simplifying sales tools and making product offerings more understandable for both agents and
customers. We can Implement a targeted sales strategy by assigning agents to high-potential geographies or customer segments, avoiding
random outreach. Introduce structured work schedules with clear, time-bound targets to increase focus and drive productivity. Profitability - The final solution will be to collaborate with the Skill India Mission to develop a vocatiIoNnaDl UtraSinTinRgY program spDecIiFfiFcaICllyU foLrT inYsurance agents.
We should design the curriculum to support individuals with minimal formal education, providing comprehensive training in insurance sales,
customer service, and digital tools. This initiative will significantly improve onboarding quality and long-term agent performance.
Yes, on average, an agent sells around 10 plans annually.
Interesting. Can you elaborate on how this training initiative would be implemented?
Certainly. The insurance could partner with Skill India or other government-led vocational training platforms to set up short-term
