title: & E-Commerce — McKinsey & Company
prompt:
The client is a quick commerce , something along the lines of Zepto, and they've been facing a decline in profits for some time.
Analyse that.
solution:
How long have they been facing this problem and do we have any information on the magnitude of decline?
We don't have information on either of those points.
Is the decline concentrated in a specific geographical region or have they faced this pan-India?
The has been experiencing this pan-India.
Have the competitors faced a similar decline?
TheyI hNavDenU't,S orT evRenY if they have, we don't know if they have faced it.
Because it's a decline in profit, we can attribute it either to a decline in revenue or an increase in costs or a combination of both.
It's attributed to an increase in costs.
I would like to do a value chain to figure out which aspect of the costs we are facing the problem with. We can break down costs into sourcing and
procurement, operational costs, delivery costs, marketing and customer acquisition, and tech and customer service. That's comprehensive, but then why did you include tech, wouldn't that be a fixed cost?
While it would majorly be a fixed cost, tech would also include variable costs such as payment processing fees, which would be a per transaction
cost that's charged by payment getaways. Then we would also have cloud costs because if the platform scales dynamically, the costs would
increase with higher app traffic. Profitability - Then we would also have a customer support that would be like a per ticket cost situation. If we are outsourcing support, then we may be charged
per interaction.
That was a good catch. What would you include under procurement?
We'd look at supplier agreements, inbound logistics, freight, and quality-related costs — including damage during transit and certification or
compliance expenses.
Procurement isn’t the issue — focus on operational costs instead. Just break that down.
Sure. We'd consider warehousing and storage, inventory management, packaging, and any expenses from returns and replacements.
We’re facing issues with inventory costs. Suggestions under each of those heads?
First, to reduce holding and storage costs, we could optimise warehousing — maybe switch to third-party fulfilment or use smaller, well-placed
warehouses. Ordering smaller, more frequent batches will also help turnover. If feasible, we could still explore switching to more cost-effective storage providers.
Second, to cut wastage, better expiry management is key — FIFO for perishables and stricter return-to-shelf rules to reduce write-offs.
Third, to tackle stock imbalances, demand forecasting using analytics or AI would help. A just-in-time approach could reduce overstocking.
Partnering with flexible suppliers and offering clearance discounts on slow-moving items would also reduce dead stock.
Lastly, we can reduce movement costs by improving warehouse layout, batching orders, or automating order placements. The storage cost angle is the problem — rent negotiations and turnover fixes are already tried. What else?
In that case, we can look at cross-docking — moving items directly from suppliers to dispatch without long storage. Dark stores, or micro-fulfilment
centres near demand hotspots, can help too. And partnering with local vendors for perishables is another practical route. Profitability - I like the local vendor idea. What are the benefits?
For one, we bypass middlemen and import fees. The supply is fresher, spoilage is lower, and we can order flexibly as per demand. Relationships are
easier to manage, and we’re less dependent on long-haul chains. Plus, it supports sustainability and builds customer goodwill — especially among
eco-conscious buyers — even if the cost savings there aren’t huge.
That’s good, and they’ve already adopted that. Some points you gave also cut delivery costs. Any additional ways to reduce those?
We could shift to incentive-based pay models for delivery staff, adopt EVs to cut fuel expenses, and set up pre-stocked dispatch bins. Efficient
packaging is another big lever.
Explain packaging efficiency a bit more.
Using lighter and simpler materials — like fibreboard instead of thick cardboard, or air cushions instead of foam — helps. Flat, stackable packaging
and standardized box sizes improve vehicle loading. For non-fragile goods, soft flexible packaging works. We could also switch to compostable or reusable materials to reduce disposal costs, and even incentivise returns.
That last part on customer returns is interesting. Just give me a rough framework off the top of your head.
We could try a deposit model — customers pay a small refundable amount for reusable boxes. Or a discount model — return X number of bags, get
₹25–₹50 off next order. Finally, riders can collect used packaging during deliveries. Simple, low-friction, and it keeps the system circular.
That works, thanks.

title: Pharmaceuticals — BCG
prompt:
Let’s begin. Your client is a medical equipment manufacturer that has been operating in India for over 30 years. They manufacture and
assemble 60-70% of their products in India and 30-40% in other nations. The primarily sells premium, high-quality products and
operates in the MNC segment, catering to both B2B (hospitals) and B2C customers. However, revenue growth has stagnated despite overall
market growth. New competitors have emerged, offering lower-cost products, and the is not performing well in the MNC segment
either. Your task is to analyze the stagnation in revenue growth and provide recommendations.
solution:
To break down revenue, I will use the following framework:
Revenue=(Price per Product*Number of Customers*Average Products Purchased per Customer) Try to think of something that includes the services as well, as the is also involved in providing services.
Since the also offers services, I will consider both product and service revenue. Would this be the right approach?
Yes. Additionally, I’d like you to focus more on the B2B segment (hospitals) rather than B2C.
Since we are focusing on hospitals, I will analyze Three key revenue drivers:
1.Price per Product – Have our prices decreased due to market conditions or competitive pressure? 2.Number of Customers (Hospitals Served) – Have we lost customers or are we unable to acquire new ones?
3.Average Number of Products Purchased per Hospital – Are hospitals buying fewer products? Profitability - 1. Price per product – If our product price has decreased, it could be due to:
Ability issue: Higher manufacturing costs forcing price reductions.
Want issue: Shift in hospital preferences towards competitor products.
2. Number of hospitals served – If our customer base has shrunk, the reasons could be:
Monetary issue: Affordability constraints preventing hospitals from purchasing our products.
Non-monetary issue: Other constraints unrelated to affordability, such as brand perception, procurement policies, or lack of additional value-
added services.
3. Average number of products purchased per hospital – If hospitals are buying fewer products, this could be due to changes in procurement cycles,
decreased demand, or shifts in hospital policies.
Would you like me to prioritize any particular area? Instead of the monetary vs. non-monetary framework for hospital retention, try structuring it differently.
In that case, I would use an Ability-Want framework:
1) Ability Constraints:
Affordability: Are hospitals unable to afford our premium products?
Accessibility: Are there any supply-side or distribution challenges affecting access to our products?
2) Want Constraints (Do they want to buy?)
Product benefits: Have competitors introduced superior or more cost-effective alternatives?
Non-monetary incentives: Are hospitals receiving better service packages, warranties, or financing options from competitors? Profitability - This is a good structure. Go ahead with your analysis. The issue lies in ability.
Since the problem is related to ability, I will focus on affordability and accessibility:
Affordability: Given that we are a premium player, our pricing is inherently high. However, we can rule out selling at lower prices with
compromised quality, as that does not align with our brand positioning.
Accessibility: The issue could be either supply-side (logistics, inventory, distribution) or demand-side (hospitals not being able to procure due to
operational hurdles).
Would you like me to refine the analysis further?
Yes. The core problem is on the demand side. Apply your Ability-Want framework and tell me your hypothesis.
Applying the framework to demand, I would segment it as follows:
1) Want: Quality of our product (but as a premium player, this is unlikely to be a concern).
Changes in hospital preferences (again unlikely, as our products are of superior quality).
2) Ability:
Affordability: If hospitals are unable to justify our pricing, we may need to introduce financing solutions or pricing adjustments.
Accessibility: Are hospitals facing difficulties in acquiring our products? For example, are procurement processes becoming more complex, or are
competitors offering bundled deals with better service packages?
Additionally, I would analyze customer experience including after-sales services, such as maintenance, support, and warranty offerings, as well as
assessing the purchase journey, whether through physical stores, the website, or direct B2B procurement channels. Profitability - I would also consider the end-to-end experience, covering every interaction from the moment a hospital engages with the to the final
purchase. However, I suspect customer experience is not a major issue, as it is unlikely to cause a significant revenue decline.
Alright. Given this, what strategies would you suggest to increase demand?
We can increase demand through two main avenues:
1.Existing customers:
Bundling strategy: Offer a basket of complementary medical devices and services to increase the total value proposition for hospitals.
2.New customer acquisition:
Marketing strategies (though not as relevant in B2B).
Exploring new segments (e.g., mid-tier hospitals).
Price adjustments where feasible.
Given the challenges in acquiring new customers, I would recommend focusing on existing customers first, optimizing our pricing model, and
enhancing our value proposition through bundling and service add-ons.
That sounds reasonable. We can close the case here.

title: Pharmaceuticals — BCG
prompt:
Suppose you are working with a medical equipment manufacturer that is facing a decline in revenue. I’d like you to think through the problem
and share your approach.
solution:
Certainly. I’d like to approach the problem by first breaking down the revenue into its two core components:
1. Number of units sold.
2. Average price per unit.
For the number of units sold, I would like to further categorize it into demand-side and supply-side factors. But before I proceed, may I ask a few
preliminary questions to better understand the context? Sure, go ahead.
Could you help me understand:
Where is the operating?
What’s the current market share?
Who are the key competitors? The has been operating in India for the past 30 years and holds a 25% market share. They import medical equipment from a global
manufacturer. In terms of competition, they are one of the major players, but small manufacturing companies have recently emerged and are
giving mild competition. Profitability - Understood. So it seems like there may be a growing market but increased competition could be a factor affecting sales. I’ll now approach it from
supply and demand angles.
On the supply side, I’d like to understand:
Is there any disruption in the supply chain?
Any issues with importing the products?
No, there are no issues on the supply side.
Got it. I'll now move to the demand side and segment it into the 4Ps of marketing:
1.Product – Understanding if there are any issues with the product fit.
2.Price – Analyzing if pricing changes due to competition have impacted sales.
3.Place – Checking the distribution channel and availability of products.
4.Promotion – Ensuring that the is communicating effectively with potential customers.
Would you like me to begin with the product?
Yes, please do.
Regarding the product, I’d like to know:
Are there any product quality issues or mismatches with market needs? Have there been any major changes in the product lineup or discontinuations?
No, the product is well-suited for the market and there are no such issues. Profitability - Alright. Moving on to price, has there been any recent increase in prices compared to competitors that could deter customers?
The price hasn’t increased significantly, and cost is not a major factor here.
Got it. Moving to place (distribution) — I’d like to understand:
Where is the product sold — through retailers, wholesalers, or directly to businesses?
How strong is the product availability in key markets?
It’s a B2B (Business-to-Business) model. They primarily sell to hospitals and diagnostic centers through a network of wholesalers and
distributors.
Interesting. I’d like to understand if there have been any disruptions in the distribution network — perhaps higher commission rates, logistical issues,
or lack of product availability in some regions?
No major issues were reported there either.
Okay, that eliminates supply-side and distribution concerns. Moving on to promotion, have there been any significant changes in marketing strategy
that could impact revenue generation?
No, marketing efforts have remained consistent.
Alright. Since product, price, supply, and promotion don’t seem to be the core issues, I’d like to now double-click on the customer base. Could you
clarify if the decline in revenue is observed across all customers or only a particular segment. In fact, we’ve noticed that existing customers (the ones who have been buying for years) continue to purchase as usual. However, there’s been a
decline in new customer acquisitions. Profitability - Got it — so the revenue decline is primarily a result of fewer new customers. To tackle this, I’d like to further segment the new customer challenge
using the 4 A’s Framework:
Awareness – Are new customers aware of the product?
Affordability – Is pricing a barrier for new customers?
Accessibility – Are there any distribution challenges for new customers?
Acceptance (Product Fit) – Is the product meeting the needs of the new customer segment?
Would you have any insights on these four factors?
We’ve checked these factors, and none of them seem to pose a major issue.
I see. In that case, I’d like to approach the problem using a Push-Pull Demand Framework:
Customer Push – Issues from the seller's side like inadequate marketing, low product availability, or poor distribution.
Customer Pull – Issues from the buyer’s side like lack of interest, perceived need, or competing product attractiveness.
Would you like me to analyze it from that lens?
Yes, please.
Since we’ve ruled out major distribution issues, I’ll focus on the customer pull side.
Have we seen any decline in product appeal among new customers? Is the product packaging, branding, or marketing campaign not resonating with them?
That’s exactly right. We’ve observed that our packaging and marketing is not as attractive to new customers, especially when compared to
some of the smaller emerging manufacturers. Profitability - Ah, I see. This indicates a customer pull issue, where new customers are simply less attracted to the product due to marketing and packaging gaps.
A couple of ways to address this could be:
1.Revamp the packaging to make it more modern and appealing.
2.Enhance marketing efforts tailored towards new customers.
3.Strengthen visibility in emerging channels where new customers seek information.
I think you’ve covered the major root cause. Thank you for the structured and logical approach!
My pleasure!

title: & Banking — McKinsey & Company
prompt:
Your client is a private bank. It wants to expand but is facing constant revenues. Identify how you would help it grow and also why the issue of
stagnant revenues is being faced.
solution:
Before starting with the solution for this, I’d like to clarify where the bank is facing this problem; in some specific areas or pan-India?
The problem is being faced in the Indian rural areas.
I’d like to clarify if it's a client-specific or -specific problem.
It’s a client-specific problem.
Alright, so this profitability case can be categorised into two ways: either the costs have increased or the revenue has gone down. Focus on both the aspects of the issue.
Profits are a function of revenue and cost. The major revenue sources for the banking sector are interest income and the fees charged by the banks.
In the costs aspect, you have the interest on deposits and the operational costs.
Focus on the operational aspect of it.
Within operational costs, you majorly have labour and advertising costs. We are facing specific issues with the labour aspect of the costs.
One of the reasons I could think of was that the people who are employed by the bank aren't working to their optimum efficiency; for a task that can
be completed by one person, they are hiring more people. Another reason could be that we are paying more wages than the average. Profitability - The issue is with the labour efficiency part. In rural areas, the problem with the employeIeNsD isU thSaTt aRftYer recruitmeDntI tFhFeyIC arUe LnTotY working to their
optimum efficiency. Additionally, as the banks are located in rural areas, there are issues with higher labour costs as the living costs in those
areas are worse, because of which the client is forced to compensate more than what the employees working in urban areas receive. Now
keeping this in mind, suggest some strategies on how to help them grow.
So there can either be organic growth (revenue growth) or inorganic growth (merger or acquisition with any other ).
Focus on organic growth.
Focusing on the costs issue, if we are able to find better living areas where the banks are located, we can sort of try to reduce costs by not
compensating the employees so much. Regarding the growth and expansion part of it, we can try expanding our banks in areas that have optimum
living conditions—maybe locate them in more central areas where more population is residing. In the short term, I suggest that we focus on some
specific cities and within them, some specific areas where we can set up new banks. Accordingly, in the long run, we can observe what the impact
has been and then decide how to proceed.

title: & Automobiles — McKinsey & Company
prompt:
Your client, a scooter manufacturing , has experienced a 20% revenue decline over the past 12 months. Your task is to identify
the issue and recommend solutions.
solution:
I have a few preliminary questions:
1.What are the ’s product offerings?
2.Who is the target audience?
3.What is the price range? 4.Are competitors facing a similar issue?
5.What is the ’s geographical market?
6.What is the production process and number of manufacturing units?
The produces a single type of scooter priced at ₹50,000–₹60,000. The crisis is specific to this and does not affect competitors. Operations are based in India with one manufacturing unit. Profitability - The revenue decline must stem from a drop in scooter sales, as variety and pricing reINmDaiUn uSnTcRhaYnged. The isDsuIeF FlikIeClyU faLlTlsY into one of three
categories:
1.Production
2.Distribution
3.Demand
Demand has remained stable. Please focus on production and distribution.
Production relies on a single machine. Is the issue related to machine efficiency or external factors?
The machine produces 80 scooters per month, but only 64 pass quality control.
Quality check failures could arise from:
Manufacturing defects
Poor packaging causing damages
Shipping losses (accidental or theft-related)
The main issue is quality control. How would you resolve it?
Three quick recommendations:
1.Diagnose the root cause – Fix or replace faulty production machinery
2.Improve quality control – Implement real-time quality checks during production
3.Expand production – If demand allows, introduce additional units to stabilize supply

title: Capability — Bain Capability Network
prompt: SBI is considering opening a branch inside St. Stephen’s College, Delhi University. How would you approach this?
solution:
College students are at a stage where they begin engaging with formal banking—opening accounts, applying for debit cards, and using digital
payments. A branch within the campus would offer convenience and help SBI build early relationships with future long-term customers. Faculty and
administrative staff would also benefit from easy access to banking services.
Based on this, I would recommend opening a branch inside the college.
That’s a start, but let’s go a step further. Aren’t there any drawbacks to this approach?
One issue could be exclusivity. If the branch is inside the college, it primarily serves just St. Stephen’s students and staff. Given the college’s relatively small batch size, the total number of customers may be quite limited.
Exactly. So how might SBI serve more customers?
If the branch were located near St. Stephen’s rather than inside, it would still be accessible to the same students and faculty—but also to students
from nearby colleges in North Campus like Hindu, Ramjas, and Miranda House. This would significantly expand the potential customer base.
That’s the key insight. What’s your final recommendation? SBI should open a branch close to St. Stephen’s, not inside the campus. This location strategy maintains convenience for Stephen’s while making
services accessible to a larger student population in the area. It ensures higher scalability and access to greater potential customers.
Right. Thank you.

title: Textile Manufacturing — BCG
prompt:
Your client is the CEO of a textile manufacturing firm. Your client wants to enter the market of technical textiles. Financial due diligence is done,
only explain us the strategic market entry plan.
solution:
When talking about textiles, in terms of the current textile manufacturing firm, where in the value chain do we lie, what are the product lines like?
They include bulletproof jackets, spacesuits and materials used in them. Value chain wise they are yarn manufacturers.
What is my objective of venturing into technical textiles, like is there any profitability metric or any other metric?
Objective was profitability. Firm was set up in Asia, South Asia, Europe.
Since the financial due diligence was done, I will talk about operational feasibility and risk. Operational Feasibility includes Barriers to Entry and Value Chain Analysis. Barriers to Entry will include all those things that will stop us from entering
the technical textile business. They can be economic, strategic, legal, brand loyalty, etc. Value Chain Analysis will simply include going over your
value chain with factors like planning, financing, production, licensing, procurement, distribution, etc.
Technical textiles have very limited competitors in the right now. You have to source the raw materials as well to enter the .
Since the number of firms are limited, the number of raw material suppliers are also limited, and all of these firms have contracts with the producers that they can only supply it to them. How do you think you will enter the market and convince the raw material providers to give it to
you? Market Entry - I think to begin with, my entry into the market should create value for the suppliers. Suppliers are under contract, but contracts can expire,
renegotiations happen, and they often seek long-term growth. I should propose a partnership model where they get exclusive rights to supply the
growing operations. We can also check if there are any overseas suppliers not bound by these contracts. In parallel, exploring backward integration
or developing alternative raw materials can give us more control over the supply chain. This multi-pronged approach ensures we are not solely
dependent on existing locked-in suppliers and positions us for sustainable entry into the market.
That satisfies me. Thankyou.

title: Banking — Kepler Cannon
prompt: Our bank is interested in launching a credit card for gamers. How would you approach designing this product?
solution:
I would take a structured approach, starting with understanding the product features, target customers, competition, and market feasibility. Before
diving into the details, I’d like to ask some preliminary questions to clarify the scope.
To better define the product, I would ask about the target audience (casual gamers, professional gamers, or streamers), key features (cashback,
rewards, or exclusive in-game benefits), existing competition, geographical scope (global launch or specific regions).
Good questions. Assume that this is a completely new product with no direct competitors. We want to design it from scratch.
Understood. I will structure my approach using the 4Ps framework—Product, Price, Promotion, and Place. 1) Product: The card would be tailored for specific segments, primarily:
Youth Gamers (18-25): Likely to spend on in-game items.
High-Income Gamers & Streamers (25-40): More purchasing power for high-value gaming equipment, subscriptions, and sponsorships.
2) Pricing: Differentiated pricing based on:
Frequent spenders on gaming transactions could receive lower fees or higher rewards. Infrequent users might face higher fees or fewer perks to encourage increased spending. Market Entry - 3) Promotion:
Digital Marketing: Advertisements in gaming platforms, Twitch, and YouTube gaming channels.
In-Game Ads: Partner with game developers to display credit card offers within games.
App Store Presence: Running promotions on gaming platforms to reach active users.
4) Place:
Since the product is digital, accessibility is not a concern. The entire onboarding and management process would be online.
Would you like me to focus on a specific area?
That covers the basics. Now, let’s evaluate the market feasibility. How would you estimate the potential market size?
To assess feasibility, I would break it down into the following steps:
Population Segmentation: Start with the total urban population, as rural penetration is low.
Age Breakdown: Exclude those below 18 (not eligible for credit cards) and those above 60 (low gaming activity).
Gender Breakdown: For simplicity, assume a higher proportion of male gamers and exclude females for easier estimates.
Income Segmentation: Identify individuals with disposable income who are likely to spend on gaming.
After applying these filters, we obtain the total addressable market (TAM) for the credit card.
That makes sense. What about operational feasibility? Market Entry - I would analyze three key factors:
People: Hiring or training financial analysts to understand gaming transaction trends.
Processes: Streamlining approval processes for faster onboarding of gamers.
Technology: Ensuring seamless integration with gaming platforms and secure transaction processing.
Would you like me to dive deeper into any specific area?
No, that was a well-structured approach. Thank you!

title: & Consumer Electronics — McKinsey & Company
prompt:
Your client is a Korean TV distributor looking to expand into the Indian market. You're advising on whether expansion is feasible and crafting a
strategy.
solution:
Alright, so I have understood it correctly, we're looking to develop a market entry strategy for our client, a Korean TV distributor aiming to expand
into the Indian market. Is that correct?
Yes, we can go ahead.
Great, thank you. I'd like to begin with a few preliminary questions to understand the context better.
When are we aiming to enter the market? Who are our major competitors, and what segment do they dominate?
What part of the value chain do we operate in? Are we involved in manufacturing, retailing, or distribution?
Finally, is there a specific rationale for choosing India? Is it market saturation in Korea, demand signals from India, or another driver like growth or
diversification?
We plan to enter within the next 2 years to grow our business. We're a third-party distributor, we don’t manufacture the TVs ourselves. Manufacturing will be outsourced. We’ve identified India due to its growing middle class and increased TV penetration. Competitors include 2–
well-established Indian brands.
Got it, and does the client have prior experience operating in highly price-sensitive, service-intensive markets? Market Entry - Not in India, but we operate in Southeast Asia, including Vietnam and Indonesia.
Lastly, could you clarify the expected price range and lifespan of the product we intend to introduce in the Indian market?
We're targeting a price range of ₹15,000 to ₹25,000, tailored to the Indian audience. The average product lifespan is expected to be around years.
Thank you for the context. Based on this, I’d like to structure my analysis in the following way:
Market attractiveness: Here, we will decide which segment to target and the expected market size, market share, and the expected profit (by
analysing profit/unit, fixed and variable costs.)
Competitive landscape: Understand who the players are and their strengths.
Operational feasibility: Evaluate if the client is equipped for distribution, marketing, and servicing in India, by outlining the barriers to entry and
then drawing out a value chain.
Go-to-Market strategy: Propose entry mode, positioning, pricing, and partnerships.
Potential risks: This includes political, regulatory/legal risks that may arise while establishing operations in India. This can be done through a
PESTEL analysis.
Can we proceed in this order?
You've outlined the strategy comprehensively. For the purpose of this case, let's focus on analyzing the market size, specifically the expected number of TVs sold in India.
Understood. To accurately estimate the market size, should we focus solely on consumer (household) TV sales, or should we also include commercial
sales, such as those to hotels, offices, and other businesses? Market Entry - Let's concentrate on the consumer market for this analysis.
Understood. For this case, would it be reasonable to assume India's population at approximately 140 crores, with an urban-rural split of 30:70?
While those numbers are valid, for simplicity in our calculations, let's assume a total population of 100 crores, with a 40:60 urban to rural
distribution.
Got it. Given the higher demand for televisions in urban areas, we can be more generous with our assumptions there. However, considering the
increasing electrification and infrastructure development in rural regions, it's reasonable to assume a growing adoption of televisions in these areas
as well.
Sure, go ahead.
Great. Based on our simplified assumption, this gives us an urban population of 40 crores and a rural population of 60 crores. To further analyze the
market, I'd like to segment both urban and rural populations into three income categories: upper class, middle class, and lower class. Given the
differing demographics, the splits would vary for urban and rural areas.
Urban population: (40 crores)
Upper class: 10% (4 crores)
Middle class: 50% (20 crores)
Lower class: 40% (16 crores) Rural population: (60 crores)
Upper class: 10% (6 crores)
Middle class: 40% (24 crores) Market Entry - Lower class: 50% (30 crores) - ELIMINATED
Considering the price range of televisions and the income levels, would it be reasonable to exclude the lower class segment of the rural population
from our target market analysis?
Yes, these assumptions are reasonable. You're also correct in excluding the lower class segment of the rural population from this analysis, given
their limited purchasing power for such products. Please proceed with the analysis based on these segments.
Understood. Now, I'd like to further segment the population based on their ability to purchase a television in the ₹20,000–₹25,000 price range. For this
analysis, we'll focus on the ability to pay, excluding factors like willingness to pay.
Urban population:
Upper class: 100% (4 crores)
Middle class: 80% of 20 crores = 16 crores
Lower class: 30% of 16 crores = 4.8 crores
Total urban addressable market: 4 + 16 + 4.8 = 24.8 crores
Rural population:
Upper class: 50% of 6 crores = 3 crores
Middle class: 30% of 24 crores = 7.2 crores Total rural addressable market: 3 + 7.2 = 10.2 crores
Combined addressable market = 24.8 + 10.2 = 35 crores.
Do the numbers look good so far? Market Entry - Yes, we can proceed.
Great. I'd now like to convert this population figure into the number of households, assuming one television per household. I'll use an average
household size of 4, which is fairly representative across both urban and rural areas. I'm also choosing to ignore outliers in urban regions where there
might be multiple TVs in a single home. Does that sound reasonable?
Yes, go ahead.
Now, assuming one TV per household and an average Indian household size of 4 members, the number of households that can afford a TV would be:
35 ÷ 4 = 8.75 crore households
Next, since we’re calculating the annual total addressable market, I’d divide this by the average TV replacement cycle or lifespan, which we’ve earlier
taken as 10 years:
8.75cr ÷ 10 = 87.5 lakh
So, the final annual addressable market for TVs in the Indian market would be approximately 87.5 lakh households.
Sounds reasonable, we can close the case here.
Thank you!

title: Capability — Bain Capability Network
prompt:
Amazon is planning to launch a new color Kindle for novels and storybooks. They have two options:
Target a premium segment—sell at a higher price, with higher margins.
Go for scale—sell at a low price, closer to cost, and capture a larger market.
What would you recommend?
solution:
I would recommend focusing on greater market share rather than exclusivity. By pricing the color Kindle more affordably, Amazon can reach a
wider audience. This includes children’s books and illustrated novels—products that benefit from color and appeal to young readers. Parents might
see it as a one-time educational investment, making it attractive. With growing digital adoption, this product could scale quickly. Interesting. What about unit economics and cost-to-serve for a larger customer base?
While margins would be lower, large-scale sales could offset this. Economies of scale could help recover costs like manufacturing. Plus, it would build
long-term customer loyalty.
That’s a valid point, but here’s what Amazon actually did. They opted for the exclusivity model—targeting a smaller, premium customer base and
charging higher prices. This reduced logistics, storage, and distribution costs, and ensured higher margins per unit. By narrowing their focus, Amazon could create a strong value proposition and manage operational efficiency.
That makes sense.

title: and Vaccines — McKinsey & Company
prompt: You’re working with an Indian vaccine manufacturer. They want to enter the European market.
solution:
Got it. Just to clarify—do we already have any export operations or is this our first time moving outside India?
We do export currently. We sell in India and also export to Africa through UN tenders and global agencies.
Thanks, that helps. So it seems like we’re already plugged into international systems like UNICEF and WHO?
Yes, most of our exports happen through those global bodies.
And what’s our product portfolio like? Are we focused on any particular type of vaccine—like pediatric, adult, travel-related, or disease-specific?
Why don’t you tell me what kind of vaccines should we sell in Europe? Understood. I’ll propose options and we can go from there. But before that—do we know where in Europe we’re trying to expand? Is it the EU broadly, or
specific countries?
Again—you tell me. Which country or region would you recommend we enter?
Fair enough. Then I’ll begin by structuring how I would approach this. First, I’d assess market attractiveness: what kind of vaccines are in demand in
Europe, how big the opportunity is. Then I’d look at feasibility: how difficult would it be to enter and scale. And finally, define a go-to-market strategy. Does that work?
Yes, go ahead. Market Entry - For the product itself, I’d suggest focusing on pediatric vaccines rather than adult or travel vaccines. The logic is two-fold: first, many
European countries have structured immunization programs for children, so demand is more stable and predictable. Second, parents are
more willing to spend on preventive care for children, particularly post-COVID.
Alright, let’s say we go with pediatric vaccines. What next?
That seems relatively low, especially given the market leadership. This suggests a productivity issue. Let’s explore solutions in two buckets,
firstly increasing the number of agents and improving agent productivity.
Yes, go on.
Then I’d assess the financial feasibility, starting with a market sizing exercise. Since you mentioned we haven’t picked a specific country yet, I’ll
take a general approach that can be applied to any 2–3 countries we shortlist later.
Go ahead with the approach.
First, I’d take the total population and apply an urban–rural split, since access and coverage can vary significantly between the two. Then
narrow down to the pediatric population—say children under the age of 5 or 10 depending on vaccine types. Market Entry - Then I’d apply several filters: Regulatory coverage: What percentage of pediatric vaccines are government-mandated?
Awareness levels: What portion of the target population is even aware of the need for these vaccines?
Willingness to vaccinate: Cultural or ideological barriers in certain countries might limit uptake.
Affordability or reimbursement: In many EU countries, vaccines are covered by national health programs—but private options still exist. So
we’d segment accordingly.
Applying these filters gives us the addressable market. We then convert that into monetary value—how many doses per child, what price per
dose we can charge, etc. This gives us the total revenue potential in dollar terms.
Alright. Would you consider competition at this point?
Yes, absolutely. I’d look at who the major players are in that region—are we competing with big pharma like GSK, Pfizer, Sanofi? Or are there
regional players offering low-cost vaccines? That would impact both pricing and positioning.
Makes sense, lets now move to feasibility.
Sure. Starting with operational feasibility—we could continue leveraging the existing relationships we have with UN agencies, WHO, or UNICEF.
They might help with navigating approvals or even acting as distribution intermediaries.
Beyond that, we’d assess regulatory complexity. Europe has stringent vaccine approval protocols—EMA processes, cold chain requirements,
pharmacovigilance, and clinical documentation. If we’re WHO prequalified already, that may reduce time to market, but local approvals will
still be necessary. Market Entry - Interesting. How would you approach country selection? I’d score countries across three dimensions:
Market Attractiveness: Size of pediatric population, spend per capita, vaccination mandates.
Ease of Entry: Regulatory pathway complexity, time to approval, public–private mix.
Competitive Landscape: Are there gaps in supply or segments underserved by current players?
We could pilot in a smaller but receptive market—say, Eastern Europe or Scandinavia—before scaling to larger markets like Germany or
France.
Alright, how would you structure the entry- B2B, B2C or B2G.
B2G would be ideal—partner with public health systems or get listed on government procurement portals. But we can also explore B2B with
private hospitals and pediatric clinics. B2C is tougher given trust barriers and limited brand recognition, but it can evolve over time via digital
platforms or telehealth partnerships.
Alright I think that gives me what I need. Thank you.

title: Pharmaceuticals — Trinity Lifesciences
prompt:
Your client is a renowned US-based multinational pharmaceutical named Cancure, specializing in cancer treatments. They are
planning to launch a new therapy called Camber, which is currently in clinical trials and expected to launch in 2025. The therapy targets patients
with HR-positive breast cancer, a segment where Product A is currently the standard of care.
How would you approach developing a launch strategy for this product?
solution:
I recommend the following steps for Camber's launch strategy:
1.Market Entry (Where to Enter):
Segmentation: Focus on HR-positive breast cancer patients, particularly those who may not respond optimally to existing therapies. Geographic Targeting: Prioritize regions with higher incidence rates and established healthcare infrastructure.
2.Market Penetration (How to Play):
Differentiation: Highlight Camber's superior efficacy and favorable side effect profile in all communications.
Stakeholder Engagement: Collaborate with KOLs to publish clinical trial results and present at major oncology conferences.
Patient Advocacy: Partner with patient advocacy groups to raise awareness and provide education about Camber. Access and Affordability: Work closely with payers to secure reimbursement and implement patient assistance programs. Market Entry - Enhanced coverage and reimbursement policies for Camber could offset some competitIivNeD prUesSsTurResY from DominaDnIt'Fs FenICtryU. TLhTisY improved
accessibility may lead to increased adoption among patients and healthcare providers, potentially stabilizing or even boosting Camber's market
share despite the new competition. It's essential to monitor payer policies closely and engage with insurance providers to ensure Camber remains a
cost-effective and attractive option for patients.
So, what are the key considerations when managing the commercial lifecycle of Camber?
Just to clarify, when we talk about commercial lifecycle management, we're referring to the entire journey—from product development all the way to
its maturity in the market. Is that correct?
Yes, that’s right.
In that case, I’d break it down into several key stages within the value chain:
Research and Development (R&D): This is where it all begins—discovering and developing the drug.
Raw Material Procurement: Ensuring we source high-quality materials for production.
Production & Process Development: Setting up efficient manufacturing processes to maintain quality.
Storage & Warehousing: Proper storage solutions are crucial to preserving product integrity.
Transportation: Reliable logistics ensure timely delivery.
Distribution: Strategizing the best channels to get the product to the right market..
Marketing: Promoting it effectively to healthcare professionals and consumers.
After-Sales Services: Providing post-purchase support to maintain customer satisfaction. Market Entry - Digital Presence: Leverage digital platforms to disseminate information and engage both healthcare professionals and patients. Implementing these strategies should position Camber effectively in the market and ensure a successful launch.
Let's discuss some recent market events and their potential impact on Camber.
Event 1: FDA releases a black box warning for patients with moderate to high risk of obesity planning to take Camber.
A black box warning is the FDA's most stringent caution, indicating that the drug carries a significant risk of serious or even life-threatening adverse
effects. This warning would likely lead to a decrease in Camber's sales, as healthcare providers may be more hesitant to prescribe it, and patients
may be reluctant to use it. In the short term, this could result in reduced revenue. Long-term strategies should include investing in research and
development to address the safety concerns highlighted by the FDA, potentially reformulating Camber to mitigate risks associated with obesity.
Event 2: A competing drug, Dominant, currently in Phase II trials, shows significantly better results than Camber. Dominant is expected to launch
three years after Camber's release.
The emergence of Dominant as a superior competitor poses a significant threat to Camber's market share. In the short term, Camber can capitalize
on its first-mover advantage by intensifying marketing efforts and establishing strong relationships with healthcare providers. Concurrently, investing
in R&D to enhance Camber's efficacy and safety profile is crucial. Exploring strategic options, such as partnerships or even acquiring Dominant, could
be considered to maintain market leadership.
Event 3: Around the time Dominant gets approved, payers and insurance providers are anticipated to provide higher coverage and ease of
reimbursement for Camber. Market Entry - At every stage, three key factors must be considered: 1.Economic Considerations:
Profitability—ensuring the product meets financial targets.
2.Operational Considerations:
Value Chain Optimization—streamlining everything from production to distribution.
Human Resource Management—making sure we have the right talent in place.
3.Risks & Barriers:
Market Entry Barriers—navigating challenges when entering new markets.
Competitive Risks—staying ahead of potential competitors.
This is pretty comprehensive, we can close this. Thank you!

title: Cosmetics — Bain Capability Network
prompt: Let’s start with a case. A skincare wants to enter India, assess the proposition.
solution:
Can you tell me a little about the background of the client and what their rationale is to enter India and if there's any sort of certain geographical
region that they're looking to enter?
The client is currently operating in the US. They think India is a lucrative market because they've seen brands like Maybelline and L'Oreal make it
big, and from a geographical standpoint, they aren't really targeting a region.
How is the product positioned in comparison to these companies?
It's similarly priced and they primarily want to operate in the premium sector. What are the client's expectations from entering the market in India?
They aren’t sure about that.
For any , the decision to enter the market would primarily be based on four factors, which would be financial attractiveness, operational
feasibility, risks involved and entry strategy. So if we start off with the first one, that's financial attractiveness, we would have to understand the market
size, what is the potentially acquirable market share and what are the profit margins that the client enjoys per unit of the product that they sell. Begin with estimating the market size. Market Entry - Technology & automation – automate filling/labeling/packaging; use MES & traceability; adopt renewable energy (solar) to cut carbon
footprint.
Okay. Now I’d like you to dive deeper into marketing.
We can just simply look at three very clean buckets, which would be awareness, conversion, and retention.
Right. Focus only on awareness.
Offline marketing – retail partnerships with free samples/mini-products in malls & stores to drive trials and conversions; participation in
beauty/wellness expos for product trials & purchases.
Traditional advertising – TV, radio, and print ads for broad exposure; festive-season campaigns with promos & discounts for targeted reach.
So the client has entered, but they have failed, so what are some barriers to exiting that they might face?
Financial barriers – unrecovered capital, outstanding loans, asset liquidation challenges (inventory/equipment/property) in a competitive
market.
Operational barriers – employee severance/HR issues, regulatory obligations (recalls, safe disposal, tax compliance, GST filings, brand
deregistration).
Brand & reputation barriers – reputational risk to founders, harming credibility for future ventures or funding.
Alright, thank you. Market Entry - Since the client is targeting urban markets with a premium product, the focus is on uIpNpDerU mSidTdRleY and upper-cDlaIFssF sIeCgUmLeTntYs, mainly female
→ →
consumers, as seen with competing brands. Starting with India’s population of 140 crore: 30% urban 42 crore; 50% women 21 crore; 40%
→
upper middle/upper class 8.4 crore potential customers.
Make sure to not round off your numbers.
→
From 8.4 crore, applying a 70% skincare usage filter 5.88 crore women. A typical routine includes 5 products (cleanser, toner, serum,
moisturizer, sunscreen), each lasting ~3 months. That means ~20 purchases/year, rounded down to 15 products per woman. So, 5.88 crore ×
15 = 88.2 crore units market size. Next, we need estimates on client’s market share and profit margins.
They expect to capture a sizable market. So we can move ahead with this market size. Now, calculate the barriers of entry for the client
entering India.
There are three barriers:
Regulatory & compliance – licensing, certifications, ingredient restrictions, labeling norms, and import duties (if importing).
Operational – supply chain complexity, raw material sourcing, marketing spend, R&D, etc.
Market behaviour – customer inertia, price sensitivity, market fragmentation, and incumbent advantage of large players.
They are looking to set up manufacturing in India, but they would be selling through third-party sources. Can you assess the operational
feasibility for this? Market Entry - Noted- Key considerations that come to mind are: 1.Production setup – infrastructure, raw material sourcing, and quality control.
2.Workforce – skilled labor with expertise in formulation and quality assurance.
3.Distribution & logistics – third-party channels, tie-ups with online platforms.
4.Export potential – leverage India’s low costs to target SE Asia, Middle East, etc.
5.Marketing – boost awareness via promotions, discounts, sponsorships.
I liked the fact that you took into account the export potential. Moving on, can you explain about the production and setup?
The first one is location selection. The second one is a manufacturing setup. The third one is compliance and certification. The fourth one is
raw material sourcing and inventory management, and the last one is technology and automation.
Can you go a little bit in depth into all of these buckets?
Location selection – proximity to raw-material hubs (Gujarat: chemicals, Tamil Nadu: plant extracts), skilled labor, infrastructure, and
ports/distribution centers for exports.
Manufacturing setup – plant layout with formulation, production, packaging/warehousing, QC areas; machinery (mixers, reactors), QC tools
(pH/consistency testers); reliable utilities with sustainability options (water treatment, energy-efficient equipment).
Compliance & certification – brand registration, product approvals (FSSAI/authority), and export compliance (FDA, etc.).
Raw-material sourcing & inventory – mix of local suppliers (scale) and importers (specialty); JIT + warehouse management to reduce
costs/stockouts.

title: Banking — BCG
prompt:
Our client is an Indonesian NBFC that has been offering car loans since 2016. They are now considering digitizing their business and
transitioning to an online platform. How would you approach evaluating this opportunity?
Thank you. To assess the feasibility of moving to an online model, I’d like to evaluate three key areas: financial feasibility, operational feasibility, and
risks. Before diving into these, could you provide some details on the current distribution model?
Of course. Currently, 80% of loans are facilitated through car dealers, who recommend our client’s financing options to buyers. The remaining
20% of loans are processed directly through bank branches who sell directly to the customers.
Understood. I’d like to start by estimating the potential online market size. Do we have data on the total offline car loan market in Indonesia Yes, the offline car loans / auto financing market size in Indonesia is US $1.2 billion.
To estimate the online market, first I will estimate the number of cars purchased in Indonesia in a year. Total population of Indonesia is 280 million.
30% Below Poverty Line (BPL) - likely not purchasing cars, so I’ll exclude this segment
45% Lower Middle Class
20% Upper Middle Class 5% Affluent Class
Therefore, 70% of 280 million will be 196 million. Market Entry - Since vehicle and loan decisions are typically made at the household level, I’ll divide this group by the average family size of 4, giving us:
196 million ÷ 4 = 49 million households.
% Families Loan Loan-Financed
Income Class No. of Families Car Ownership Assumption Cars Owned
(out of 49M) Penetration Cars
(45/70) x 100 = 50% own 1 car on average 31.5 x 0.5 = 90% take car
Lower Middle 31.5 million 14.18 million
→
64.3% 0.5 cars per household ~15.75 million loans
(20/70) x 100 = 75% own cars 2 cars on average 14.01 x 1.5= 75% take car
Upper Middle 14.01 million 15.77 million
→
28.6% 1.5 cars per household ~21.02 million loans
(5/70) x 100 = 100% own 2-3 cars on average 3.48 x 2.5 = 25% take car
Affluent 3.48 million 2.18 million
→
7.1% 2.5 cars per household ~8.7 million laons Market Entry - Total Loan-Financed Cars: 14.18 + 15.77 + 2.18= 32.13 million households
→
Starting from total loan-financed cars = 32.13 million apply internet penetration (×0.8) and online adoption (×0.5): 32.13 × 0.8 × 0.5 = 12.86 million
households.
Most individuals buy a car once every 8–12 years. I will use a standard average of 10 years to represent the replacement cycle.
12.86 ÷ 10 = ~1.29 million users annually
Therefore, annual market size of car loans is 1.29 million households.
Let’s consider the competitive landscape. There are 3-4 major players in the online loan market. How would you then assess the market share?
Since there are 4 major players, I will assume each hold almost an equal amount of market share.
1.29 x 0.25 = ~0.323 million
What about the influence of positive word-of-mouth on the market share?
Certainly. I will assume that due to a positive word-of-mouth, adoption rate will increase by 10% as more people will switch to online loan plans.
Therefore,
0.323 x 1.1 = ~0.35 million households
How would you assess the financial feasibility of this transaction?
The total serviceable market is 0.35 million households. Could you provide details on the expected cost structure for financial feasibility? The fixed costs for this transition are estimated at $10 million, and the variable cost are around $5 million per year. Revenue per head is $
annually. Market Entry - In that scenario,
Revenue: 0.35 x 70 = $24.5 million
Total Costs: 10 + 5 = $15 million
We get an estimated profit of $9.5 million. This suggests that transition is financially feasible.
Understood. How would you assess the operational feasibility?
To evaluate operational feasibility, I will assess three key areas: people, technology, and process.
People: Transitioning online requires upskilling employees. We will need training programs, maintenance teams, and enhanced support services.
Technology: The must develop a digital platform that allows both customers and dealers to access loan services seamlessly.
Process: The shift from offline to online will require adjustments to the distribution model, internal operations and compliance measures.
Would you like me to expand on any of these points?
No, that was a thorough breakdown. Let’s now discuss potential risks.
To evaluate risks, I will apply a PESTLE framework, though I will focus on the most relevant factors: technological, economic, and cultural risks.
Technological Risks: Since online car loans are not yet common in Indonesia, there may be a technological adoption barrier. Customers may lack
trust in digital lending or face challenges in completing transactions online.
Economic Risks: Cars are often considered a luxury, making car loans highly sensitive to economic conditions. During times of economic
slowdown, the car economy could face similar fate slowing down the car financing process.
Cultural Risks: Large financial commitments, such as car loans, are traditionally handled in-person, often through trusted dealers. Shifting to an
online model may require significant behavioral change among customers and dealers. Market Entry - That’s a strong analysis. Could you summarize your recommendation?
Certainly. Based on my analysis:
The transition to an online model appears financially viable, with projected annual profits of $9.5 million.
Operationally, the shift requires investment in technology, training, and process realignment but is feasible with strategic execution.
Risks exist, particularly around adoption barriers and economic downturns, but these can be mitigated through phased implementation,
partnerships with dealers, and trust-building measures.
Great approach! That concludes our discussion. Thank you.

title: CRM — Accenture
prompt:
Your client is a CRM (Customer Relationship Management) that utilizes AI to assist other businesses and customers with their
interfaces (e.g., Salesforce) and is experiencing a decline in its market share. Suggest how you would help them.
solution:
Do we have any information on whether the decline in magnitude of the market share is regional or nationwide?
Yes, the decline has been observed Pan-India.
Is the issue internal, meaning something we are doing wrong, or is it external, like facing stronger competitors?
It’s an internal issue.
Is the product not functioning well, is our marketing lacking, or are we falling short in how we support and engage our customers?
The problem lies in customer relations.
1.Customer Support – Are customers receiving the assistance they need when they reach out for help?
2.Onboarding and Training – Are we effectively guiding users when they first start using our CRM?
3.User Experience (UX) – Is our interface frustrating, difficult to navigate, or overly robotic?)
It’s the interface setup that’s what customers are struggling with. Give some potential suggestions to improve the same.
The customers can fill out feedback forms, or designated representatives can reach out to them to provide a more personalized experience. Market Growth & Sizing-
Give a more in-depth solution to the problem.
If users are experiencing issues with the interface, they may feel overwhelmed, confused, or disconnected while using the product. This impacts not
only usability but also their trust in the platform.
To address this, observe real users to identify where they encounter difficulties or frustration in the interface.
Simplify the design by reducing clutter and ensuring that key features are easy to locate and use.
Additionally, implement guided walkthroughs for new users, complete with tips and short tutorials, to help them get started smoothly.
Okay, that concludes your case. Thank you.

title: Financial Services — Kepler Cannon
prompt:
Our majorly specializes in cross-border remittances and is experiencing revenue challenges. However, we are not looking to analyze
why revenue is declining but rather how we can grow our revenue. How would you approach this?
solution:
Understood. Since remittances form majority of revenue, I would break this down into two components:
Total Number of People Sending Remittances
Average Amount Sent Per Person
Do we have any insights on whether the number of people sending remittances or the amount they send has changed?
Yes, the number of people sending remittances has declined. I would use the growth matrix to explore possibilities:
Existing Product, Existing Market
Existing Product, New Market
New Product, Existing Market
New Product, New Market Would you like a detailed analysis of each one?
The is focused solely on remittances and is not considering new products. We can explore growth within existing markets or expand
into new ones. What factors should we assess for market entry? Market Growth & Sizing-
To evaluate new market entry, I would consider:
Market Attractiveness: Assessing remittance demand, customer behavior, and regulatory environment.
Financial Feasibility: Understanding costs and expected revenue.
Operational Feasibility: Ensuring smooth transaction processing and compliance.
Risks: Evaluating geopolitical, economic, and technological risks.
Would you like me to explore any of these in further detail?
That’s a good framework. Now, let’s discuss revenue sources. The generates revenue by charging a percentage fee on each
remittance. How can we increase revenue through this model?
I would categorize revenue growth opportunities into three segments based on transaction types:
Government Transactions: Explore opportunities in defense payments, cross-border grants, and aid remittances.
Business Transactions: Facilitate international trade, such as import-export payments and raw material purchases.
Personal Transactions: Focus on e-commerce and global shopping trends, particularly in fast fashion and consumer goods.
Would you like me to focus on any specific segment?
That’s a useful breakdown. The also earns revenue from onboarding fees charged to businesses that use its services. How can we
increase revenue from onboarding fees? I would apply the 4Ps framework (Product, Price, Promotion, Place) to optimize onboarding fees:
Product: Improve service quality by offering faster or premium-tier transactions for higher fees. For example, urgent money transfers costing
more. Market Growth & Sizing-
Price: Introduce differentiated pricing based on size. Large multinational firms can be charged higher onboarding fees, whereas small
businesses may receive discounted rates to drive adoption.
Promotion: Increase visibility and partnerships in high-growth regions to attract more companies.
Place: Adjust pricing based on digital penetration levels in different countries—charging less in high-volume digital economies or increasing fees
in niche markets.
Would you like me to refine any of these strategies further?
That works.

title: & Consumer electronics — McKinsey & Company
prompt: Your client is a startup looking to open a 3D printer business. You have to estimate the market size for 3D printers in India.
solution:
Sure. Just to clarify, are we only looking at a specific location or pan-India?
Pan-India is fine.
Got it.
Market Size = Number of printers × Price per printer
To estimate the number of 3D printers that can be sold in India, I’ll divide the market into two broad segments: residential and commercial.
Sure, sounds good. Alright, I’ll start with residential.
I’ll begin by considering the total number of households in India, which is approximately 40 crore. This can be further segmented into urban and rural,
but given that 3D printing is a niche product, I believe it’s reasonable to focus on urban households.
So, around 30% of the households are urban, which gives us approximately 12 crore urban households. I’ll divide these urban households into income
segments: Low-income (30%) = 3.6 crore households (but we assume no demand from this segment)
Middle-income (50%) = 6 crore households
High-income (20%) = 2.4 crore households Market Growth & Sizing-
Now, for the adoption rates of 3D printers:
Let’s adjust the adoption rates to match our target of 6.5 lakh printers annually from residential demand:
→
Middle-income (50%): I’ll assume 0.1% adoption rate 0.001 × 6 crore = 60,000 printers
→
High-income (20%): To reach the target, I need to assume 2.5% adoption rate 0.025 × 2.4 crore = 6 lakh printers
Now, the total urban households likely to purchase 3D printers is:
60,000 (middle-income) + 6 lakh (high-income) = 6.6 lakh printers annually.
Sounds good. Let’s move on to the commercial end.
Sure. I’ll break down the commercial demand into three main sectors: Sector, Business Services Sector, and Educational Institutions &
Research Facilities.
Let’s first consider the Sector. Based on available data, there are about 60 lakh relevant industries in India, with the following distribution:
→
Small industries (50 lakh) Assuming 1 printer per , that gives us 50 lakh printers.
→
Medium industries (8 lakh) Assuming an average of 1.2 printers per , that gives us 9.6 lakh printers.
→
Large industries (2 lakh) Assuming an average of 3 printers per , that gives us 6 lakh printers.
So, the total demand from industries would be:
50 lakh + 9.6 lakh + 6 lakh = 65.6 lakh printers. Assuming a 3-year repurchase cycle, we adjust this to approximately 20 lakh printers annually for the Sector.
Next, let’s look at the Business Services Sector like architecture firms, design studios, etc.
I am not aware of the exact number, however can I assume it to be around 30 percent of industries? Market Growth & Sizing-
Let's take it as 10 lakh for both of the following 2 categories.
Alright, assuming 80% adoption, we will have 8 lakh printers annually.
Finally, we have Educational Institutions & Research Facilities, such as schools, colleges, and research labs, where 3D printing can be used for
education or research.
Let’s assume 80% adoption among these 10 lakh institutions:
10 lakh institutions × 80% adoption = 8 lakh printers annually.
Now, combining residential and commercial demand:
Residential demand = 6.6 lakh printers annually
Commercial demand = 20 lakh () + 8 lakh (business services) + 8 lakh (educational institutions) = 36 lakh printers annually
Perfect! Let’s round off and assume it to be 40 lakh. What next?
Now we can move on to estimating the price of each printer on average.
Here’s the breakdown of costs:
Technology & Raw Materials: This includes the hardware components, plastic filament/spools, internal machinery, etc.
→
Estimated at Rs. 5,
Processing & Assembly: This covers the labour and factory overheads required to manufacture and assemble the product. →
Estimated at Rs. 2,
Packaging, Storage & Distribution: This includes packing material, warehousing, transportation to retailers/customers, etc.
→
Estimated at Rs. 3, Market Growth & Sizing-
So, the total cost per unit is Rs. 10,000.
Do these numbers sound right? Is there any percentage or value of margin given?
Let’s take 20% for the margin.
So, the total value per printer would be Rs. 10,000 + Rs. 2000 = Rs. 12,
Now, the market size would be:
40 lakh printers × Rs. 12,000 per printer = Rs. 4800 crore.
Perfect, that’s it for the case. Thank you.

title: Financial Services — Kepler Cannon
prompt:
Let’s discuss a pricing case. Our charges a percentage fee for every transaction between two countries, and we need to determine
what that percentage should be. How would you approach this? We have three primary channels through which customers can send money:
Bank-to-bank transfers: Digital transactions where money is directly transferred from one bank account to another.
Card transactions: Customers use their debit or credit cards to transfer money internationally.
Cash transfers: Customers can deposit cash at partnered grocery stores, which then facilitate the transfer, allowing recipients to withdraw
cash in another country.
Given these options, how would you approach pricing for each channel? Which channel should be priced higher and which one lower? I would price these channels based on the level of effort and costs involved for the . Bank-to-bank transfers require the least intervention,
as they are entirely digital and do not involve third-party partnerships. This means they can be priced the lowest.
Card transactions fall somewhere in the middle because they involve processing fees from card networks, but they are still largely digital.
Cash transfers, on the other hand, require significant operational effort—partnering with grocery stores, managing cash handling, and training store
staff—so they should be priced the highest to reflect these additional costs. Now, imagine an Excel sheet with three key columns: corridor (country pair US to India, India to Africa, etc.), product (transfer type), and channel
(digital or not). There are multiple permutations across 100 country pairs. I want to understand where we can maximize our profits. How would
you analyze this? Pricing Strategy-
I’d start by gathering competitor pricing data for each corridor, product, and channel combination. Then, I would create a deviation analysis
comparing our pricing against competitors.
Identify corridors with the highest deviation: Where we are charging significantly more or less than competitors.
transaction volume—focusing on areas with high volume, as those would be the biggest opportunities for increasing profitability.
Assess ease of price adjustment: Look for corridors where we can change pricing with minimal disruption.
What variable would help us determine if a price change is actionable?
Price elasticity of demand. We need to estimate how sensitive customers in each corridor are to price changes. If demand is highly elastic, reducing
prices could drive more transactions, increasing overall revenue. Conversely, if demand is inelastic, we may have the flexibility to raise fees without
significantly losing volume.
What’s the formula for price elasticity?
The formula is: Elasticity= (% change in price) / (% change in quantity demanded)
Let’s say we identify the top 10 corridors where we can improve profitability. How would you recommend implementing these pricing changes
while minimizing costs?
I would use the 3Cs Framework: Customer – – Competitor
1. Customer Understand price sensitivity by corridor and channel.
Pilot pricing changes in select markets and monitor impact on transaction volume.
Communicate clearly to avoid customer churn. Pricing Strategy-
2. Ensure operational readiness: systems, support, and analytics should handle pricing changes smoothly.
Roll out changes gradually to manage risk and gather learning.
Track performance to ensure revenue gains exceed implementation costs.
3. Competitor
Benchmark pricing against key competitors in each corridor.
Identify underpriced corridors where we can increase fees without losing share.
In highly competitive markets, consider lowering fees to gain volume.
Thank you!

title: Pharmaceuticals — BCG
prompt:
Suppose you are tasked with pricing an immortality pill — a pharmaceutical product that grants indefinite life. How would you approach this
problem?
solution:
Certainly. Given the nature of the product, I would approach pricing using three standard methodologies:
1.Cost-based pricing — Pricing based on production and R&D costs.
2.Value-based pricing — Pricing based on the perceived value of the pill to the customer.
3.Competitor-based pricing — Pricing in comparison to market alternatives.
To begin with, I would like to ask a few clarifying questions. Are there any existing competitors offering a similar product or close substitutes? No, this is a first-of-its-kind product, so there are no direct competitors.
Understood. In that case, competitor-based pricing would not be applicable, so I would focus on cost-based and value-based pricing.
For cost-based pricing, I would require details regarding R&D costs, production costs, and potential sales volume. Could you provide me with these
figures?
Certainly. The total R&D cost incurred was approximately $1 billion, and the other miscellaneous costs such as production, distribution, and marketing sum up to around $2 million.
Thank you. To arrive at a cost-based price, I would need an estimate of the number of units sold to amortize the R&D cost over the expected sales
volume. Do we have any estimates on potential demand? Pricing Strategy- That is a missing piece. Could you outline how you would estimate the demand?
Sure. I would approach this by segmenting the potential market:
1.Global population — Segmenting based on income and affordability.
2.Healthcare institutions — Selling through hospitals or medical facilities.
3.Ultra-high net-worth individuals (UHNWIs) — Who might pay a premium for the product.
The demand could be substantial. However, for practical pricing, I would use a conservative estimate of HNW individuals and calculate the cost per
unit based on a breakeven period of, say, 5 years. Would you like me to calculate a breakeven price, or explore value-based pricing first?
Let's proceed to value-based pricing.
Certainly. Since the product offers indefinite life, its perceived value would be enormous. To quantify this value, I would draw parallels with life
insurance policies, since people typically place a monetary value on their lives through insurance coverage.
Assuming an individual opts for a ₹1 crore life insurance policy, they typically pay around ₹1,000 per month as premium. This equates to ₹12,
annually, and over an average lifespan of 70 years, the total premium paid amounts to approximately ₹8.4 lakh.
Given that the pill effectively guarantees life indefinitely, the perceived value should logically exceed the lifetime insurance payout. To simplify, I
would round up the value and propose a base price of around ₹10 lakh per pill.
InteDreIsFtinFgI aCppUroaLcTh.Y Would you also factor in the exclusivity of the product and the purchasing power of different segments?
Yes, I’d use a tiered pricing model — ₹10 lakh base for general consumers and a premium version for UHNWIs.
Also, early-bird or subscription models could help drive demand and steady revenue.
Excellent Approach. Thank You

title: & Consumer Durables — McKinsey & Company
prompt:
You are the MD of a consumer durables . Your stock prices are underperforming compared to competitors. Identify the issue and

solution:
Preliminary questions:

The produces home appliances, lighting products, and ceiling fans. It operates pan-India and was listed two years ago. It has underperformed since listing but performs well in revenue and profitability.
Stock price performance is influenced by:
Investor sentiment
Future earnings projections
DIndIuFstFryI pCosUitioLnTingY
Liquidity and shareholding patterns
A lower Price-to-Earnings (P/E) ratio may indicate weak investor confidence. Unconventional Possible reasons:

What are your recommendations?
Organic Strategies:
1.Expand to new markets (domestic & international) for revenue stability
2.Strengthen Unique Selling Proposition (USP) – Focus on sustainable production, government incentives
3.Diversify product lines within existing categories
Inorganic Strategies:
Merge with or acquire a well-established player to expand reach and credibility

title: & Banking — McKinsey & Company
prompt: Your client is a bank, and you have to solve the problem that they have been facing with their deposits.
solution:
Before starting with the solution to this, I’d like to clarify, since when has the bank been facing this problem?
They’ve been facing this problem for about 6 months. The bank is big on lending, but its deposits have been a problem.
There are three types of deposits, which are savings, current, and term. What kind of deposits has the bank been facing problems with?
The term deposits have been a little on the slow side, but that's not really of significance to us. Let us look at current account deposits instead
because that’s the main thing that has been affected.
So the current account = Number of Account Holders × Average Deposit per Holder. When the deposits are a problem, I’d like to clarify if the number of account holders is the problem or if the amount deposited is the problem.
The number of account holders has been a problem. Why do you think the number of account holders has been declining?
There are two reasons that I can think of for this:
If it's an -wide problem, then perhaps people are not relying on deposits anymore and are now preferring other methods for putting in their
money. If it's not an -wide problem and is a client-side problem instead, then perhaps it has something to do with the kind of experience that we are
giving our customers, the kind of price that we are offering, or maybe some additional facility that our competitors are providing but we are not.
Alright, thank you!

title: & Unconventional — McKinsey & Company
prompt: Your friend is not happy. You have to help understand why is he not happy?
solution:

How do I know this friend?

Family reasons

Let us focus on professional reasons. He is part of a band and the issues that he is facing are coming from the band only. The reasons could be human or non-human. Under human reasons, we can look at the following:
Internal conflicts within the band
A member may have left the band Unconventional - Under non-human reasons, we can look at: The band might be facing negative publicity
It might not be receiving a good number of gigs
It is a non-human reason but it’s not pertaining to the above reasons.
Ok, so I will take another approach to break it down. It can be before performance, during performance and after performance.
It was post-performance. The issue is coming from the response they are receiving.
We can break it down into general audience and critics.
The issue was coming from critics.
Critics could be further classified as famous music personalities, newspapers and awards.
Yes, they haven’t been able to receive a music award which is Ireland’s biggest music award. What could be the possible reasons for this?
This can be due to personal or professional reasons.
One of the jury members has a personal problem with the band, which is why they never get nominated. What could the recommendations be?
The recommendations can be as follows:

Friend - To look at other awards they can get or try to sort out the issue with jury member.
