profits in real-time, the buy and sell signal, etc. These services being provided by competitors, attract customers. How can a system for tracking
real-time value chains be made? Come up with a value chain for the same.
Firstly, the company will decide about the technology and the employees involved i.e., it can either outsource or have in-house technology. Usually,
tech firms keep in-house technology because of proprietary software or in-house data. Also, there are established factors in the area, so it is best to
keep in-house technology.
Coming to the product, a platform should be set up. For the platform, the sources from where the data should be sourced will be found out, the
sources must provide relevant and accurate data, the platform should be user interface friendly and convenient, it should have graphs, and must
give relevant insights to the same, a proper alert system should be developed so that if the portfolio falls or rises below or above a user determined
level then it over text or mail the client gets a notification or the respective portfolio manager may call the client himself to discuss reallocation.
Thirdly, for reliability, there should be a safety and compliance team for every investment product.
Lastly, there should be a support team for post-customer service to minimize downtime and to make customers familiar in case of the new system.
The organic growth part is done, what can we do for inorganic growth?
The company can look for joint ventures, form strategic alliances, etc.
No, think of something else.
Since currently we only have a plus point in wealth management, perhaps, we can increase the number of plus points in the form of tax planning,
estate planning, etc., apart from just managing their wealth since we have banking relations with the client, and it is more likely that the client will
stay and invest in the company. Moreover, the company can also start doing quantitative trading.
This is it for the case. Thank you.

title: Retail — Accenture
prompt: A watch manufacturer is facing a decline in revenues. You have to analyze the root cause and recommend solutions to improve the situation.
solution:
C: Do we have more information about the company in terms of its location, customer segment and channel of distribution?
So, the company is based out of India and is spread across cities. It caters to the Middle-Income segment and manufactures watches for
females, males and kids. The company has both an online and offline presence.
Okay, do we have information on the percentage of decline and since when are we facing it?
There has been a decline of 20-30% in the last three years.
Okay so, revenue is calculated using the following formula:
Revenue= Number of units sold x Price per unit x Frequency.
Has there been a significant change in any of these aspects particularly?
There has been no change in price & frequency.
So, coming to the number of units sold, the decline could be either because of the change in demand or the change in the supply of the product.
Yes, go ahead.
So, is the decline faced by our competitors as well or is it specific to us?
Yes, the industry is also facing a downfall. However, our decline is more than the industry.
So, I'd like to go by the Supply side first. Understanding the supply value chain, we have the R&D, Procurement, Production & Distribution.
Moving on to R&D, is the company laying focus on the same & constantly evaluating alternatives to better understand the market & come up with
innovative products?
Yes, you can move to the next one.
Has there been any change in the raw materials being used or their price, change in supplies/suppliers, change in transportation medium of raw
materials, etc.?
No, you can proceed.
So coming to Production, have there been changes in the machinery's efficiency/effectiveness, labour availability/cost/productivity, power, etc?
There is an issue with the machinery.
Is the number of machinery we have less in number or is the installed capacity less than required? Or is the efficiency/effectiveness or the
maintenance of the machinery a problem? Has our technology become outdated?
So, the firm has been seeing reduced productivity due to improper maintenance resulting in improper utilization of the installed capacity. Apart
from this, no major issue. You can move to the other side i.e. demand.
Okay, sure, let'
s look at its different aspects - Awareness, Availability, Accessibility, Affordability, Needs/Wants, Customer Experience and Competition.
Coming to awareness, are people aware of the product through our promotional channels?
No problem here.
Next is availability, is it so that our product is not readily available or not easily accessible to the market?
No, availability and accessibility are not an issue.
Coming to affordability, since we cater to the mid-income segment, I don't think that will be a problem. Have our prices changed in recent times?
No, our prices have been stable.
All right, coming to the understanding of the needs of the market & customer tastes & preferences? I believe nowadays people have shifted towards
smartwatches and there has been a great demand in the market for the same.
Yes, you have rightly pointed out that the needs of the market have changed over the years. We manufacture only Analog Watches however, the
customer's preferences have shifted towards smartwatches.
And what does the competitive space look like? Is it extremely competitive?
So, our competitors are making both watches, thus, people prefer it over us. You can move on to the recommendations.
Here are some recommendations:
1. Short term: To collaborate/acquire any digital watch manufacturer to start manufacturing smart watches as soon as possible so that we don
INDUSTRY't lose
a bigger chunk of the market and adapt well to the changing customer needs and wants.
2. In the Long term, I believe new machinery needs to be introduced so that we can manufacture smartwatches achieving economies of scale.
3. For proper attention towards the maintenance & repair of the machinery new supervisors should be adopted.
4. The piles of Analog watches could be sold on sale over e-commerce websites at huge discounts and offers so that the actual target audience
present could buy them.

title: Oil and Gas — Kearney
prompt: Your client is into Upstream Oil & Gas and has reached out to you for growing their top-line revenues.
solution:
C: Could you please let me know what all the upstream oil & gas includes?
Upstream Oil is drilling oil wells which give crude oil mixed with sand and water. Then the oil is refined through various processes stage by stage.
Do we have more information about the company in terms of its location and operations? Any other information about the market structure?
So, the company is based out of India and is not among the top 10 companies. It'
s a joint venture between private equity and a global offshore
maintenance service provider. The company drills oil on a contractual basis.
Thank you for the information. Do we have information on their capacity and the kind of customer segments it caters to?
Yes, their installed capacity is 100 Million barrels of barrels per day. Their customers include upstream oil companies, the operators and owners
of the land.
I'd just like to clarify the objective once that is to grow the topline revenues of the company, right?
Yes, correct!
I'd like to know if we have some information about the market situation in terms of the consumption of such services & what does the competitive
landscape look like?
As I mentioned that the company is not among the 10 top stakeholders in the oil industry. They are profitable being experts in off-shore (i.e. on sea)
oil drilling but they lack expertise when it comes to on-shore drilling. Talking about the market, in India their market share is less than 1% of the
total market and the consumption stands at about 5%.
I see the scope in terms of market size is quite limited as the major customers are restricted to upstream oil companies. Since the upfront expenditure
is so huge the size of the market to tap is limited.
You have made a good point here. The company has its jaws in the Middle East, USA and Russia. Apart from our main objective they want to open
up their operations in a few countries like the Middle East, USA & Russia. Let's explore that first. Why do you think they would want to expand out of
India?
I think the market in the Middle East is either more attractive, offers more operational feasibility or has fewer risks involved as compared to India.
Yeah, there were some weather issues off-shore India. If we set up operations in these countries then what factors should we keep in mind?
Okay so first of all, the top-line revenue can be formulated into
Number.of Customers * Revenue per customer * Y-O-Y Growth
The number of customers depends on market size, urban-to-rural ratio as well as the ratio between industrial area, residential area and green cover.
Revenue per customer largely depends on current income levels and current demand.
We could also consider the risks involved. For eg: Environmental Regulations and areas of export and import.
For market growth, we can take into consideration distribution through new and existing channels.
Let's say their decision is solely dependent on the costs incurred.
Okay, on the cost front, they should compare
Capacity expansion in India(increase in barrels of oil per day) vs set up of value chain in the new market
Management of value chain in India vs management of value chain in the new market
Land and labour costs in both the countries
Excellent! Could you please summarize quickly?
Yeah, sure. So, to start with there are two types of demand in this case: industrial and residential.
To solve the case we need to keep in mind factors such as regulations, environment, management of the value chain, barrels of oil per day, and
population concerning demand. Another point crucial to the case is areas for import and export and the difference between India and our target
country for expansion.
That is it for the case. Thank you!

title: Bookstore — Praxis
prompt: Your client owns the oldest bookstore in Delhi and now wants to open a bookstore in another part of Delhi. This store should be an experiential bookstore, like a book cafe where you can eat and read. Your objective is to increase the revenue by 15-20%.
solution:
C: Has the client decided on this kind of book cafe or are they open to some other kind of experiential bookstore?
Let'
s just focus on book cafes.
What kind of books do they sell? What is their current model and who are their competitors?
It is a standard bookstore that offers a wide range of books for sale and rental. Their main competitors are offline booksellers, as they do not
consider online sellers to be direct competitors due to the in-store experience that they provide. Their business model is B2C, and the focus is on
providing excellent service to their customers.
The first step would be to assess the financial attractiveness of setting up a new bookstore - whether it would be able to achieve the objective or not.
The second step would be to evaluate the operational feasibility - can the client open such a bookstore? What would be required for it?
Lastly, it is important to identify any potential risks and barriers that could impede the success of the bookstore.
What kind of customers would you exactly focus on for this new bookstore?
I would be looking at the age range between teenagers and young adults (16 to 30 years). Additionally, elderly people could also be considered.
We could divide it into three age categories:
16 to 30
30 to 65
65 and above
In the age group of 16 to 30, we could focus on students who are doing distance learning.
We would focus on the percentage of each age category that would be interested in book reading and this experience.
How many libraries are there in Delhi?
Number of libraries in Delhi = Area of Delhi / Area covered by one library
Area covered by one library = Demand fulfilled by one library / Population density of Delhi
Population density of Delhi = Total population of Delhi / Total Area of Delhi
Assumption: Each library = 1 lakh books
If the area of Delhi is 1500 km sq and the total area covered by each library = 80 km sq.
Then, the number of libraries in Delhi = 1500/80 = 18 to 20 libraries.
Since this is a very small number, what should be done?
One thing that can be done is to reduce the number of books there are in a single library. Instead of 1 lac, you keep your library very small and have
multiple libraries.
Great, thank you. We can close the case now.

title: BEVERAGE — L.E.K.
prompt: Our client is a Nimbu Paani vendor outside a college, and recently, his sales have decreased. Examine the underlying cause and make some recommendations to address the problem.
solution:
C: Okay, that'
s interesting. Could you please clarify the timeframe of the situation? Is it before the COVID-19 pandemic, during the pandemic, or after
it?
This is a post-covid situation
I understand. Could you please specify the season during which this event takes place? Is it summer, winter, or monsoon? Are there any unique
things such as exams or festivals, or changes in consumer preferences?
Its summer season and there are no other events going on alongside
Oh, Okay. Are there any new vendors nearby which may have led to an increase in competition?
No, there haven't been any new vendors in the area.
Okay, I see. Are there any notable issues in the storage or transportation aspect of the business?
Yes, there have been issues with storing lemons during the summer months. The heat has been causing spoilage, resulting in losses.
Have there been any changes in the quantity, price, or size of the glass you use?
Yes, we have recently reduced the quantity per glass and increased our prices.
So, if I understand correctly, the decline in sales can be attributed to the changes in the price and quantity ratio, as well as the storage issues causing
spoilage.
Yes, that'
s correct. Let's move on to recommendations to help steer the business back on track. What would you suggest to improve the current
situation?
I will divide my recommendations into two parts: short-term and long-term.
For the short term, I suggest implementing the 3 measures:
1. Firstly, the vendor could use wet clothes as a shelter to protect the lemons from heat, minimizing spoilage. He could also consider relocating the
storage area to a shaded location to reduce the impact of heat.
2. Secondly, purchasing raw materials in smaller quantities but more frequently would help in reducing the storage duration in turn reducing
spoilage.
Moving on to long-term strategies, as upgrading might not be feasible for a Nimbu Paani vendor, I propose looking for alternate storage options,
such as refrigerated containers, to maintain the quality of lemons.
3. Another option could be to tie up with the college authority to secure a dedicated storage space that meets the required temperature conditions.
So, by implementing these short-term and long-term measures, the business can address the storage issues, mitigate spoilage, and ultimately
regain stability and profitability.
Sounds valid. Thank you.

title: Demat A/c — McKinsey
prompt: The client is a demat account opener who wants to make an end-to-end digital process of opening the accounts. Guess the market size of people willing to open an account when the process is entirely digital.
solution:
C: What is the geography and time frame?
It is the 6 main metros of the country. The time frame is irrelevant as only the market size is being analyzed.
Do you want me to include people who already have a Demat account?
Yes, the total strength.
We will look at the household population of the 6 metro cities. We will segment it into income groups and then age brackets. After this, we will see the
net banking penetration in those groups which will depend on certain factors. Further, for someone who has a net-banking account, are they aware
of what a Demat account is and how it works? The last filter would be the average number of demat accounts that households have.
Can you give me an individual analysis instead of a household one?
Sure, we will see the number of individuals in the metros and focus on five factors-
1.
Income
2.
Age
3.
Net-banking penetration
4.
Awareness
5. The average number of Demat accounts
The population is taken as 6 crores. I would like to divide the population into the following four income brackets,
1.
Below the poverty line population, which would not be the target audience
2.
Lower class, which would also not be the target audience
3.
Middle class, which we can take as 30%, i.e. 2 crore people with an average annual income of 2.5 lakh rupees
4.
High class, which we can take as 30%, i.e. 2 crore people with an average annual income of greater than 2.5 lakh rupees.
Further, I would like to segregate the middle and high-class income brackets age-wise as below-
1.
0-18 years, which is not considered
2.
18-65 years, which includes both middle and upper classes within this age bracket
3.
65+ years, which includes both middle and upper classes within this age bracket
For analyzing net-banking penetration, I would take the following approach. In the 18-65 years bracket, net banking penetration would be 100%
considering the metropolitan area, in the 65+ years bracket, penetration would be a lower number, say 40%.
The number of people in these groups having a net-banking account is based on internet provision, general awareness, the willingness of the
population to have a net-banking account, safety concerns, etc.
Finally, looking at this number concerning the average number of demat accounts per person, we can arrive at an approximate value for the market
size.
Okay, we can close the case now.

title: Consumer Goods — L.E.K. Consulting
prompt: Calculate the market size of wedding gowns in India.
solution:
C: Should I calculate the value in terms of market size or the revenue generated through its sales?
Calculate the number of units sold.
Are we considering any particular religion or all the religions in the country?
Only Christian wedding gowns
I will be following the Household approach. The entire population can be divided into 2 categories - Urban and Rural. If it is fine, can I assume that the
rural people cannot afford these gowns?
Okay. Please exclude rural areas.
Furthermore, only females require wedding gowns. So I will further divide the population based on gender. These gowns will be needed by women in
the age group 18 - 35 as this is the age group in which people in India usually get married.
Now, around 2% of India's population comprises Christians. Roughly, India's population is approximately 120 crores. Hence, 2 crores 40 lakh people are
Christians.
Assuming the number of men and women to be equal, a crore 20 lakh people are women. Out of this figure, roughly half of the females would belong
to the urban areas which leaves us with 60 lakh urban females. Further on, only 60% of them would fall in the gap group of 18-35 which gives us 36
lakh females.
Therefore, around 36 lakh wedding gowns are sold in India.
Sounds valid. Thank you.

title: Aviation — PWC India
prompt: Guesstimate the number of viewers of an advertisement flashed outside Kolkata Airport.
solution:
C: Can I know what the daily average footfall of the airport is?
One flight comes to the airport every 10 minutes.
Okay, so that translates into 6 flights per hour and 24 x 6 = 144 flights per day.
For simplicity, we can assume the traffic to be 150 flights per day. Further, we can assume that there are 200 passengers on each flight.
Okay, we can move forward with these assumptions.
Now, we can calculate the daily footfall using the formula:
Number of flights x number of passengers per flight = 150 x 200 = 30,000
We can assume that 60% of passengers notice the advertisement. Can we go ahead with this assumption?
Yes, go ahead.
We can calculate the number of viewers of the advertisement using the formula:
Number of viewers = 60% of daily footfall of the airport = 60% of 30,000 = 18,000 viewers
How would you go about calculating the number of viewers who are converted into customers?
We can assume the conversion rate to be 30%. This means that:
Number of viewers converted into customers = 30% of total viewers = 30% of 18,000 = 5,400 customers
That's it for the guesstimate. Thank you!

title: Insurance — Accenture
prompt: Guesstimate the number of people who have car insurance in India.
solution:
C: To estimate the number of people with car insurance in India, I would start by considering the total number of cars in the country. Usually, anyone
who buys a car is required to have car insurance.
So, you suggest using the number of cars as a starting point. How would you proceed from there to estimate the number of people with car
insurance?
I would suggest assuming that 95% of the people who own a car also have insurance.
Yes, that sounds like a reasonable assumption.
Let us begin by calculating the number of cars in India
The country's population is approximately 140 crores, with 40% residing in urban areas and 60% in rural areas.
Based on these proportions, we will assume that only 5% of individuals in rural areas purchase cars.
The urban population can be further segmented by income levels: Upper (10% of the urban population), Middle (30% of the urban population), and
Lower (60% of the urban population).
Further, I'll assume that the percentage of car owners in each segment is as follows: Upper (100%), Middle (90%), and Lower (20%).
Yes, please continue with the calculations.
Okay sure.
Urban Population = total population * 40% = 56cr
Rural Population = total population * 60% = 84cr
Number of cars in the rural population = 84cr * 5% = 4cr (approx)
Number of cars in the upper-income segment = 56cr * 10% * 100% = 5.6cr (approx)
Number of cars in the middle-income segment = 56cr * 30% * 90% = 15cr (approx)
Number of cars in the lower income segment = 56cr * 60% * 20% = 7cr (approx)
So, total number of cars = 4 + 5.6 + 15 + 7 = 31.6cr
Now, as per my first assumption, car insurances are 95% of the number of cars.
Thus, the number of car insurances is approximately 30cr.
Okay, that seems valid. Thank you.

title: Polythene Bag — Kearney
prompt: Guesstimate the number of poly bags used in a year.
solution:
C: Is any specific area being taken into consideration? And any particular time frame?
Yes, Delhi. You can take it as any year. Say 2021.
Taking the population of Delhi as 2 crores, I'd like to divide the population by 4 (approximate family size in nuclear families in a metropolitan city like
Delhi) to arrive at the household consumption of poly bags.
Therefore, we have some 50 lacs household in Delhi. Assuming 60% of the households live in Urban areas i.e. 30 lacs of Delhi & the remaining live in
rural i.e. 20 lacs. Now looking at the uses of poly bags, polythenes are used for local vegetable shopping, milk purchasing, grocery shopping, and
eatables like chips, cold drinks, etc. We also need to take into consideration the frequency of such transactions.
Number of poly bags used = Number of poly bags used per family × frequency
Poly bags are also heavily reused for other purposes such as packaging lunch boxes, reusing the same for dustbins, shopping etc. In rural areas, I
believe vegetable shopping, purchasing milk, and shopping for items involving low transaction value happen daily. So, their weekly consumption
would be at least 4 times. And not everybody uses poly bags, women in rural areas prefer to carry their cloth bags while shopping, so out of 20 lacs
households, I am assuming that 80% use polythenes regularly because of convenience and ease in Availability of polythenes.
In rural areas, 16 lacs households (20 lacs household*80%) *4*52= 3328 lac polythenes in a year.
In urban areas, generally, people tend to order groceries, even vegetables and milk through apps like Zepto, Blinkit, Amazon Fresh, Instamart, ITC,
Reliance Mart etc and paper bags are generally used for the outer packaging in place of polythenes. And due to their education levels, people prefer
paper, jute and cloth bags over polythenes. Therefore, I am assuming that 70% consume polythenes regularly.
In urban areas, 21 lacs household(30 lacs households*70%)*2*52= 2184 lac polythenes a year
Total poly bag consumption in Delhi in a year = Urban consumption + Rural consumption = 3328 + 2184 = 5512 lac polythenes a year.
Therefore, the approximate consumption of poly bags in Delhi is 5500 lac polythenes a year.
Sounds fair. Your assumptions are correct. Thank you!

title: Home Appliance — PWC India
prompt: Guesstimate the number of refrigerators sold in Kolkata annually.
solution:
C: What is the estimate of the population of Kolkata?
1 crore.
Okay, so the number of refrigerators sold in a year can be calculated using the formula:
No: of refrigerators sold in a year in Kolkata=Total no: of refrigerators owned by the population / average life of a refrigerator
We'll proceed with this approach. How will you calculate the total number of refrigerators owned by the population of Kolkata?
Firstly, we'll assume the average number of members per household to be 4. Consequently,
The number of households in Kolkata = 1 crore / 4=25 Lakhs
Further, we will assume that 60% of the households own 2 refrigerators and the remaining 40% own 1 refrigerator. Can I move forward with these
assumptions?
Yes, go ahead.
Now, we can calculate the total number of refrigerators owned using:
2 x 60% of 25 lakh=2 x 15 lakhs= 30 lakh refrigerators
1 x 40% of 25 lakh=1 x 10 lakhs= 10 lakh refrigerators
Total number of refrigerators owned by the population of Kolkata = 10 lakhs + 30 lakhs = 40 lakh refrigerators
Okay. How will you calculate the number of refrigerators sold in a year?
Can we assume the average life of refrigerators to be 5 years?
Yes, go ahead.
A household will purchase a new refrigerator once its existing one is way past its useful life. This means that we can calculate the number of
refrigerators sold in Kolkata in a year using the formula:
Number of refrigerators sold in a year = total no: of refrigerators owned by the population / average life of a refrigerator
=40 lakh / 5=8 lakh
We can conclude that around 8 lakh refrigerators are sold in Kolkata in a year.
That will be it. Thank you!

title: L.E.K. Consulting Footwear — L.E.K. Consulting Footwear
prompt: Estimate the number of Nike shoes sold in India in a year.
solution:
C: Should I take into account specialized Nike products like Jordans? What about second-hand or resale items?
No, just focus on regular brand new Nike products that are sold as the first transaction.
Understood. What time period does this refer to? Pre-COVID, post-COVID, or during COVID?
This pertains to the post-COVID times.
Got it. Do I need to cater to any particular age group?
No, there are no specific age group requirements.
Alright, let'
s begin with estimating the shoes sold in India first and then we'll calculate Nike's share using its market share.
So, India's population is approximately 140 crores, with 40% residing in urban areas and the remaining 60% in rural areas.
Now, let'
s further segment the urban population based on income levels: 10% of the urban population falls into the upper-class
category, 30% belongs to the middle-class, and the remaining 60% represents the lower-class.
Considering these proportions, it can be assumed that individuals in both rural areas and lower-income groups in urban areas typically do not
purchase Nike shoes due to income constraints. Another condition is that males tend to buy more sports shoes due to less footwear options as
compared to females.
Third assumption is that there are roughly 55% males and 45% females in urban areas.
May I go ahead with these assumptions?
Yes, you can go ahead with these assumptions. 60
Okay, I'll move on to the calculations now.
Urban Population= 140 crores x 40% = 56 crores
Male Upper-class in urban areas= 56 crores x 10% x 55% = 3 crores (approx)
Male Middle-class in urban areas= 56 crores x 30% x 55% = 9 crores (approx)
Males in the upper and middle class in urban areas = 12 crores
Female Upper-class in urban areas= 56 crores x 10% x 45% = 2.5 crores (approx)
Female Middle-class in urban areas= 56 crores x 30% x 45% = 7.5 crores (approx)
Females in the upper and middle class in urban areas= 10 crores
I'd like to take a few more assumptions:
70% of males and 40% of women opt for shoes as footwear choice considering age groups and footwear options
On average,
Males in the upper class will have 2 pairs each
Males in the middle class will have 1 pair each
Females in both upper and middle will have 1 pair each
In our case:
Males with shoes = (2 x 3 crores x 70%) + (1 x 9 crores x 70%) = 10.5 crores
Females with shoes = (1 x 2.5 crores x 40%) + (1 x 7.5 crores x 40%) = 4 crores
So, assuming these shoes are renewed every year, total shoes sold = 14 crores
Now, Nike's market share can be assumed to be around 35%.
So, the number of Nike shoes sold in a year will be 35% of 14 crores which is 5 crores approx.
That'
s it. Thank you. 61

title: Sports — BCN
prompt: Calculate the revenue generated by all sports events in India in 1 year.
solution:
C: Sure sir, should I include all segments or any specific one?
Calculate only the revenue generated by stadiums i.e., revenue generated by the sale of tickets.
I got it, I will be approaching the problem via supply-side. I will first divide the region into tier 1, tier 2 and tier 3 cities
Now the formula which I will be using for calculating revenue is as follows:
Total revenue= Number of stadiums*Number of seats*Average occupancy rate* Cost of an average ticket*Number of matches/events in a year*
Number of cities (Values differ for each tier)
For tier 1 cities, let us assume that there are 20 stadiums per tier 1 city with an avg capacity of 20,000 each and the number of tier 1 cities in India is 10.
Since tier 1 cities attract more crowds and matches occur frequently, an average of 10 events/games would be held in a year with an occupancy rate
of 90%. The cost of an average ticket is assumed to be 5,000.
Total revenue in tier 1 cities= 20*20,000*
.9*5000*10*10= 1,80,00,00,00,000= 18,000 crores Should I start with tier 2 and tier 3 cities?
No need. You did a good job.
Thank you, sir. However, for the other 2 tiers, I would take lesser values as they are not as developed as tier 1 cities.
Yes, that's correct. We can close the case now.

title: Coins — Bain & Company
prompt: You're in a shopping mall. Guesstimate the number of coins in the mall.
solution:
C: What kind of a mall is it i.e. where is it located and what is its size?
Consider a medium-sized mall from Kolkata.
What time of the day is it?
It is a Wednesday afternoon and the time is between 1:30-2 pm.
Okay, can I just have a few minutes?
Yes, sure. Remember to break down the places where the coins would be present.
So the total number of coins in the mall can be calculated using
Number of coins in the mall = Number of coins in shops + Number of coins in employees'and visitors' pockets + Number of coins in the parking
area
Okay, you can assume that the number of loose coins lying around is zero. You can continue with the estimate further.
Firstly, for the shops and the coins present in the registers we can use
Number of coins = Number of shops x Coins per shop
As it is a medium-sized mall let'
s assume there are 4 floors with each floor having 20 shops on average. Can I move forward with that assumption?
Yes, go ahead. 63
Now, for coins per shop, we will use
Coins per shop = Coins per register x Number of registers
Let'
s assume that out of the 20 shops on each floor, 4 are big ones, 7 are medium ones and 9 are small ones. A big store is expected to have 6
registers, a medium is expected to have 3 whereas a small one is only expected to have 1. So in total 54 registers are expected to be present on each
floor.
Alright, what about coins per register?
For coins per register, we can take different approaches where the first one is taking the capacity of each coin compartment in the drawers of the
registers into account.
completely filled)
Number of coins= Volume of coin compartment / Weighted volume of the coins x Certain percentage(assuming these compartments are not
The second would be taking the total cash kept in coins and further dividing it by the denominations.
Number of coins= Total cash kept in coins/ weighted value of a coin
So let'
s go ahead with the second approach.
Can I assume that these stores keep 1000 rupees in coins on average?
Yes, you can. Now go ahead with the segmentation of these coins.
So let'
s assume that out of every 10 coins the company has, one is a 20 rupee coin, four are 10 rupee coins, three are 5 rupee coins, and one each for 1
and 2 rupee coins. This is assumed taking into consideration the facts that 20 rupee coins are relatively new, and 10 and 5 rupee coins are pretty
convenient for buyers.
So the weighted value of a coin would be
Weighted value of coin = Σ(probability of the coin) x (value of the coin)
i.e. 0.1x20 + 0.4x10 + 0.3x5 + 0.1x2 + 0.1x1=6.8 rupees per coin
Number of coins in one cash register = Total amount in coins / weighted value of a coin
Number of coins in one cash register = 1000/6.8 = 145 coins(approx)
Total number of coins in all the shops = 4 floors x 54 registers per floor x 145 coins per register = 31,320 coins
That is a valid number, you can move on to the next component.
All right, moving on to the coins in employees'pockets and in visitors'pockets. We can group them since they are pretty similar and I think a better
segmentation would be by gender since women carry larger handbags which would contain more coins as compared to men's pockets or wallets.
Go ahead. For simplicity let'
s assume that the total number of visitors in the mall is equal to the number of employees.
Total number of employees = number of floors x Σ(size of the shop)x(number of employees) + Mall staff
So going ahead with the bifurcation of our shops and assuming that big shops employ 20 people, medium 10 and small 4, the total no of employees
would be-
Total employees= 4 floors x (4x20+7x10+9x4) employees =744.
Assuming that the number of workers employed by the mall for cleaning and overall maintenance is 100 we end up at a total of 844.
Thus the total number of people in the mall at the time is 844+844= 1688.
Can I go ahead with this number?
Yes, it sounds correct.
So the total number of employees will be 1688, out of which 676 are females and 1012 are males. To calculate the number of coins the employees and
visitors have, can we assume females have 50 rupees in coins and males have 20 rupees in coins?
Yes, it is a fair assumption.
So performing a similar analysis as done above but assuming that in a pool of 20 coins, one will be a 20 rupee coin, five 10 and 5 rupee coins each, six
2 rupee coins, and three 1 rupee coins.
Weighted value of coin = Σ(probability of the coin)x(value of the coin).
The weighted value of a coin =
0.05 x 20 + 0.25 x 10 + 0.25 x 5 + 0.3 x 2 + 0.15 x 1 = 1 + 2.5 + 1.25 + 0.6 + 0.15 = 5.5 rupee.
Now,
Number of coins = Total amount in coins / weighted value of a coin
Number of coins a female carries = 50 / 5.5 = 9 coins (approx).
Similarly, the number of coins a male carries = 20 / 5.5 = 4 coins (approx).
Now we calculate the total number of coins in the employees' and visitors' pockets using
= Number of females x Number of coins a female carries + Number of males x Number of coins a male carries
I.e. 676 x 9 + 1012 x 4 = 10,132
Now, what about parking?
So for parking, we can take the number of parking meters and see if they even have a coin system or not and take the loose coins in cars into
account. Can I assume that the mall has a capacity of 200 cars, the meters are emptied every night, they have a coin system and some employees
of the mall come to the mall in their cars?
Yes, go ahead.
Assuming the cost for parking an hour is 10 rupees and some cars are owned by employees so they will pay their due at the end of the day. Every
hour, 10 cars enter and leave the space out of which 2 pay in coins. Are these assumptions correct?
Yes, go ahead. We can assume that the mall opens at 10 am, no car leaves for the first hour and subsequently, cars leave and come.
Keeping in mind all these assumptions, we can assume that after the first hour- 10 cars enter and leave the space out of which two pay in coins and
the rest pay in cash. As a result by 2 pm, 30 cars will have left the parking space so the total no of coins in the parking meter would be
= Cars leaving the space in an hour x Percentage of cars that will pay in coins x the number of hours
I.e. 10 x 0.2 x 3= 6.
Okay, so is there any other segmentation that you would like to do?
We can look at the mode of transportation that people use to get there. For example, if they are using metros or buses more than cars they'll have
more coins.
Okay, good thinking. Now please give me the total number of coins according to this approach.
Total number of coins would be = Coins in shops + Coins in employees'/visitors' pockets + Coins in the parking space
= 31320 + 10132 + 6 i.e. 41,458.
This is it for the guesstimate. Thank you. 67

title: Online Food Industry — Praxis
prompt: Calculate the number of deliveries done by Swiggy in Delhi in a single day.
solution:
C: Could I start with some preliminary questions?
Yes sure, go ahead.
What kind of deliveries are we talking about? Is it only food deliveries or other deliveries as well? Is it a special day or a weekday?
It is only food deliveries and as for the day, let's assume it to be a weekend.
Alright, let's assume it's a Saturday. I would like to suggest two approaches:
1) Demand side approach: I would split the day into different slots and do a Peak Hour Analysis. Let'
s say 7 am - 11 am and then 11 am - 2 pm,
Total Number of orders = Peak hours x Number of orders in one peak hour + Non-peak hours x Number of orders in non-peak hours
2) Supply side approach: Calculate the deliveries made by the delivery partners.
So, which approach would make more sense?
I would go by the supply-side approach right now; it would be more convenient and would require fewer assumptions.
The formula used:
Total Number of deliveries made by Swiggy in Delhi in a single day = Number of Swiggy delivery partners in Delhi x deliveries made by a delivery partner
each hour x Total number of hours worked by each partner.
Let's assume that there are 1 lakh delivery partners in Delhi.
Okay, so we will calculate the deliveries made by each partner:
The deliveries per hour, per partner = 1 hour / Average trip time x Deliveries made per trip.
Can you calculate how many deliveries a delivery partner can make in an hour?
Yes, sure but before that usually what happens is that delivery partners can make multiple deliveries in a single trip when they leave from a restaurant,
especially in a case when 3-4 orders are made in the same society. Should I factor in this too?
No, the case might get complicated, let's assume that only one order can be delivered per trip
C: Alright,
The average trip time = Time taken by the delivery person to reach the restaurant (10 minutes) + Time taken to pick up the order from the restaurant (5
minutes) + Time taken to travel the distance (25 minutes) + Time spent when the delivery partner calls the customer after they reach the location (1
minute) + Time taken by the delivery partner to reach the main door of the house (5 minutes) + Time taken to hand over the actual delivery to the
customer and asking them to give some ratings (1 minute) +Time taken by the partner to come back to their vehicle which they have parked (3
minutes) = 50 minutes
I believe this number is kind of inflated and the actual number would be around 35 minutes.
Good, let's take it to be 50 minutes to 45 minutes.
Okay, so
No. of deliveries that a partner can make in 1 hour = 60 min/45 min
On average, approximately 1.5 deliveries can be made.
Effective working hours = 16 hours ( 7 am - 11 pm) - 3 hours (rest, time taken to refresh) = 13 hours
Number of deliveries= 1 lac delivery partners * deliveries made per hour (1.5) * total number of hours worked = approximately 19.5 lac deliveries
Would it be the case if 1.5 deliveries are being made every time in a single hour?
No, when many orders are being made (for eg. during lunchtime), instead of 1.5 deliveries, delivery partners might be making 2-3 deliveries.
But during early morning and late night hours, the delivery partners may not be working at their full capacity, so instead of 1.5 deliveries, let's assume
70% of 1.5 deliveries are being made, so we could break down the entire day into different time slots and estimate the number of deliveries that will be
made for each slot.
Let's say one of the time slots is 7 am to 11 am. In this slot, not many orders will be made, so you can say that 70% of 1.5 deliveries will be made so:
Total deliveries from 7 to 11 am = 70% * 1.5 * 4 = 4.2 deliveries
A similar procedure could be followed for the other time slots.
It would basically club the demand side approach and supply side approach, so it is a Peek Hour Analysis plus a bottleneck approach to club
together.
Great, we can close the case now.

title: Animals — McKinsey
prompt: You're in a market and there is a dog in front of you. Your task is to guesstimate the number of hair on his body.
solution:
The approach to this question is to divide the body into various 3D figures and thus simplify the question. For example, the body of the dog can be
taken to be a cylinder, the tail can be taken to be a cone and the face can be a hemisphere. What can the assumption for the number of hair per cm
be?
You can take an assumption that there is x number of hairs on 1 cm square area on the body of the dog.
The approach then is to calculate the area of the various 3d figures and get the answer in cm(s) and then multiply that with x to arrive at the answer.
I'm making a few assumptions to arrive at the precise number, I will be considering the following measurements:
1.
Body (Cylinder):
Length (L): 50 cm
Circumference (C): 30 cm
2.
Tail (Cone):
Length (T): 20 cm
Circumference (C'): 15 cm
3.
Face (Hemisphere):
Radius (F): 10 cm
Now, let'
s calculate the areas of each part:
1.
Body (Cylinder):
Radius (r): C / (2π) = 30 cm / (2π) ≈ 4.77 c m
Area = 2πrL = 2π * 4.77 cm * 50 cm ≈ 1507.96 cm²
2.
Tail (Cone):
Radius (r): C' / (2π) = 15 cm / (2π) ≈ 2.39 cm
Slant height (l): T = 20 cm
Area= πrl = π * 2.39 cm * 20 cm ≈ 301.59 cm²
3.
Face (Hemisphere):
Area = 2πR²/2 = 2π * (10 cm)² / 2 ≈ 314.16 cm²
Total surface area = Area of body + Area of tail + Area of face ≈ 1507.96 cm² + 301.59 cm² + 314.16 cm² ≈ 2123.71 cm²
Finally, assuming an estimated number of x hairs per square centimeter, the total surface area should be multiplied by x to estimate the total
number of hair on the dog's body. Assuming x= 100 hair per square centimeter:
Estimated total number of hair = Total Surface Area * x ≈ 2123.71 cm² * 100 hairs/cm² ≈ 212,371 hairs
That is the correct approach, great job.

title: Sports — Bain
prompt: Since you are quite passionate about cricket, how would you go about a guesstimate for the different sources of revenue at a cricket match?
solution:
C: I am assuming that the ticket prices for front stands are Rs. 2000. Can I go ahead with this assumption?
Yes you may, and I would also like to remind you that some people will get free tickets, like influential people and it'
s highly recommended that
you reduce their charge since they get to watch the match for free.
So while evaluating the revenue generated from tickets alone I will be dividing the stands into two sections: the front stands and the side stands.
For the front stands I assume 1000 people haven't been charged. Since I am assuming there are 10,000 people in the front stands and 1000 weren't
charged, we are left with 9000 people who are to be charged Rs.2000 each. Therefore 9000 x 2000=1,80,00,000 Rs. Now to the side stands wherein I
assume there are 27,000 people, charged Rs. 1,500. This will amount to 27,000 x 1,500=4,05,00,000 Rs.
As for the revenue accrued from the non-ticket sales, I assume there are 8 different stands. Since there are also gaps between the stands, where
each stand has 4 pillars. I assume 32 hoardings in a stadium, which can be the same or for different brands. I also assume Rs. 10 Lakh to be given for
each hoarding. Therefore 10,00,000 x 32= 32,00,000 Rs. Then we come to the matter of merchandise purchased by the fans, however, not everyone
will buy t-shirts when they go to a match. I assume 30% of the crowd will buy the merch which is priced at Rs. 500. 36,000 x 30%=10,800 people, now
10,800 x 500= 54,00,000 Rs.
Now we come to water bottles where let's assume everyone in the front side will buy a water bottle which is worth Rs. 20, causing sales of water
bottles in the front side to be 9000 x 20=1,80,000 Rs. 50% of the crowd will buy cold drinks priced at Rs 40, so 4,500 x 40=1,80,000 Rs worth of cold
drinks will be purchased.
In the side stands, 50% of the 27,000 people will buy a water bottle, priced at Rs. 20. This leads to total sales of water bottles in the side stands being
13500 x 20= 2,70,000 Rs. Meanwhile, for the cold drinks, I assume only 20% will buy cold drinks. 27,000 x 20%=5400, then, 5400 x 40=2,16,000 Rs.
Total Revenue = Revenue from the sale of Cold Drinks+Revenue from the sale of Water
=1,80,000+1,80,000+2,70,000+2,16,000
=8,48,000 Rs.
I am satisfied with this model as you have covered all the points. We can conclude the case.

title: BCG Automobile — BCG Automobile
prompt: Estimate the number of car tyres sold in Delhi NCR in a year.
solution:
C: Are we contemplating the purchase of new car tyres, the replacement of existing tyres, or both?
Both cases.
Okay, are we considering private vehicles, commercial vehicles or both?
Take into consideration private vehicles only.
Okay, which types of tyres are we contemplating - tube tyres or run-flat tyres?
We're considering all types of tyres.
Alright. I understand that each car is typically sold with five tyres, including the spare tyre. Furthermore, I will also take into consideration the
differentiation between diesel and petrol cars, as diesel cars have a limited lifespan of 10 years in Delhi NCR and petrol cars have 15 years.
Sounds valid. You get brownie points for that.
So, based on the information, we will start by dividing the population by 4 to determine the number of families, assuming each family consists of four
members. Additionally, we will allocate the number of families into different income segments. This will help us estimate the number of cars in Delhi.
Population of Delhi= 2 crore
Number of families would be= 2 cr /4 = 50 lakh families
We suppose the income segmentation to be
Lower class 30%
Middle class 60%
Upper class 10% 75
Okay, that seems fine.
Furthermore, we will assume that 10% of the low-income group own 1 car. In the middle-income group, we will suppose that 30% own 1 car and 20%
own 2 cars while the other 50% do not own a single car. In the High-income group, we will consider that all own a car, with 40% of them owning 2 cars
and 60% of them owning 3 cars.
This way we can calculate the total number of cars in Delhi.
Great approach, now carry on to solve the guesstimate.
Lower class = 10% of 30% of 50 lakh = 10*30*50,00,000 / 100*100
=1.5 lakh
Middle class = (30% of 60% of 50 lakh) + (20% of 60% of 50 lakh) * 2
= 9 lakh + 12 lakh
= 21 lakh
Upper class = (40% of 10% of 50 lakh) * 2 + (60% of 10% of 50 lakh) * 3
= 4 lakh + 9 lakh
= 13 lakh
The total number of cars in Delhi comes out to be around 35.5 lahks.
So, we assume that every car needs to get its tyres replaced after 5 years on an average. We also assume that out of the total cars in Delhi, 25% are
new ones, which have been purchased in that specific year. This way, we will directly have the number of tyres sold in a new car.
New Cars sold in Delhi= 25% of 35.5 lakh = 8,87,500 cars
Older Cars Present in Delhi= 75% of 35.5 lakh = 26,62,500 cars
Now for the older cars, let'
s suppose that 15% of the older cars need all 4 tyres replaced in a year, as the spare tyre is usually a newer one and does
not require getting replaced.
20% needing 2 tyres changed in a year
15% needing only 1 tyre changed in a year.
We also assume that people only get either 1 or 2 or all 4 tyres replaced.
The number of cars needing all 4 tyres replaced= is 15% of 26,62,500 =3,99,375 cars
The number of cars needing 2 tyres replaced= 20% of 26,62,500 =5,32,500 cars
The number of cars needing only 1 tyre replaced= 15% of 26,62,500 =3,99,375 cars
Total number of tyres sold in Delhi in New cars = 8,87,500 cars * 5 = 44 lakh tyres approx
Total number of tyres sold in older car = 3,99,375 cars * 4 + 5,32,500 cars * 2 + 3,99,375 cars * 1
= 16 lakh + 10 lakh + 4 lakh tyres approx
= 30 lakh tyres approx
Total tyres sold in Delhi = 74 lakh tyres approx.
Very good, you have estimated that accurately, we can wind this up now.

title: Airline food catering — BCG
prompt: Your client is an airline catering company that provides food packets to regional airlines. The problem that they are facing is that their cost per packet is 20% higher than that of their competitor. There's only one competitor in this market. And for simplicity, assume that the only ingredient they use is rice. It is a very regulated industry and airlines can only secure packets from these two companies, and these caterers can in turn only procure their ingredients from panel vendors. Those vendors publish a rate card at the end of every quarter and they have a very strict "no exceptions, no discount" policy. Identify the reason for the increase in costs.
solution:
C: The costs could be increasing due to an increase in either of the components of the cost structure. This would include payment terms, which entail
the method of payment to vendors, and transportation costs.
I would recommend that you ignore the payment terms and transportation costs. What other factors could be behind the increase in costs?
Okay, then there's the inventory cost in which the company can be disadvantaged if the company buys in a quarter in which the price of rice is
higher due to volatility in markets.
If we are able to solve this problem, we would have a 5% improvement.
It is also possible that the company is facing higher fixed or variable costs. Fixed costs do not vary with the level of output produced while variable
costs, such as the cost of raw material/ingredients, vary with the level of output produced.
The fixed costs of the company have not changed. The problem lies in the cost of ingredients, in this case, rice, which has been increasing at an
alarming rate. What could be the reasons for this?
Since rice is the only ingredient, we can estimate the cost per packet using the formula: quantity of rice per packet * per unit cost of rice. Now, an
increase in the cost per packet could be due to an increase in the quantity of rice used per packet or due to an increase in the per unit cost of rice.
The per unit cost of rice has not changed. The problem lies in the quantity of rice per packet, which has increased. What could be the reasons for
this?
An increase in the quantity of rice used per packet could arise due to wastage during the process of transportation of ingredients or during
manufacturing.
You are correct, the problem arose mainly due to the wastage of ingredients. That is it for the case, thank you!

title: Manufacturing — McKinsey
prompt: Your client is the second biggest machine manufacturer in the world and they are situated in Kanpur. Their profitability has taken a hit in the past few years. Analyze the reasons for the same.
solution:
C: The profit of a company = Revenue - Costs. So a decline in profits could be due to an increase in costs, a decrease in revenues or a combination of
both.
The revenues of the company have been growing at roughly the same rate over the past couple of years and show no traces of decline.
So the problem lies in the costs of the company. The company's costs have been increasing at rates faster than their revenues. Now, the costs of a
company can be divided into fixed costs, which are independent of the number of units produced, and variable costs, which vary with the number of
units produced.
The variable costs have increased over the past couple of years. Could you state some of the reasons for this?
To do that, I will go chronologically through the company's value chain. So we will procure the necessary raw materials. This will constitute the
sourcing costs. Then the raw material will be transported to the manufacturing site, which will generate transportation costs. There'll also be inbound
logistics, like the inbound inventory of raw materials. Further, manufacturing can have various issues- like capital, labour, machines etc. Then we'll
have to push products to distributors, which will entail further costs.
I would like you to focus on the very high raw material component costs of machines by giving some solutions to reducing these costs.
One solution would be to conduct research on cheaper materials which can be used. Next, we should look for nearby sources so as to reduce
transportation costs. Talking to suppliers and helping them with technology will greatly help in reducing sourcing costs. We should also invest in R&D
and try to design our machines in a manner that reduces the number of components required.
Ok, so suppose you are the designer of the machines, how will you go about designing your machines in a more cost-effective way? What is your
process going to look like?
It's going to be based on the shape, size, durability, and weight. I'll look at the machines of the company's competitors, reverse engineer them,
analyze all their components, work on solving the shortcomings in their machines and develop a product that uses fewer components. However, at
the same time, I'll make sure to maintain the uniqueness of the company's product so that the customer's perception of the product does not
change.
That was all for the case, thank you!

title: Retail — Kearney
prompt: Your client is a Cricket Bats manufacturer and is facing a decline in revenues. Analyze the reason behind the same and suggest some measures to improve the situation.
solution:
C: Alright, so before proceeding ahead, I'd like to know more about the client in terms of the location of the company, outlets, customer segment it
caters to, etc.
So, the company is based out of India only and has outlets spread across India. I would like you to identify the customer segment.
Based on usage, the customers can be divided into 2 segments: professional use & personal use. Under professional use, bats are generally used for
playing matches, tournaments, leagues, training purposes etc.
The company sells bats for both categories and is among the top 3 in the relative market space.
Does the company only manufacture bats or other products as well?
The company only manufactures cricket bats- both heavy and lightweight depending upon the usage.
Alright, I'd also like to know how much decline the company is facing and since when?
There has been a decline of 20% since the last quarter.
Are our competitors also experiencing a similar recession?
No, it is limited to us.
Revenue=No. of bats sold× price per unit of bat×frequency of purchasing a bat.
Has there been a change in one of them or should I analyze each?
So, the prices have been stable and the frequency is unchanged.
Has the number of units sold declined particularly for any of the two customer segments or is the percentage decline the same for both?
So, the sales have particularly dropped for professional usage of cricket bats.
Coming to the decline in the number of bats could either be a demand problem or a supply-side problem.
Analyzing the supply situation first as the problem is specific to our company.
Yes, that'
s a fair approach. However, I'd like you to look at the problem from a different perspective. What could be the various factors affecting
the supply in the business environment?
We can look at the PESTLE approach as it will help us understand the underlying factors responsible for the same.
The following 6 factors could be responsible:
Political factors: the government in power, the policies and schemes regarding production capacity, and following certain mandates regarding
the production of goods and services.
Social factors: expectations and social obligations that a firm has to fulfil.
Economic factors: the situation of the economy in terms of prices of commodities, inflationary/recessionary concerns, peace in society,
competition in the market, and barriers to entry and exit.
Technological factors: a significant change in the technology being leveraged for production affecting the efficiency and effectiveness of the
labourers, this also calls for training purposes & focusing on capex.
Legal factors: production of goods in compliance with laws & restrictions, following legal mandates.
Environmental factors: producing goods & services while taking care of the environment so that the goals of sustainability could be achieved.
Has any of the aforementioned factors led to a decline in the number of units being sold?
So you have carefully analyzed the possible factors affecting the sales. So, according to a recent government & legal mandate the heavyweight
bats'have to be reduced by 10 grams each so that it fits the requirement of the policy.
Okay, so I believe this in turn affects the look and feel of the bat and professionals especially would be discouraged to buy such bats and this will also
affect the sales of lightweight bats.
You have rightly identified the actual reason behind the problem! That'll be all.

title: E-commerce — Bain & Company
prompt: Your client is an online marketplace, a national platform and they've been noticing that their supply chain costs have gone up. Help us in identifying the reasons and give recommendations.
solution:
C: What kind of an online marketplace are we talking about?
Take something like Flipkart.
So before we start can I ask some questions?
Yes sure, go ahead!
Are we the seller?
We procure the goods from the sellers.
Do we have our own warehouses and ship to customers or do we get it sent directly from the sellers to the customers?
We pack the goods at our warehouses and then ship them.
What type of shipping do we do, is it long haul shipping or last man shipping and is it city to city or inside a city?
We ship on a long-haul basis.
Can I get some time to make a system for the complete supply chain and we can discuss area-wise to find out where the problem lies or where we
can make improvements?
Your client has already tried the basic stuff so don't mention that, they have a lot of data so maybe try to use that.
So let's begin with the supply chain. You begin with procuring the goods from the seller, then you transport them to your warehouse and then put
them in your storage. Now we arrive at the logistics part of the chain where you give the goods to a logistics company with whom you have a
contract that gives it to your customers and then the customers make the payment or maybe the payment has been made earlier if it'
s an online
payment. This part of the supply chain is one that many people forget - returns and replacements. So, in the end, we charge a delivery cost from the
customers. What is the supply chain cost that we bear ourselves?
We do charge a delivery cost but we don't pass on all the costs to the customers.
So for the recommendations let'
s take a backward process. Since we end the supply chain by charging delivery costs, I suggest passing on a higher
proportion of that cost to the customer depending on the nature of the good.
Since we have a lot of data we can figure out if the customer needs the goods on an urgent basis or if he wants the product at a later date, we can
then figure out where to pass a higher delivery charge or a lower one.
So you are recommending a delivery system where the delivery fees would be charged based on when you want the goods to be delivered. Yes
makes sense. Do you have more recommendations?
Now as we move backward in our supply chain my recommendations for reverse logistics are mainly based on the idea of how to reduce returns
and minimize replacement costs, making it more efficient.
So for wrong, defective and damaged products you can tackle them by ensuring better packaging, good training of employees, and rigid quality
control, you can look at your data to find outliers. You can also dispose of the product, recycle it or give it back to the seller, we can explore all 3
options on a case-by-case basis by using the extensive data the company has.
We can find a way to gain maximal value out of the returned products taking all costs into consideration and relations with suppliers. Minimize your
transport and handling costs.
Okay, move ahead with the other steps in the supply chain.
For logistics, the client will most probably have an external contract that can be studied, we can look for better alternatives and see if our logistics
needs are being met efficiently without paying for stuff we don't need.
If the company is facing specific problems related to its procurement of goods how do you care to handle them?
The process of procuring goods from the sellers could be optimized in a way that they are done in batches. Whereas for storage, there are a lot of
steps involved and maybe there is an inefficiency present there that we can work on. We can figure out a way to reduce the storage requirements in
the first place by making a more efficient procurement algorithm.
What are other ways you think we can analyze the supply chain?
We can look at setting up our supply chain although that is a more expensive option. We can even use the data to look at a supply chain model and
cut costs by working in bulk where we can, for example, have a hub or a decentralized system.
That is it for the case. Thank you!

title: Tractor — BCG
prompt: Your client is a Tractor Manufacturer. You have to help them increase their profits.
solution:
C: Can I start with some preliminary questions?
Sure, go ahead.
How has the industry been performing?
The industry has been doing well.
What are the major products that we have or sell?
For the simplicity of this case, consider it to be only 1 tractor.
Who are the customers?
It'
s mainly farmers from the rural areas.
What is the geography of production and distribution?
For production, take any North Indian state (say U.P.) and the distribution happens all over India through end-to-end manufacturing stalls in
rural areas.
What has been the problem and is it specific to a particular place?
The profit has declined 10-15% in the past 6 months and the problem is not specific to any geographic location within India.
Thank you for answering my questions. So we begin by taking the equation of
Profits = Revenue - Cost
We further break down Revenue into Supply and Demand, and Supply further into Production, Distribution and Demand. Now, which area does the
problem lie in?
So, the problem lies in the demand.
Demand comprises external and internal demand. By hypothesizing that the issue is an internal problem using the Pestle framework, we can
eliminate the external demand factors.
For Internal Demand, the customer journey can be broken down into pre-sales, during-sales, and post-sales.
Sorry to interrupt but at this point, you can assume that the problem arises during the post-sales phase.
Thank you for the clarification. Now within the post-sales phase, we have factors such as insurance, payment mechanisms, and after-sales services.
So I would index these metrics with those of other competitors since they have been successful.
Thank you for the clarification. Now within the post-sales phase, we have factors such as insurance, payment mechanisms, and after-sales services.
So I would index these metrics with those of other competitors since they have been successful.
At this point, assume that the problem is a result of the payment mechanism.
Well, if that'
s the case, I'll assume that since payments are done through E.M.I. and loans, the competitors have cheaper/more affordable options
than our firm.
That is indeed the case and now problems are arising as a result of the competitors'tying up with new banks and now issuing lower priced/cost
E.M.I. Now what would you recommend as a solution for this case?
Let me provide short-term and long-term solutions.
For the short term; we can provide easier paying mechanisms such as BNPL. Also since there is no product differentiation between ours and the
competitors, we can add tools that help in farming as attachments, making our product different and thereby increasing sales.
For the long term; we will have to reach out to better banks and partners for lower-cost E.M.I.
That is it for the case. Thank you!
Brownie Points-
The Interviewer really liked the breakdown of internal and external demand.
By eliminating the external demand factors, the customer journey could be addressed directly.
Instead of breaking down the customer journey into pre-sales, sales and post-sales which are a bit vague, it'
s better if you break it down into
actual steps of the customer journey.

title: FMCG — BCG
prompt: The client is a biscuit manufacturer who has been seeing a decline in his profits for the last 6 months. You need to identify the reasons behind this.
solution:
C: Can I ask you a few questions about the company as I move ahead to solve the case?
Sure. Go ahead!
What are the products and how are they priced?
The pricing of the products is similar to competitors which are at competitive market prices. The client has two products one is a regular biscuit
like Tiger, parle g which holds 90% of the business and the other one is a chocolate cream biscuit which holds 10% of the business.
Where is the operational base of the company?
They majorly work in western India, where the manufacturing units are set up and the distribution takes place.
As there has been a decline in profits for the last 6 months, is it across the industry or specific to our client?
It is specific to our client.
Is it specific to a particular unit or across all units of our client?
Across all units.
As Profit = Revenue - Cost, should I focus on the decrease in revenue or an increase in cost as profits have declined?
First, give me the major revenue streams of the biscuit brand.
The revenue of the firm is majorly generated from both the goods sold i.e. the basic biscuit and the chocolate cream biscuit.
Revenue for the company = No. of units sold x Price of a unit
Alright, however, our client does not face a problem on the revenue side, let'
s look at the cost side. What are the major costs a biscuit
manufacturer faces?
So to look at the cost side we can take a look at the supply chain first. We have a B2C market, where the client does the raw material procurement,
manufacturing and distribution, at the end giving it to the distributors who sell it to retailers who further sell it through retail stores.
Now we can divide the cost factors into two which are Fixed costs and Variable costs.
For fixed costs, we have Land, Labour, Building, Power Equipment and Machinery. For variable costs, we have the Raw Material, Transportation,
Processing, Storage, Packaging, Distribution and Marketing-related expenses.
We have been noticing an increase in the variable costs, can you identify where the problem is arising in this section?
I can take you through the variable costs involved in each step of the supply chain.
Go ahead with it.
So for raw material procurement, we have the supplier margin, transportation, and cost of the raw material.
Secondly, for transportation, it depends on whether it'
s outsourced or if a truck is used for transportation that has either been leased or if it is in part
then the costs comprise wages and fuel costs.
Thirdly, for processing, we have land, labour, and power costs.
Whereas for manufacturing, we include machinery costs such as repair, and servicing.
In packaging we have the material that we use for packaging and also the machinery and labour we need for that.
For storage again we have labour, land and power. Also, the risk of theft is present. Lastly, for distribution, we only have the distributor margin.
Can you elaborate on the costs involved in raw material procurement?
In raw material procurement, the cost can be different mainly due to 2 reasons- change in raw material or supply mix.
Right, the client has changed the raw material used in the last 6 months. However, this led to a decrease in costs so it cannot be the reason for a
decrease in profits. Do you need me to cover anything else?
Let'
s go to manufacturing and you can tell me if there is anything that can lead to an increase in the cost but before we go into the components
can you tell me how a biscuit is manufactured?
So there are primarily four stages of making the biscuit in a factory - mixing, forming, baking, and cooling. In the mixing stage, flour, sugar, water and
other ingredients are mixed in the right proportion in large mixers to form the dough. The forming period is related to the setting up of dough on large
plates. After that, the dough is baked and allowed to cool down for a bit, following which it is cut into many small biscuits.
You are talking about an FMCG product and your goods are consumed almost daily so there is a specific criterion that FMCG products need to
pass. This is prominently active in electronic goods and electronic appliances.
There is a quality check that the biscuits need to pass which takes place right before packaging and right after manufacturing.
Yes, the reason behind the decline in profits is quality checks. When it comes to quality check half of our biscuits crumbled and half of our
biscuits don't hold their shape. Can you answer why?
Sir, we have only been seeing this for 6 months and that is the time frame where we have been using the new raw material so maybe the new
material requires a new manufacturing mechanism which we haven't tried till now. It could be a possibility that the dough needs to be made at a
different mixing ratio or maybe the biscuit needs to be baked at a higher temperature or for a longer time.
That is correct, we need to give it more setting time however our client has not been able to identify this till now and that is the issue.

title: Aviation — Bain and Company
prompt: Your client is SpiceJet, and they have a declining profit when compared to Indigo. Why is that happening and what are some recommendations that you could give regarding the same?
solution:
C: Could I ask some preliminary questions?
Yes, sure, go ahead.
For how long has this been happening? Is this region, route or industry-specific?
So, this has been happening for the past 1-2 years and this is not region or route specific and instead affects the entirety of SpiceJet's revenue
pan-India. Also, this is not an industry-specific problem either (since Indigo is fairly profitable and not facing any issues).
Okay, to carry out the analysis of revenue, I will take into account the various revenue streams present in the airline industry.
Total Revenue = Passenger Service + In-flight sales + Tools and Packages + Advertisements and global media partners.
in-flight sales, advertisements and tools and packages are not of major concern for us right now, hence I want you to focus on passenger
service for now.
Okay, the revenue from passenger service can be understood by looking at the ticket price. The revenue here is basically
Revenue= Ticket price * No. of tickets sold
Ticket pricing is not a problem. However, it is the quantity which is having difficulty. Furthermore, when comparing domestic routes to
international routes, domestic routes have been fairly stable and have not experienced much change, while international routes (Dubai, Hong
Kong and the Maldives) are facing problems. To understand the problem further, let's look at its competitor Indigo.
Yes, sure. I will carry out a comparative analysis of SpiceJet and Indigo in this context. Now, Indigo has many more international routes, which also
happen to be extremely active. In comparison, SpiceJet caters to fewer international routes, which are tourist/holiday destinations that are not
frequented as much, leading to a stagnation in revenue for SpiceJet.
Great job on the analysis of Indigo and while it is correct, it has been observed that Indigo has a major control over its costs. What are your views
on the same?
Okay, so coming to cost, I first take some major cost drivers:
the aviation fuel cost (the increase which had to be computed);
the nature of the aircraft (whether it was purchased or on lease) and that of the lease of the storage facility;
the employee cost.
Out of these, fuel and employee costs are consistent across the industry.
All right, does SpiceJet own aircraft?
Yes, it does. What do you think are the major costs incurred due to this?
The various costs are fuel costs, maintenance ( Excluding in-house labour), insurance and storage of aircraft. Which one of these could be a
problem?
The cost of storage of aircraft has increased which is a major concern for us. What are your consequent recommendations?
We can have long-term and short-term recommendations:
Under short term,
We can try to focus on increasing revenue so that there is a subsequent increase in profits. One way to do that is by increasing marketing efforts,
which would involve tie-ups with tourism companies (for packages for foreign destinations which favour SpiceJet); and looking into routes which
had sufficient travel demands at the moment.
We can also look at cutting down on costs. One way to do that is by removing areas not currently in use and which only serve to put an additional
burden on the company
Under long-term recommendations,
Instead of owning all aircraft, we can look to lease some of them or even lease out our aircraft to other companies to have an additional revenue
source
Great, thank you.

title: RETAIL — KEARNEY
prompt: One of the big format retail stores such as D-mart are facing losses. Identify the reason behind it and suggest some recommendations to deal with this problem.
solution:
C: Alright, could you please tell me when was this issue faced, was it during Covid? And were there any other factors such as the rise of delivery
companies like Dunzo, zepto etc?
No, we are not considering that it existed in the times of Covid and no such formats were present at that time.
Okay, can I please know more about the store as to how many branches are facing this issue and what is the customer base?
Okay, the company is facing losses in one particular branch and considers the customer base the same as D-mart or Big Bazaar
Got it, After breaking down the supply chain, it basically depends on two factors: the number of units and the average cost. So could you tell me
whether there's a decrease in the number of units sold or an increase in the average input costs?
So basically, the average order amount is falling per person even though the number of people has increased.
So according to the profitability formula, Profit = Revenue - Cost
Based on the information provided, the problem lies in the falling average order amount despite an increase in the number of people. This suggests
that the revenue may not be growing at the same rate as the number of customers. This situation leads to lower profitability if costs remain relatively
constant or increase.
Then, the problems are : Competitive Pricing which leads to lower revenues and Inefficient Inventory management leading to higher costs and thus,
leading to overall losses.

title: Consumer Goods — McKinsey
prompt: Your client is a consumer goods player in chicken meat with a plant in Bangkok. Optimize logistics cost with the target as 10% reduction of cost in one year.
solution:
C: Could you provide more details about the nature of the business, its location, and operating timings?
Certainly. The client operates as a B2B player and serves a network of 20-30 vendors through fragmented transportation.
These vendors are located in different areas, approximately 30-40 kilometers away from the plant.
Could you elaborate more on the working hours or delivery timings?
Certainly. There is a fixed delivery timeline, with deliveries scheduled at various times between a two hour window from 8 am and 10 am every
day. These deliveries are made to the vendors at different locations within these hours.
What about the transportation aspect?
Various trucks go for delivery and deliver to 4 to 5 vendors each. The transportation operations are handled by a third-party agency. Our
objective is to reduce the costs associated with their services.
Understood. So, transportation costs depend on factors such as truck hours and distance traveled, which need to be minimized to achieve cost
savings.
So, firstly
Total cost = number of trucks X cost of each truck.
The number of trucks can be determined by the following formula
Number of Trucks = Quantity Demanded / Quantity that can be accommodated in each truck
Additionally, the cost per truck can be calculated by taking into consideration the fuel expenses and the number of hours it is utilized.
How do you suggest optimizing the capacity of the truck? The client is currently utilizing smaller trucks for transportation and has implemented
network optimization by installing a GPS system.
To optimize capacity, we can focus on utilizing larger trucks. This will allow us to transport a greater quantity on each trip.
That'
s an interesting suggestion. Now, what are your recommendations for reducing fuel costs?
I have three recommendations:
Firstly, implementing network planning using GPS systems can help us track location and time, allowing for optimized coding.
Secondly, forward integration can be explored as a potential solution.
Lastly, we can group vendors based on specific criteria, such as assigning one truck per vendor based on their location and preferred delivery time.
Thank you, that will be all for now.

title: Chewing Gum — Bain
prompt: Your client is a bubblegum and mint manufacturer and they have been witnessing a decline in profits. Your main objective here is to delve into the decrease in profits and find out an accurate reason for this decline.
solution:
C: Can I ask you a few questions about the company before I proceed to solve the case?
Yes sure, you can ask me questions related to the company's profile and work.
Thank you. So, firstly, for how long and by how much are the profits declining?
The firm has been seeing a decline in profits for 3 years now. In the first year, they faced a 5% decline, then it became 10% and then 15%. Essentially
every year the profits are declining by 5% more.
Okay, also, can you please let me know where the product is produced and where it's distributed?
The production goes through in only one factory and the products are distributed PAN India. Local tea shops account for 75% of sales, while small
retail shops account for 25% of the sales.
What are the different products the company produces and are there only certain categories of products that faced the decline?
The company produces bubble gum and mints. The decline was observed in all the categories of products produced.
What is the size of packaging that the company uses for its products? Could you please throw some light on the price point?
Individual packets of gum are sold priced at about Rs 1-2 which is similar to the rest of the sellers in the market.
Was the company facing the same level of decline all across India?
Yes.
