Also, what about the rest of the companies in the industry? Were they also facing a similar decline?
Big brands such as Wrigley's selling their products in big stores do not face such a decline, however, big and trim both brands that have been
making sales in tea stalls and small retail shops have been witnessing a similar decline.
Profit has two components, revenue, and cost since
Profit=Revenue - Cost
Therefore, the decline in profits could be due to either a decrease in revenue, an increase in costs, or a combination of the two.
I want you to focus on the revenue aspect in this case.
We can break the decline in revenue.
Revenue = Number of Units * Price per unit
Price per unit would not be a problem since we're around industry margins and thus, it shouldn't hurt us.
All right, let's now move on to quantity.
We will break down the quantity as well. This seems to be an industry-wide issue. I want to proceed with a formula-based approach.
Quantity = Number of people entering tea shops and small retail shops * percentage of people buying chewing gum or mints * percentage buying
our brand
The situation can now be broken down into internal and external factors, where under internal I would look at the product and the company
reputation, and so on. But this will be eliminated after noticing that this was an industry-wide problem.
Good, let's focus on external factors.
Under external circumstances, we can look at the customer journey of buying chewing gum. It should be about people entering tea shops or small
retail stores, so accessibility to stores. Then I will look at their experience inside the tea store. The factors to be considered will be the promotion from
the seller's end, in the way that he is selling chewing gums/mints, where he is placing the products in the shop, how he's promoting it, and incentives
to purchase it.
Okay, any further analysis?
Generally, people smoke and then have chewing gum/mint. We can focus on the conversion rate of people smoking and chewing gum.
That is correct. Go on.
Our profits have been declining for the past three years, thus, we should take Covid into account.
There hasn't been a major shift in consumer preference patterns. What else do you think it could be?
In that case, we can focus on the payments aspect. This situation can be broken down into methods of payment and ease of payment. Under ease
of payment, majorly, there are difficulties in making payments or some sort of problem with change. As for methods of payment, UPI has become
very important.
Yes, the problem is the combination of these two aspects. These people have not started UPI payments yet, and earlier, the cost of cigarettes was
19 rupees, so having your 1 rupee chewing gum made you pay just 20. But now the cost of cigarettes is 20 rupees, so when a customer pays 20
rupees for cigarettes he is left with no chance to buy the chewing gum. Can you give your recommendations?
We have to promote UPI because UPI is going to be the future of payments.
We can look at having bigger batch sizes and push the bigger batches in small retail rather than cigarette shops, because in cigarette shops when
you're coming you have to buy maybe one or two gums maximum. So we should look to sell in batches, buy and sell these batches in retail, not in
cigarette shops.
For the cigarette shops in particular, some kind of discount system could be worked out where until we're able to get the UPI structure, maybe if
could work out some discount system where we can make the cigarettes 19 rupees, subsidize them, and work out an agreement with the cigarette
sellers. If these cigarette shops work on credit where you pay off your tab usually at the end of the month, in such a case a customer is more likely
to spend that extra rupee. So structuring systems like this to pay in weekly/monthly instalments should also be implemented.
We could also enter big retail shops, however, we'd have to pay huge margins there, so we can do a cost-benefit analysis and look at it in the long
run.
Thank you, we can close the case now.

title: Fashion — McKinsey
prompt: Your client is a deodorant manufacturer and has suffered a loss in profitability for the past six months. Figure out why and provide your
solution:
appropriate recommendations
Can I start with some preliminary questions?
Yes sure, please go ahead
Thank you, so what does a decline in profitability exactly mean profitability is different from declining profits and profitability essentially refers to the
margin
Yes, here profitability is the margin essentially which has declined by 3.5%. What that means is if the profit margin was 10% earlier now it is 6.5%
Okay and is this problem isolated to us or other deodorant manufacturers in the industry also?
No, It is just us.
What exactly does our company do? Do we manufacture the product ourselves?
Yes, We manufacture and market deodorants ourselves. Essentially, we control the supply chain.
What are the types of deodorants? What are the product categories that we are into?
There is just one kind of SKU. One size, one type consider it a homogeneous product
Is it a commoditized product or a very differentiated product? Is it a product in which the brand matters or is it just a product of convenience.
To simplify the case, you can consider it as just one type of deodorant. This is a product of convenience since people don't care about which
deodorant they are buying as long as it is a deodorant.
Okay and what is the current industry status and the competition status?
There are 4 other firms and the market is equally divided. So including us, there are 5 firms each having 20% market share.
Okay, so I will start with the profitability framework
Sure, go ahead
We mainly have 2 components of profit margin- revenue and cost. Since profitability was declining by 3.5%, the reason could be that the cost remains
the same, revenue decreases or if revenue remains the same, the cost increases or vice versa leading to a decrease in profits. Do we have a quantum
of each?
We will come back to it later, let's start with revenue for now
Okay, jumping into revenue:
Revenue = Price of one bottle X Number of bottles that you sell
So, if revenue has gone down it is one of two things i.e. either we are not able to charge the desired price or we can sell less number of bottles, basically
a volume issue.
In this case, the volume has declined. Can you think of possible reasons?
The reasons for the decline in volume can be:
There is enough demand however due to some supply-side issues in production or distribution we are not able to get our product to the right
people at the right time.
There is a demand issue, they are not demanding as much of our deodorant as they usually do.
Good, there is a demand-side issue so could you focus on that
Sure, under the demand side, there are two stakeholders. We sell to retailers and retailers sell to the end consumers. So the drop in demand can be
either from the retailer's side or the end consumer's side.
In this case, it is the end consumers that are the problem.
We can go to the end customers and figure out what essentially drives the decision and why we are facing this problem. Now, the customer journey
has a simple framework that revolves around the factors that determine that the end customer desires your product. These are:
1.
The customer should know about your product
2.
The customer should want/desire the product
3.
The customers should be able to buy the product
Awareness and accessibility i.e. the 'know and the can' bucket can be ignored here. Please focus on wanting the product or desirability of the
same.
What we can do here is to compare our company to its competitors. We make a type of table that includes our product, our competition's product
along with the factors that they are compared to. The factors are the product and things around the product. Under the product, we have features but
we know that the scent or fragrance is the same so we figure out what other features that matter. Things around the product including where the
product is stored, displayed, packaging and salespeople if there are any, etc
Let's first focus on the features of the product.
Okay, when you think of deodorant, the basic factors are fragrance and durability. However, we know that they have the same fragrance. So we
come to the durability that can be understood by how
1.
An individual bottle can have more sprays
2.
An individual spray can last longer
So, our bottles have become such that one spray is lasting longer and hence people are not buying as much, and hence the decrease in demand
since one bottle lasts longer.
But why are our sprays lasting longer?
This is basically because our RnD team had come up with a new type of ingredient that makes it last longer. This is patented and the patent is for
5 years. Anyway, we can now move on to the recommendation side. We know that this ingredient is causing our sprays to last longer and thus
explains the decline in profitability.
The 3 recommendations that I have are:
1.
We can aggressively market this feature and help it differentiate from our competitors and try to increase the market share since this is
increasing the desirability
2.
We can increase the price
3.
If this new feature is not cared about by the end customers, then we can roll back this RnD and always go back to our old formula
We can close the case now.

title: Book — Kearney
prompt: Your client is a legal publishing house, they publish law books, they want to enter the Indian market and they want to know how many law books will be sold in a year in India
solution:
C: Are we talking about law books that students read to become lawyers or judges or those used as guides in the courtrooms by lawyers and judges?
We are talking about both kinds.
So the formula I'll be using to calculate the total no of law books sold in India in a year will be-
Total books = the number of law books used by colleges + no of law books used by professionals practising law.
So to start we'll calculate the number of law books used by colleges. Here we should,
1.
First, begin by finding out the number of law colleges in India and then calculate the total number of law students in these colleges.
2.
Now we split these students on the basis of how many years of their degree they have completed.
3.
Once we have completed this we calculate the average number of books used by a student in each year. For example, a 3rd-year student uses 6
books while a 2nd-year student uses only 3 books.
4.
Now we multiply this average number by the number of students in each year respectively to find out the total number of books used in a year in
colleges.
Now we need to calculate the number of books used by practising court lawyers. We can do this by calculating the percentage of law students who
become lawyers and the number of books they use or we can look at the total number of courts in India and find the number of books used by each
type of court.
You can use the first approach.
Is it correct to assume that the number of cases a lawyer deals with in a year depends on their skill set?
Yes, it is like that.
C: We start with the percentage of law students with a license and we split them on the basis of their skills
1.
Low skilled lawyers
2.
Medium skilled lawyers
3.
High skilled lawyers
On the basis of this, we can assume the number of cases a lawyer will be handling in a year. Now we can also split the cases on the basis of difficulty
as cases with low difficulty will be requiring fewer no of books compared to a case that will be more difficult.
This will give us an estimate of the number of books lawyers will be using in India in a year.
Great, we can close the case now.

title: MISCELLANEOUS — NTT DATA
prompt: Your client has to explore new geographical locations. Your client has hired you to accomplish this task. What should they consider and what would your approach be?
solution:
C: Firstly, what Target Audience does the client want to hit? What is the geography he wants to expand his venture into? What is the political scenario
of the area he wishes to expand into? Are there any trade barriers or policies for international trade?
You have analyzed the information required for the case very well, but you have to give 3 to 5 general suggestions. Please elaborate upon things
that the client must keep in mind while expanding.
All right while considering expansion into a new market, there are several factors our client should carefully evaluate.
Competitive landscape:
We should analyse the competition in the market by conducting a thorough analysis of existing competitors, their market share, product offerings,
pricing strategies, distribution channels, and customer base.
Market attractiveness :
We should examine the demographics, consumer behaviour, cultural factors, and trends specific to the market our client wishes to expand into. This
would involve analysing the potential demand, market size, growth rate, and profitability of the target market.
Partnering or Mergers and Acquisitions:
We can consider partnering with or Merging or Acquiring local companies to facilitate market entry. We will have to identify reliable partners who
possess local market knowledge or established distribution networks which would expedite our client'
s entry into the new market. This would be
beneficial as partnerships would provide access to resources and shared risks that would enhance our client'
s competitive position.
The decision of either partnering or merging or acquiring should be taken after thorough research and analysis of the company and our client'
s
financial condition.
Political and legal conditions:
When expanding into new markets, the legal and political conditions of that country should also be considered. Analysing the political stability,
regulatory environment, licensing, labour laws, trade barriers and legal risks is to be done before entering the new market.
You have clearly explained what your client should pay attention to before entering a new market. Can you suggest a framework for analyzing this
situation?
Yes, we could have used PESTEL analysis as well.
Under the PESTEL analysis, we assess the external factors that can impact an organization. It examines the market under six significant factors:
Political
Economic
Social
Technological
Environmental
Legal
Great, you have covered all the requirements your client should consider. We can wind this case up now.

title: Transportation — Praxis Global
prompt: A logistics company is involved in the transportation of packages, like those from Amazon. They are our primary client who wants to transition into the electric fleet. Conduct a market entry analysis to determine whether they should go forward with this fleet. Start with determining the financial feasibility.
solution:
C: Under financial feasibility, I will do a cost-benefit analysis concerning the normal cost by comparing the cost involved in running a traditional fleet
with the cost incurred while running an electric fleet. But since they are transitioning into an electric fleet, we will also consider the initial cost of
actually buying the vehicles. But on the other hand, once we abandon the traditional fleet we can scrap it too.
What about the operational feasibility?
In terms of driving, there is not much difference between the two but the source of power. Earlier it was petrol or combustible fuels whereas now it will
be electricity. With regards to electricity, there will be batteries and corresponding issues such as degradation over time, faulty batteries exploding,
batteries having a particular range and unlike traditional transporting vehicles, you cannot refuel them immediately.
Alright. Now conduct a Risk Factor Analysis and tell me how they should go forward in this field.
Coming to the risk factor and pestle analysis, only the E, T and L parts of the PESTLE analysis are involved. PESTLE stands for political, economic,
sociological, technological, legal, and environmental.
Under the economic part (E) there was a threat of new entrants as many more logistic companies will be trying to shift into the electric fleet. Also,
those pre-existing with the traditional fleet will of course act as our competitors.
Coming to the technological part (T), there will be risks such as the battery degrading over time as well as faulty batteries exploding etc.
And considering the legal aspects (L), we would have to register the new vehicles under new registrations rather than the pre-existing vehicle
registrations.
The Political, Sociological and Environmental aspects of the PESTLE analysis, though less relevant, could also be discussed in brief. Talking about
the political (P) aspect, we could leverage subsidies and tax holidays from government plans like the FAME (faster adoption and manufacturing
of electric vehicles) policy and the Switch Delhi policy. To consider the sociological (S) part, marketing strategies could raise awareness about
health consciousness and lifestyle attitudes. The lower AQI generation might also minimize the state-wise carbon taxes. Finally, in the
environmental (E) part, government policies favouring a cleaner environment could be used for lowering input costs.
Okay, we can close the case now.

title: Hotel — Praxis Global
prompt: European Aggregator-based platform wants to venture into the US market. You have to analyze the feasibility of the venture.
solution:
C: What is the objective of the client? What services do they provide?
The client wants to expand their business. They provide ranking to hotels on platforms like booking.com, and Trip Advisor, and also set prices
and display them to the customers.
What is the customer base and geography?
The customer base is hotel chains and the client is operating in pan-Europe and wants to venture into the pan-US market
Let us consider three aspects:
1.
Market Attractiveness-How attractive the US `
market is. First, we will look at the market size i.e. how many hotels are actually there. We will also
look at the market share which will be captured upon entering the market. It depends on aspects like market size, market share, and industry
growth in the near future which will give the expected profitability.
We will focus on the expected profitability = market size*market share*expected profit
2. Operational Feasibility: On entering the country, how will they set up their operations such as the licenses to enter the country, the requisite stuff,
platform or website for after-sales services and customer support? We also have to focus on the GTM strategy i.e.how they will penetrate into
the market, what would be their place, price, promotion strategies, and what kind of product specifications they can launch in the given market.
3. Associated Risks Of Entering The US Market: Political risks, instability, legal barriers, competitors, substitutes.
I want you to delve only into the first aspect and calculate the market size.
Okay, we will proceed as follows:
No of hotels(market size)= Total no of customers using 4-star, 5-star hotels/no. of customers per hotel
We have two aspects here:
1. To obtain the total no. of customers we have to divide the population on income basis and age group basis.
INCOME BASIS: The population is divided into Below the poverty line, lower middle class, upper middle class, and upper class on the basis of income.
We will only consider the last two groups as the first two cannot afford them.
AGE GROUP BASIS: People who are 60 years or above would prefer travelling less as compared to people in the age group of (18-40). Our primary
focus, therefore, is on the working population.
This way we obtain the target population i.e. the customer base. Also, We can take 5 per cent of it for international travellers who prefer to stay in
four-star, five-star hotels.
2. For obtaining customers per hotel we will multiply the maximum capacity of a hotel by the occupancy rate on a particular day.
Now, occupancy rates can be high, medium, or low like 80%,60%, or 40%. Take the weighted average of all such rates and obtain the required
occupancy rate.
This is how we obtain the market size.
Okay, we can close the case now.

title: Automobile — Bain & Company
prompt: An automotive company currently manufactures only petrol cars. There are 4-5 players in the market with it being the largest one. With the shift in technology to electric vehicles, predict when the market would become electric to ensure that the company enters the EV space at the right time. What are some factors that would make you predict this?
solution:
C: I would like to begin by asking some preliminary questions.
Sure, go ahead.
What is the time frame we are taking into consideration here?
5-10 years would be ideal.
Are we focusing on retail or commercial customers?
We are focusing on retail customers. I would like to know the factors that affect the shift of the market from petrol vehicles to electric ones.
So three major factors could cause this shift and they are:
Demand - The demand for EVs should be high
Infrastructure - Charging stations etc, the infrastructure has to be conducive enough for widespread EV adoption
Supply - enough manufacturers in the market
Infrastructure and Supply can be handled by us, tell us more about the demand aspect of it.
Let's look at a few macro-level parameters to analyze the situation. I will consider 6 buckets:
The political factors would be the political signalling of the government towards renewable energy adoption, related policies, and proposals for
transition.
The environmental factors would cover how environmental regulations will pan out over the next few years.
The social aspect should be based on when people realize the need for renewable energy.
Better technology for EVs is also an important factor.
The legal factor affecting the demand here would be based on how easy it is for EV companies to enter the market.
The economic impacts would focus on the affordability and accessibility of EVs
Okay so out of all these factors which one do you think is the most dominating?
According to me, affordability will be the most dominant factor here.
How would affordability affect demand here?
Three things can play out -
1.
Petrol cars have become more expensive
2.
Petrol prices rise or electricity prices decrease or both
3.
Incomes rise
This sounds fine but I want you to pay more attention to micro-level factors. What parameters would an individual take into consideration while
buying an EV? 118
So the factors that consumers may focus on while buying a car would be the needs they have for a car and what product features they are looking
for. Customer needs can be broken down into mobility needs, fuel needs, and maintenance There's also a prestige and experiential element in cars
apart from driving convenience.
Can you elaborate on these?
Sure. Starting with fuel, the customer will see the mileage, recharge timeline for EVs, and alternative options in case the car runs out of battery.
For maintenance, the customer will see the cost, availability, and frequency of care required. The prestige element will mostly depend on the brand
value.
Okay, let'
s only focus on the cost to the consumer. Assume that the petrol car is cheaper to buy but costlier to run compared to an EV. The cars are
primarily similar in all the other aspects so affordability is the only factor influencing their decision. How would you go about it now?
Here, the consumer will have to conduct a break-even analysis. This can be done by comparing the initial prices and operational costs of a petrol car
and an EV. This will help him calculate how long it will take for the consumer to recover his extra initial investment.
Assume the price of a petrol car to be 7 lakhs and the price of an EV to be 10 lakhs. The difference between their operational costs is 60000 every
year where EVs have lower operational costs. What will the consumer do?
It would take the consumer 5 years to recover the initial difference and thus break even. If the consumer plans to keep his car for more than 5 years
he will go ahead with an EV otherwise the petrol car would be more suitable for him
That is it for the case. Thank you.

title: Cosmetics — Praxis Global
prompt: A Cosmetic company has to launch an organic soap in India. They are an environmentally and labour-friendly company. Given the case, what is required to be done?
solution:
C: Can I ask some preliminary questions?
Yes sure, go ahead.
What is the Competitive Landscape?
There are four companies in the vicinity and one among them is regional and the other three are major international companies in the market.
What is the market price of organic soaps as compared to normal cosmetic soaps?
Higher Prices as compared to Normal Cosmetic Soaps. The company is itself a major international brand and is functional in many regions
globally.
What is the Overall Valuation?
The company has not installed its stores anywhere and usually sells through retail.
What is the significant objective for giving this company a shape and existence in India?
To get an appreciable exponential increase in the market share of the company. Can you define the financial feasibility and operational
feasibility descriptively and crucially?
No of units sold= Market size * Market share.
For example,
No of people purchasing soaps =10
Market share=⅕
Then, Units Sold=⅕*10=2 units.
For profitability,
Profit per unit= Price-Variable Cost and
Financial Feasibility =Profit per unit- Fixed Costs.
Okay, Now explain the operational feasibility
Operational feasibility is the capacity to operate and function in the market of a particular region. For Example, if a company has to target India as its
potential market, then the company usually checks whether they have the capacity for labour, for setting up offices and manufacturing etc.
What would be the Guesstimate for the market share?
Let us consider the Indian Population and divide it into 70% Rural and 30% Urban. We are neglecting the rural population, as the product is high priced
and not much known to people.
Now, the household size for Urban Population is obtained by dividing it by 4 and proceeding with the income splitting to get 30% BPL, 40% Middle
Class and 30% Richer Class and again neglecting the BPL class for obvious reasons.
Okay. Determine the same for the rest of the sections as well.
For the middle class, a family of 4, on average consumes 3 soaps in a month, and the same family but from the richer segment would consume 4
soaps of the company. This results in the number of soaps used and not the number of organic soaps used.
In middle-class families, preference would be more towards normal soaps. Suppose, in a group of 10 people, only 1 would be willing to purchase the
organic soap, and thus market size would turn out to be 1/10th of the assumed population.
How are you going to find the market share for our company?
We'll analyze the growth and look at the last entrant among all the four competitors in the market. This helps us to conclude that if all the rest are
bigger companies and have 20% market share, then we can acquire about 15-20% as we are a bigger brand. Then, consider the price and reduce it
by the variable cost and multiply it to get the total market size.
Okay, we can close the case now.

title: EV — BCG
prompt: The client is an electric scooter manufacturing company which is the biggest in India. They want to enter the new market, the 3 options are Eu, Latin America.
solution:
C: Before I begin with the solution can I ask some preliminary questions?
Yes, sure.
What is their primary objective, and why are they looking to enter into a new market? Electric scooters are new, and the market could be better in
India.
They want to diversify markets. The product is a niche in many categories but not in anyone's main category.
Are there any cost constraints?
No.
What are other electric scooters existing in the market apart from ours?
There are other scooters which are cheaper but ours are still the best.
What are the other parameters of the product?
Everything that the customer cares about is performance.
Who are the customers that are buying the scooters?
Mainly people who are trying new products.
What if we compare it to the Chinese product? Should we look at the similarities to the Indian market or some other factors?
First, you can go ahead with the similarities factor.
We could look at the similarity in terms of economic growth status, road condition and production condition. The EU has a high-income market and
has a lot of regulations. The demand conditions will not be the same there and the EU is also geographically far away from India.
Okay, go on.
Latin America is also very far away but in terms of economic development, production capacity and government regulations it is similar to India. It Is
close to India but not similar in terms of customer demographics and income. However, the customer demographics and income levels in Latin
America are different from India, and the demand for electric scooters might not be the same.
In terms of similarity to the Indian market, which one would you recommend?
I would recommend Latin America as geography is the only concern and the rest of everything is quite similar.
Ok, In Latin America if we consider Brazil then what would you say?
Then we look at certain parameters of Brazil which include existing opportunity, market share, growth rate, the financial viability of the operation,
operational capacities and risks
What will you look into for risks?
We would need to consider both internal and external risks. Internal risks could include factors such as production costs, marketing expenses, and
operational efficiency, while external risks could include factors such as government policies, economic stability, and competition.
Do you have any other recommendations or insights?
Yes, I would suggest that the client conducts thorough market research to understand the customer demographics, preferences, and buying
behaviours in Brazil. This would help the client tailor their marketing strategy to suit the local market and increase their chances of success.
Great job. This is it for the case. Thank you for your time and effort.
Brownie Points-
Ask the right type of questions and get a better idea of the product.

title: FMCG — BCG
prompt: The client is Reliance Limited and is thinking of venturing into the field of grocery supplies. How should it plan its procedures to achieve its aim?
solution:
C: Can I start with some preliminary questions?
Sure, go ahead.
May I know what is the objective behind entering into this particular segment and not any other sector?
The objective is to maximize profits.
What time frame is the client looking at for establishing its operations? Are there any budgetary constraints at their entry?
The client has decided on a time period of two years. There are no budgetary constraints.
Are there any well-established and fully consolidated competitors in the market?
Yes, there are a few competitors but we think if Reliance enters the market they would be able to capture a good deal of market share. You can
proceed with the structure.
Okay. So we can bring three factors into the light, namely attractiveness of the market, operational feasibility and the risk associated with entering
the market. Attractiveness includes within its purview- the market size, market share & expected profitability that can be achieved.
Don't focus on attractiveness, rather emphasize the aspect of operational feasibility and lay out the value chain.
The process of setting up a value chain will begin with the establishment of a dedicated platform following which we will work towards the
identification and acquisition of potential suppliers and delivery partners. This will ultimately help in the wide-scale distribution along with providing
the apparatus necessary for providing last-mile delivery to the customers.
What are the benefits that can be given to the suppliers so as to make them form connections with the client?
Two categories fall under the structure of benefits: Monetary and Non- monetary benefits.
Monetary benefits would include the commissions, incentives and margins that will be provided to the suppliers for their service.
Non-monetary benefits relate to providing the suppliers with discounts in subscription/enrolment fees, helping them with their publicity
campaigns along with other benefits like loyalties etc.
How would you proceed to acquire new customers?
That depends, are we targeting both urban and rural areas?
What do you suggest regarding this?
I think Urban areas would be better to target since the people there would be having the awareness and willingness to make use of these methods.
Moreover, the availability of high-speed internet and smartphones would also help in integrating them into the customer base. However, we will have
to delve deeper into the market size of urban areas to do a cost-benefit analysis so as to come up with a perfect conclusion on whether to go ahead
with this approach.
No, there is no need for that. How would you acquire new urban customers?
The following five factors will play an important role in this:-
Firstly, awareness which includes promotional acts like ads by influencers, campaigns in famous places, and publicity in Reliance supermarts to use
platforms, billboards, and print media.
The second aspect is affordability which includes 2 aspects within its scope.
The first one relates to selling the product initially at a low price even if it leads to minor losses in the short run as it will help in capturing the
market. order to capture the market ie.
The second aspect includes giving discounts, offers and referral programs along with reducing or waiving the delivery fee, only if permitted by
budget.
Thirdly, accessibility deals with establishing last-mile delivery operations with the help of the establishment of local warehouses. Advertisement of
special features such as a10 minute delivery option can help in attracting customers.
Availability constitutes our fourth aspect and concerns ensuring that enough stock is available to meet the needs of customers in peak hours. This
can be achieved by analyzing consumer demand patterns and accordingly filling in the inventory.
Last, efforts should be made to provide the customer with an enriching and user-friendly experience. This entails reducing the otp reception time,
Making attractive categories for easy navigation, Providing case-insensitive features for applying promo codes and ensuring good customer support
with the help of 24/7 call centres and chatbots.
Great, that is all for the case.
Brownie Points-
In case of familiar clients, one should refrain from asking questions relating to what it does and what kind of products it offers.
Questions about time frame and budget are important because they are crucial to understand whether inorganic methods of growth can be
deployed such as entering into a merger or acquisition.

title: Automobile — Kearney
prompt: Kia Motors wants to enter the Indian market space. Their sister company Hyundai Motors is already present in India while their opposition, Maruti Cars is the current market leader. So, the case questions are, Should they enter the Indian market? If yes, then what should be their target segment? What is the market size of passenger vehicles available in India for them? What is the market share they can expect in India in the forthcoming 3-5 years?
solution:
C: Is the objective to have a greater market share or greater profitability?
In this case, it will be both.
Are there any budgetary constraints?
No, there are no budgetary constraints.
And how precise do you want me to be with my approach?
You just need to explain the approach and not come up with actual numbers.
I will divide the population of India into rural and urban segments. Since Kia Motors would be producing luxury products, I assume that rural people
largely won't be able to afford it, so it will have a majorly urban market.
For the purpose of simplification, can I ignore the rural market?
I would discourage that line of thinking.
Ok, understood. Then the required formula would be,
Number of households=urban population / average household size
Then I will divide the households into income categories such as Below Poverty Line (BPL), low-income, middle-income, and upper-income
categories. And since Kia Motors is a luxury car brand, I would be ignoring the BPL and low-income categories. I will assume that the number of
people who would be using a 4-wheeler is in the middle and upper-income categories. After doing so I will calculate further statistics using the
following formulae
Total cars=Number of eligible households frequency of cars
Number of cars demanded per year = total cars / average lifespan of a car
Assuming the average lifespan is constant, the number of cars demanded increases if eligible households increase (assuming frequency is also
constant).
So, we can think of this as
A=(1+r)p
where,
p= Principal/ Number of cars
r= interest rate/growth rate of population
A= Amount/ Total amount of cars demanded in a year in urban areas
We can further Implement this same approach to calculate the number of cars demanded for the rural areas and then sum up the two components
which will give the number of cars demanded per year in India. Upon multiplying by market share, it will give the market size of passenger vehicles for
Kia Motors.
What are the parameters to look at, to understand the market share KIA Motors can capture in India?
The 4 parameters I have in mind are,
1.
Growth rate: To understand the current condition of the industry, and its growth statistics.
2.
Competitors: To understand how much market share the competitors have and how much Kia Motors can capture.
3.
Current synergy with Hyundai Motors: This is because current synergies with the sister company can help Kia to capture a better market share.
4.
Market Fragmentation
Other factors that can be considered to understand the market share are,
1.
Location: The location you enter the market from will be key in determining your total market share.
2.
USP/ Product Differentiation: How your product is different from other competitors also plays a huge role in how Kia Motors can enter the market
and capture a large market share.
That is it for the case. Thank you.

title: Government of India — McKinsey
prompt: Your client is the government of India, the Ministry of Transport and they are planning to launch green fuel buses. Should they do that? If so, then how?
solution:
C: Can I start with some preliminary questions?
Sure, go ahead
Why do we want to launch these buses? Do we have a particular objective in mind?
What do you think would be the objective to launch green fuel buses?
According to me, the primary reason would be to reduce emissions and make it more environment friendly and the second would be reducing the
cost of operations and making it more efficient. If it somehow improves customer experience that could also be a reason.
Good, so we are looking at two of them i.e cost of operation and air pollution, we want both of them to go down. We are looking to introduce these
buses phase-wise across India.
Okay, should I start with a particular city or zone?
In the first phase, we are looking to introduce them in Delhi and that is the decision that we are processing right now.
Okay, so what are these green fuel buses exactly?
CNG buses are just like CNG autos.
Do we have the power to change these buses because usually these buses are run by state transport corporations?
Good question. We can do that.
We have three things to look at before making our decisions which are:
1.
If this makes sense financially i.e it reduces the cost of operations and helps us achieve our objective there
2.
If it helps us reduce air pollution and helps us meet our air pollution targets
3.
We'll also have to look at it from an operational point of view. Is it possible to run these buses?
Let's begin by looking at the financial side
Sure, now I can divide financials into two parts - the operating and the capital part.
Under the capital part, we have,
the cost of getting new buses and the gains which mean the existing buses which you will not throw but use alternatively.
Under operating,
We compare the old and new bus structures and figure out if there is an appropriate reduction.
Good, let's start with the capital side. What will be the number of buses that we are looking to convert? You don't need to give me a number, just
the approach and structure will work
The number of buses in Delhi = Number of bus trips demanded daily / Number of trips an individual bus can cater to daily
How will you estimate the number of bus trips demanded daily?
So I will take the total population of Delhi and apply the relevant filters to figure out how many people are taking the buses and how many trips are
taking place in a day.
Income: We'll have to exclude the upper-income class because they don't prefer buses
Age: Majorly students and the working class would be taking buses
Frequency of travel: Some people will take multiple trips in a day and some people will take a trip every few days in a month.
Alternative modes of travel: We'll have to factor in metro trips, auto trips, etc. which are offered at a competitive cost.
Understood, How will you compute the number of trips an individual bus can cater to?
Number of trips an individual bus can cater to daily = Capacity of the bus X Average duration of one trip X Operating time of the bus
The capacity of the bus = Capacity in peak hours * Trips demanded in one hour * No. of peak hours + Capacity in non-peak hours* Trips demanded
in one hour * No. of non-peak hours
Hence, for example, if a bus is operating for 10 hours in a day and it has 100 seats, each trip lasts for 30 minutes. So if a bus was operating at 100%
capacity throughout the day, then in one hour there are 2 batches so 200 in an hour and 2000 in a day.
Okay great, Let's move on to the operating costs.
Under the operating costs, we have two things that are the old cost structure and the new cost structure under these CNG buses
What are the operating costs under both cost structures?
Operating cost= Cost of the fuel + Maintenance costs + Wages paid to the drivers + Parking costs of the buses
Good, now coming to the old cost structure 60% of our operating costs are fuel, 10% are drivers, 25% are maintenance and 5% are parking costs.
Under the new cost structure, the fuel costs become half of what they were, the driver costs and the parking costs remain the same but the
maintenance costs become 35% instead of 25%.
Have the operating costs gone down?
Just for clarity, could you please confirm that this means that fuel was x amount of rupees, and now it is half of that?
Yes, basically it is 30 now instead of 60
Thank you, let's take our old operating cost to be 100, and using the above-given information we see that under the new cost structure:
New Operating Costs= X
Fuel Costs = 30
Driver's Cost = 10,
Parking cost = 5.
Maintenance Costs= 35% of X
So, 65% of X = 45
X= 69.77
Hence, we see that the old operating costs were 100 and the new operating costs are 69.77 which means that there is almost a 30% reduction in costs
Thus, we can say that financially, we are happy with both the capital and operating costs
What can you say about the goal of emission reduction?
One way is to compare the old cost structure with the new cost structure and check if the reduction is good enough.
Total Emissions= No. of buses * Emissions per bus
Is there something else we could consider?
Yes, an additional factor could be the reduction in external emissions. It may be possible that in the production of diesel, emissions are higher than in
the production of CNG. Now because we are making the switch to CNG, we are reducing the demand for diesel and thus doing away with these
emissions.
Yes, this seems fine. Please look at the operational side now.
Okay under the operational side, I'll look at the value chain that is manufacturing, distribution, research, and development, etc., and the
manufacturing costs. There will also be operational requirements, transportation requirements, customer requirements, and finally maintenance.
Okay, could you elaborate more about the operational requirements?
Sure, under the operational side:
1.
If the CNG buses have less range than the diesel buses then the buses won't be able to traverse the same route as before. They won't reach from
one bus stop to another without having to refuel a lot in between and thus it won't make sense. So, how the distance traveled by each bus
changes after the switch to CNG is a problem.
2.
We would also have to consider the cost incurred if the drivers need some additional training.
3.
Since we are changing the fuel type it is important to note that there should be enough fuel available at enough places. Hence, fuel requirements
are a major concern
Good, could you briefly explain talk about maintenance?
Under maintenance, we would have to see if there is any need to set up alternate maintenance.
Okay great, according to you what are some other barriers to market entry?
Some other barriers to entry would be:
1.
Legal in case there is some law/regulation or trade unions objecting to this change.
2.
Physical barriers if the new buses are larger, they cannot fit in the old place or on the road. This is unlikely but possible.
3.
Technological barriers would be difficulty in implementing or creating the technology.
The risks involve considering the important stakeholders like the drivers, customers, and government officials.
Okay, lastly let's talk about the customer risk.
Customer risk includes the difference in customer experience and since this is a new product, they may be hesitant and may not want to use it which
can be a problem for us.
Great, thank you. I think that's enough.
Feedback:
1.
The overall structure was okay.
2.
While clarifying the objective, clarify the metric for example by how much you want the emissions to be reduced.
3.
Make sure that you have the correct idea about the desired time frame because that gives an idea about the feasibility.

title: Beverage — BCG
prompt: Company X distributes brands of liquor to all outlets like restaurants, bars, retail shops etc. There are 200 outlets you supply to and they are controlled by 2 cartels. Each cartel controls 100 outlets. At the first of every month, you are presented with a proposal from the heads of both the cartels. They will tell you the number of cases they want to buy and their respective commissions. The commission will be applied per case. The constraint on you is that you can only pay out 10,00,000 as commissions and you can only sell to one cartel. How will you choose one? As a sales manager, delve deeper into the situation and build a framework regarding the same.
solution:
C: Okay, so from what I have understood, the framework will be such that-
1.
It should maximize the no. of cases you buy
2.
It must minimize the commission you pay out
Yes, let's say there are 4 brands of liquor that you are selling.
Brands Quantity (per outlet) Commission charged (per outlet) Quantity (per outlet) Commission charged (per outlet) Prices
B1 500 200 800 200 1000
B2 375 134 1000 490 2000
B3 150 1000 700 300 2500
B4 50 1995 60 4000 7500
Firstly, we will calculate sales from Cartel A and Cartel B followed by the calculation of commission and profit.
Sales from Cartel A = 500*1000+375*2000+150*2500+50*7500 = 20,00,000
Sales from Cartel B = 800*1000+1000*2000+700*2500+60*7500 = 50,00,000.
Commission for Cartel A = 500*200+375*134+150*1000+50*1995 = 4,00,000
Commission for Cartel B = 800*200+1000*490+700*300+60*4000 = 11,00,000
Profits from Cartel A = 20,00,000-4,00,000 = 16,00,000
Profits from Cartel B = 50,00,000-11,00,000 = 39,00,000
Okay, then according to you what would be a better option for Company X - Cartel A or Cartel B?
The sales from B are significantly higher than that of A but the commission is a little higher than 10,00,000 which is the constraint given to us. In this
case, if we just fulfill 90% of their order, then the required condition will be met.
Sales of Cartel B = 720*1000+900*2000+630*2500+54*7500 = 45,00,000
Commission for Cartel B = 720*200+900*490+630*300+54*4000 = 9,90,000
Profit from Cartel B = 45,00,000-9,90,000 = 35,10,000
Thus, in my opinion, Cartel B is a better option.

title: Plant Based Meat — BCN
prompt: Your client is an International plant-based meat company. They want to enter the Indian Market. How do they go about it?
solution:
C: That'
s interesting. I would like to get a better understanding of the problem. Where is the client currently based? What'
s the product line? What
customers do they cater to?
The client is based out of the US. Currently, they have a robust product and supply chain and focus on a single product which is regular plant-
based meat. They cater to high-end and health-conscious customers.
That'
s great. When are they planning to enter? Do they have a budget in mind?
They are planning to launch in Delhi in the next 1 year. There is no budget.
Alright. We can analyze the situation in three parts.
1. Market Attractiveness
2. Operational Feasibility
3. Risks involved
Makes sense
To begin with, let's look at the target market size. Out of the 2 Cr people residing in Delhi, the demand for plant-based meat comes primarily from the
high-end and upper-middle class since the product is priced at a premium. Also, a certain amount of awareness is required for this product, so
target consumers can be further narrowed down using preference filters such as health and environmental consciousness.
Therefore,
Target households = Total population of Delhi x Urban Population x Upper and Upper Middle-Class x Health and Environment Conscious consumers
/Numbers of members per family
={(2 Cr) x (50%) x (20%) x (5%)} / 4
= 1,00,000/4
=25,000 Households
Can you further segment these households based on certain factors?
Sure, these households can be further categorized based on age and education. Since millennials, young and educated people are more attracted
to the specified product. We can assume that 20% of the identified households will have a higher propensity to consume. Here, I would like to know
the price per kg of plant-based meat.
Sure, the price of a packet is ₹1000 per kg.
Great. Then we can calculate the weighted average consumption and revenue generated as follows:
Consumption Pattern % Distribution, Packets consumed per month
High 20%, 6
Medium 50%, 4
Low 30%, 2
Weighted Average 3.8
Total market size = 3.8 x 25,000 x 12 X 1000
= ₹1,14,00,00,000
We have a well-established name in the international market. However, there are a few local competitors. So, assume that we will be able to
capture 20% of the market
Attainable market = ₹1,14,00,00,000x 20% = ₹22,80,00,000
Is this figure good enough?
How would you decide if it is?
I would deduct the cost from the total revenue, to calculate the profit and compare it with our existing profits and see if it causes a sizable increase.
Great. Based on these metrics, you can assume that the profits are lucrative.
Alright. Next, I'd like to analyze how we will set up the value chain. For this, I would be listing down the chain of steps that we would have to go through
to start operations:
Statutory Compliances- The laws of the land that need to comply with the required food licenses.
Setting up offices - The decisions to set up headquarters, regional offices, and warehouses at different locations
Production decision - Whether to produce within India, import from our foreign factory, or give out production contracts
Hiring Personnel- Hiring high-quality staff in different managerial and operational domains
Distribution - Develop channels to supply our products to retail and online markets. We will have to tie up with delivery startups to ensure timely
and smooth deliveries.
Marketing - Improving customer awareness and generating social media presence. Collaborate with lifestyle influencers and food bloggers. We
can also partner with high-end retail stores
Let'
s assume that all these decisions can be made by our client
Brilliant. In that case, the proposition is operationally feasible as well. Now, we can look at the risks
Yes, go ahead.
From an entry standpoint, our client could encounter the following risks:
Customer risks - Consumers might be apprehensive about shifting their eating habits. Customer fatigue could be a potential risk. Consumers
might initially hop on the bandwagon but eventually lose interest in the product.
Competitor Risk - Risks entailing improved competitor goods, reduced competitor pricing, more aggressive marketing by competitors, or threat
of new entrants
Value Chain Risks - As our product is perishable, we will have to ensure proper warehousing and transportation.
Interesting, can you mention any strategies to eliminate competition?
Sure, we can adapt and modify our product. Product localization as per the taste and preferences of an Indian consumer can help us gain a larger
market share.
Amazing. Seems like you've covered it all.

title: Automotive — Kearney
prompt: C: What is the objective of this? Is it to maximize profit, increase revenue or increase their market share?
solution:
They are looking to have a profitable business in the first few years and a sizable market share but no target has been set up for the revenue.
What is the product portfolio? Are they looking to launch the same type of cars that they manufacture in India?
They might be open to a few changes as and when they are required but for now, they are planning to go ahead with the same models.
For us to pinpoint a country for our client, first we have to look for a country that has the most financially attractive market for the company to achieve
their objectives. Next, we should look for markets where it is feasible for the company to set up its business. The last step would be to look for barriers
that the company might face. So in order to search for the best market we should divide the geographical territory on the basis of continents. So we
have 5 different markets and to calculate if we'll have a good market share and profit over there we have to calculate the market size and rank these
continents subsequently. The market size will be calculated here by the given formula -
Total population x Percentage of population that can drive a vehicle x Percentage of population that owns cars x Percentage of the population that
can afford a car.
Let'
s narrow the markets down to the Southeast Asian market, the Western European market, and the African market.
So on the basis of market size the ranking would be
1.
Southeast Asia,
2.
African market (lower population that can afford a car) and the last would be the
3.
Western European market.
Now we look at the market where we can attain the highest market share and the rankings for this will be the African market as they are still
developing, the Southeast Asia market already has a lot of competitors and the last would be the Western Europe as they already have a lot of
competitors who use very efficient technology.
So the next factor we'll be looking at is operational feasibility and for this, we will look at the value chain that will be required to develop goods in
these foreign markets and the steps here could be done either directly by the company or they could be outsourced. The value chain here would be
1.
To get the required permissions and licenses,
2.
The research and development part which includes finding out the hotspots where we can place our products,
3.
The third step is manufacturing the cars,
4.
The fourth step is setting up offices in the market,
5.
The fifth step is hiring the executive staff,
6.
Generating the demand and,
7.
The last step is after-sales service.
How do you think that the manufacturing of cars can take place?
The firm can either open a production facility itself or they can merge with another car company and form a joint venture so that carrying out the
production becomes an easy task.
Do you think that you are missing something when it comes to the manufacturing of the cars?
Is it possible for the firm to export the cars instead of setting up a facility in the country itself?
Yes. So now, we have the value chain and we need to figure out if we should set up a production facility there or export these cars. What all things
would you be looking at to evaluate what the client should do?
For manufacturing cars in the market, first, we will have to get permission from the government to acquire either land or an existing factory where we
can produce the cars, next, we would also have to hire competent staff and buy machinery for the factory and the last step would be to look for
storage and transport facilities. This also associates a huge fixed cost for the client initially.
Now for exporting cars to the market first the company would have to get a licence from both the Indian and the foreign government, pay an export
fee if there is any, look for an airline company that we might want to partner up with, look for storage facilities in the foreign market and the last one
would be to look for a third person who will carry out the operation on behalf of the client.
What is the most important thing that you feel the client should look at while making the choice between exporting and manufacturing?
I think labor plays a major role here as they are involved in the production process as well as the value chain.
How will you rank the three markets discussed earlier on the basis of labor?
I think labor should be very competent and if the market does not provide us with skilful labor, we would rank it lower.
Yes, but maybe you are missing out on something.
We also need to consider the wages of the labor.
Correct.
So the market's supply of labor and their wages would help us rank the three markets. So first would be Western Europe, then the Southeast Asian
market, and Africa would be the last one as even though the wage rate over there would be low the firm would have to incur a lot of cost for training
the employees.
Great, we can end the case now.
Extra notes -
Instead of going country-wise to look for the best country, you should first identify on the basis of the best continent as this will narrow down the
number of countries you will have to look at.
Keep in mind the wage rates of the labor and exporting of cars.

title: Tools — BCG
prompt: Your client is a global tools manufacturer who entered the Indian market three years back. Wherever they go, their share remains in the top 3% but in India, they have just 1.5% market share. They have orders from global offices that will either improve it within 18 months or shut it down in India.
solution:
C: Before I begin with the solution can I ask some preliminary questions?
Yes, sure.
What are the types of tools the company deals in?
They primarily deal in tools like hammers, screwdrivers etc.
Is data for the competitors available?
Yes. The top player has a 35% market share, the second has 15% and the 3rd one has 10%. These top 3 players are domestic homegrown brands
and are strong.
Okay so as it is easier to capture market share in a growing market than in a stagnant market we can now talk about organic and inorganic
methods to grow our product.
Let's look at the organic methods first. Go ahead with working on the product.
We can first look at improvements for the existing product or develop new products altogether.
Okay now explain how we can grow our existing products. 150
So for existing products, we can look at the customer segments like the ones we want to target. We need to determine what their needs are and what
the market is delivering. If there is some difference here try to come up with a solution that is reflected in our products. Also, we can look at the
quantity and the price. This is the organic side.
What strategy do you recommend that will help the firm in realising its goal?
I think for this we can consider acquiring some of the companies present in the market.
Okay. So will you recommend acquiring one of the big 3 companies in the market or some of the remaining small companies to improve your
targets?
There are pros and cons associated with both cases but we can narrow it down. Now If we go for smaller ones, the major con is that we will have to
do the process again and again, and then there may be integration problems as well for the 4-5 companies. Pros would be that at the same time,
we can perform the due diligence with ten at a time.
Okay but let'
s say you have acquired a bigger one for 10% now you have two options: either keep it as it is or give your name. What about that?
So for the big companies, we can initially take their name and gradually bring it under our name. A clash might still be there.
This is it for the case. Thank you.

title: FMCG — BCG
prompt: Your client is an FMCG manufacturer who wants to grow their revenue by 200%. Suggest a suitable course of action for the same.
solution:
C: Sure. With the objective of tripling the revenue, I would first like to understand our client better. What are its primary product offerings? By when
does the client want to reach the 200% growth mark?
We wish to reach the goal in the next 3 years. The company is a national brand, similar to Bajaj, with 3 major product offerings- fans, tubelights,
and geysers. However, for the sake of this exercise, let's focus on fans and geysers only.
Sure. Where is the manufacturing plant of the company situated?
It'
s in Delhi NCR.
Thank you! Revenue growth can be achieved either through: 1. Organic means or, 2. Inorganic means i.e. Joint Venture and Acquisitions.
You can limit your analysis to organic means of growth.
Sure, in terms of organic growth, Revenue = No. of customers X Revenue per customer. Which of the two should I focus on for growing our business?
Let's understand how we can increase the number of customers.
Under number of customers, we can look at:
1.
Growth via existing channels or;
2.
Look for new channels of growth
Let's see how we can grow through existing channels.
We can look at existing channels through three lenses:
1.
Work on the supply end i.e. increase capacity of production
2.
Increase the reach of our distribution channels or;
3.
Look at increasing the demand of our products by improving their quality
Is there anything specific I should focus on?
How do you plan to look at distribution?
For this, we consider distribution to be the product of the number of channels, the number of distributors per channel, and the number of orders per
distributor.
Okay, what about increasing the demand of our products in the market?
For increasing the demand, we can look at product, price, and marketing & positioning. Under product, we should focus on the design, build,
longevity, and energy efficiency of the fans and geysers. For price, we should see if our fans and geysers are competitively priced in the market. For
marketing & positioning, we should revisit our existing marketing strategies, and see if there are any gaps.
Good. For positioning & marketing, it'
s vital to segment our customers into categories. What do you think are the possible categories for our fans?
For our fans, the plausible customer segments can be-
1.
Households
2.
Schools
3.
Offices
4.
Factories
Okay, can you suggest some positioning and marketing strategies for the client?
Sure. As a brand, we should strive towards becoming a household name which can be done by turning customers into loyal purchasers. This can be
done by:
1.
Running loyalty programs: This will help us improve customer retention and give them enough incentives to repurchase and refer us in their
circles.
2.
Cross selling: This can be done both at our stores online and offline. Cross selling will help our customers feel understood and increase our
revenue.
3.
Providing exceptional after sale services: Including new services like online automated support resources and repair and upgrade offers in our
after sales service mix will help us generate customer satisfaction and loyalty.
Thanks. That'll be all.

title: Stationery — BCG
prompt: Your company is a laminate manufacturer and they want to increase their sales. Suggest ways to achieve this.
solution:
Before I begin with the case can I ask some questions?
Yes sure, go ahead.
Do they have a timeline for this increase in sales? Is there a target level as well?
They don't have a time limit but they want to increase sales by 20 percent.
Where are they located?
They are located all over India.
What is the product exactly?
It is a material pasted on doors, tables, etc and it is usually 0.8mm-1mm.
What about the competitors present in the industry?
It is a big industry and there are about 50-60 players in the market. The top three competitors in the industry are A, B, and C which have a 45%,
25%, and 20% market share respectively. The leftover market share is made up of unbranded laminate manufacturers.
Who are the customers?
The client has a B2B and B2C business model. They serve contractors and retail shop owners. These are the end consumers.
Okay, so the plan of action for an increase in sales is as follows, we split our approach into two segments -
Organic growth channels
Inorganic growth channels
Organic growth channels can be split into revenue per customer and total customers, and then revenue per customer can be divided into revenue
per unit per customer and average units sold per customer.
The number of customers can be increased through existing channels or new channels. Under new channels, we can explore newer geographies,
product mixes, or customer segments.
The inorganic growth channels to increase sales would be joint ventures or mergers and acquisitions (M&A).
We don't want to explore inorganic growth channels. Let's stick to organic growth only. Specifically, let'
s focus on using existing channels to
increase the number of customers.
Okay, so we start with the extraction of raw material, then we move ahead to the transportation of raw materials from suppliers to the production
centres. The third stage is processing i.e. production. The fourth stage is the distribution to businesses or customers; the last is generating customer
demand.
Let'
s look at the distribution aspect to increase the number of customers we have.
Okay, can I split the total distribution volume into the number of distributors and the amount sold per distributor?
Yes, you can.
Now we focus on these two aspects to increase our customers. We can focus on increasing the number of distributors, the amount sold to every
distributor, or both.
How will you determine if the amount sold per distributor should be increased? 156
Let's say that we go to different distributors and we find that they are getting stocked out pretty quickly or that there is a lot of wastage at their
distribution centre. In that case, we can increase the amount we sell to these distributors. we can even ask the customers if they feel that the
distribution centres run out of stock quickly.
Okay but how will you determine that you should increase the number of distributors?
So for this, we could look at the concentration of distributors. If we have the total area of a region and the number of distributors in that region, we
can see how many distributors there are per km^2.
There may be something else too that you might be missing.
Area covered by distributors, quantity sold per distributor per km^2.
Alright, give me a solution based on the region.
If the client gives information about the region it is covering and the pin codes there, we can see the number of distributors per pincode and likewise,
if we feel that a pin code has a huge population but the number of distributors is less, we can increase the number of distributors there.
Okay, continue.
My second solution is that we look at the pincode to pincode distance and figure out if there are specific isolated pin codes or ones that are not
served by distributors at all. We can introduce distributors in these areas.
That is all for the case. Thank you!

title: Sanitation — McKinsey
prompt: Your client is a third rank player in a sanitation service company. Your main objective here is to dig deeper and recommend ways of expanding
solution:
and growing the business.
Okay, how much do we want to grow in terms of sales/profits? Could you possibly give me a number for the same?
We would love to grow as much as we can while keeping in mind the time limit. A time limit, in this case, is 1-2 years to show returns to our
investors.
What specific products does the company sell?
The company deals in products like toilet seats, sanitary devices, etc.
What are the price points for the company?
Price points cater to middle class customers, we target them all over India. We have a processing unit in the middle of the country and all the
products are shipped across the country. Sales are carried out through small retailers.
Who exactly are the players in the market?
There are two international players in the market in terms of rankings. We also have a fragmented market after that, i.e. an unorganized market.
In this industry, patents are not very strong, so any technology can be copied easily. The international players come up with superior technology
and we just copy the same.
Okay, is there any other relevant information that you would like to give me?
Break the entire situation down in terms of price and quantity for your analysis.
We have Total Revenue = Average Price × No. of units sold, where the average price can be broken down into absolute prices. But, in my opinion, we
should avoid touching prices and rather focus on quantity. Quantity can be broken down into production, distribution, and then supply. First, let us
focus on supply. Is the current supply enough or should we produce more?
We are capable of producing more.
Yes, I think that approach should be followed. Secondly, as for the distribution aspect of it, what are the present distribution channels that the
company uses?
We have distributors for this purpose. Sales per distributor and no. of distributors both are problematic for us. At present, the no. of distributors is
large and the sales per distributor are too low compared to industry standards.
One possible reason for this could be the transportation costs which should be dealt with immediately.
Okay, what do you have to say about demand?
We can break down the demand into revenue per customer and no. of customers. Let us consider an example to analyze the situation.
If I have to buy a house, the situation can be broken down into 3 cases:
In the first case, I buy a toilet and after a period of time, I am purchasing a wash basin from the same company.
In the second case, we can consider the reconstruction market where people leave their houses and put them on lease.
The third case will be the actual repurchase where we will focus on repeat customers and customer retention.
Okay, give some recommendations for such cases.
The recommendations that I have are:
In the reconstruction market, we can form some set of systems through which we can get in touch with these reconstruction agencies. We can
help them by expanding horizontally.
Another recommendation is that we could make the consolidation of distributors strong by training them with the help of retailers.
Thank you, we can close the case now.

title: Global Foundation — McKinsey
prompt: Suppose your client is a global foundation. They want to improve the participation of women in the labor force by prioritizing a few sectors and analyzing areas of opportunity. The client wants McKinsey to figure out a blueprint for the same. The given statistics are - 1. Currently, the labor force participation of women in rural and urban areas is less than 20%. 2. Women under consideration for the programme are of ages 15-54 years.
solution:
C: What exactly do you mean by blueprint?
We are looking for 5-6 ideas that need to be focused on for maximum impact alongwith the kind of incentives that can be given to increase'women
s participation in labor.
First, I will divide the women population into rural and urban areas.
Within each, I wish to categorize the industries into skilled and unskilled. In rural areas, the major skilled labor industries would be handicrafts,
agricultural manufacturing, and manufacturing of basic goods, and the major unskilled labor industries would be contracted labor, agricultural
workers (tillers, sowers, etc), waste management, house-help, etc.
Can you elaborate more with regard to agriculture?
I will break the structure into two channels-
1.
How can we increase participation in existing opportunities?
2.
How can we create new opportunities?
As far as increasing participation goes, I would like to point out a few issues,
1.
Fragmented ownership of land which leads to low output
2.
Need for a law for equal wages for women
3.
Need for training and vocational programs for women
4.
Political Representation for women
We will skip the second part for now. Let us move onto the analysis for the urban sector.
In the urban sector, in general, we have more skill-based industries as compared to unskilled ones. The skilled industries are Healthcare,
manufacturing, professional services, government sector, NGOs, etc. The unskilled industries are contract Labor, house-help, agricultural workers, etc.
Could you tell us more about women's participation in the healthcare sector?
To understand why women don't get into the healthcare sector, I would like to analyze the life cycle of a typical healthcare employee. Through this
exercise, I can visualize three barriers to women's participation in the healthcare sector:
1.
Educational barrier - low literacy rates of women in the country
2.
Entry Barrier - number of available jobs and the number of jobs given to women. Nurses are majorly female but female doctors and support staff
are fewer in number.
3.
Job-related barriers - Work environment, travel safety, home commitments.
Elaborate more on the Education barrier and recommend what can be done.
Let's analyze the life cycle of a woman seeking education. There are issues in primary-level schooling.
In addition, women can be incentivized to take up medical courses after class 10th by revamping the course structure. Such courses, especially
biology, can be more interactive and engaging by emphasizing gaining practical experience. Further, the number of medical colleges in India is
abysmally low with women participation being even lower. The concept of quotas also discourages participation.
That is all for the case. Thank you!

title: Rugby — L.E.K. Consulting
prompt: There is a rugby league client who wants to expand into Latin America. Walk me through how you would help the client assess which country to enter and what factors they should consider.
solution:
C: Which countries in Latin America would you be considering?
We are focusing on Mexico, Brazil and Chile.
Sure, I would begin by gathering some information about each of the three countries they are considering: Mexico, Brazil, and Chile.
Okay, what other factors apart from this would you consider?
We would also need to consider several factors including the potential market size, the level of competition, the local infrastructure, and the cultural
fit.
Can you elaborate on how you would assess the potential market size?
Certainly. We would need to look at factors such as the population size and demographics, the current interest in rugby or similar sports, and the
disposable income of the population. We can also analyze the popularity of OTT platforms in each country to estimate the potential revenue from
subscriptions.
And how would you evaluate the level of competition in each country?
We would need to look at the existing sports leagues in each country and their popularity, as well as any existing rugby leagues or teams.
We can also assess the level of interest in rugby or similar sports and the potential for growth in the market.
What about local infrastructure?
We would need to evaluate the availability and quality of stadiums, transportation, and other facilities necessary for hosting live games and
supporting the league operations. We can also consider the level of government support and investment in sports infrastructure in each country.
And finally, how would you assess the cultural fit?
We would need to evaluate the cultural attitudes towards sports, particularly rugby or similar sports, in each country. We can also consider any
language or cultural barriers that may impact the league's operations and marketing efforts.
Great, and once we have gathered this information, how would we use it to determine which country to enter?
We can use a weighted scoring system to rank each country based on the factors we have evaluated. We can assign weights to each factor based
on the client'
s priorities, and then score each country on a scale of 1-10 for each factor. We can then calculate the overall score for each country and
use that to determine which one offers the best potential for the client.
And what pricing strategy would you recommend for the client in this new market?
I would recommend a cost-based pricing strategy, where we analyze the league's costs and then add a markup to ensure they are making a profit.
We can also analyze the pricing strategies of existing sports leagues in the market to ensure that our pricing is competitive and attractive to
potential customers.
Thank you, that will be all.

title: Aerospace — Bain
prompt: Your client is a heli-air service provider and the client wants us to come up with a viable price and service. What could be the price?
solution:
C: Could I ask some preliminary questions?
Yes, sure, go ahead.
What is this service exactly and where is it offered?
This is a form of inter-city transport between two points in Delhi. The service is offered at an airport (so basically you need to go to the airport to avail of
this service) and the total travel time is 30 minutes with a maximum capacity of four people.
To price this, I will take the cost-based approach.
Okay, you can go ahead.
The fixed costs I will include are any fixed investments which were incurred in this and try to understand with how many trips you wish to retrieve your fixed
investment. The variable costs that would be included are land, employee costs, aviation fuel costs, maintenance/purchase charges for the helicopter etc.
Could you provide some figures?
The various costs are as follows:
The rental cost of the particular helicopter = 16 lakhs
The cost of the airport storage facilities = 2 lakhs per month
The salaries for the employees = 4 lakhs per month
Other maintenance charges = 1 lakh.
You can assume the cost of one trip to be approximately Rs. 4500 if we factor in the cost of fuel and the average duration of a flight.
Understood, and how many days is the helicopter functioning for? What is the average number of trips per day?
We are functioning for almost 25 days in a month and taking 8 trips per day.
So all in all,
Total Fuel Costs in a Month = No. of trips in one day X Cost of one trip X No. of days
= 8 X 4500 X 25
= 9,00,000
Total Cost in a Month = Rental Cost + Cost of Airport Storage+ Salaries + Other maintenance charges + Fuel Costs in a month
= 16,00,000 + 2,00,000 + 4,00,0000 + 1,00,000 + 9,00,0000
= 32,00,000
Now, What is the capacity of the helicopter?
The capacity of the helicopter is around four people. However, practically speaking, you wouldn't have four people for inter-city travel for all 8 of
the trips daily. So, let's take the average for each trip as three people.
Okay, and how many people will we have in a month?
Almost 400 people in a month.
So, the Cost of each Person per trip = Total Costs / No. of People
= 32,00,000 / 400
