= 8000 167
So the pricing cannot be any less than 8,000
What do you think about the viability of the product?
Inter-city travel could easily be accomplished using a cab. While travel time differs considerably, with the cab taking two and a half hours, and the
helicopter service taking around 45 minutes (taking into account the time involved in getting in and out of the helicopter), the cab service is way
cheaper (taking 2,000-3000 Rs. even if you were using a premium service) when compared to the helicopter service (costing 8,000 Rs.). Hence, I
believe that it is not viable.
In that case, what are some consequent recommendations you would like to offer?
The recommendations we can look into are:
Check whether you can have an inter-state service instead of an inter-city one (a more viable alternative to using the aeroplane for example)-
essentially making the service more viable by changing the geography up.
Focus on a particular section of people, which in this case would be the richest segment of the population and make the service available for
routes which can only be traversed through the air.
Great, thank you.

title: Pharmaceutical — McKinsey & company
prompt: Your client is a pharmaceutical company in the US market and they are planning on launching a new drug for Hepatitis-C, with the given competitors and customer base. The cure that is currently present in the market protects for a year. There are around 100,000 patients in the US annually. There are two alternative medicines available or this cure, both of which are mentioned below:- A generic medicine that does not have a patent and costs $1,000 annually for the treatment. Around 80% of the patients prefer to take this medicine. An innovative medicine made with newly introduced technology that has a patent and costs $20,000 annually. Talking about the market shares, 20% of patients prefer to go for this innovative alternative. These innovative ones are only used when doctors recommend them. Can you price the new drug accordingly with the help of a value-based analysis rather than using a cost-based or a competitive approach?
solution:
C: Sure. I'll need some information first. How are patients currently paying for these expensive treatments?
There is 100% insurance coverage from the companies.
Do we have patents for our products, generic and alternative ones? Also, how exactly do we go about the usage of these products and do they have
any apparent side effects?
There is a 20% chance of liver failure from hepatitis C, irrespective of the medicine you use. However, this is not fatal. So out of the 100,000
patients, 20,000 require liver transplants which usually cost an additional $30,000.
There are no side effects as such and the protection lasts for a lifetime, so there is no need to take this drug regularly. This is one unique thing
about it that would lead doctors to recommend this. We also have a patent taken out for this.
We can start by listing down the various factors and then compare the generic option, the innovative option, and the newer hepatitis drug that we
are launching. The factors here could be:
Cost
Side effects
Success ratio
Consistency
Annual requirement.
So, let'
s start by listing down the various factors and then compare the generic option, the innovative option, and the new hepatitis drug that we are
launching.
Okay, so what do you think the new drug should be priced at by taking into account its value aspect?
Since the person is mitigating the 20% chance of liver failure by using our product, it provides them with 100% assurance that they won't be facing
liver failure. They are essentially saving their lives and reducing their chances of dying by 20%. Here they're not limited by what the product costs but
by what the life insurance companies will pay, because they're going to end up bearing the whole cost, especially the price of our product. The
customers would of course be willing to consume our product because of the 100% efficiency that is provided.
Can you please elucidate?
Sure. The cost of living is ascertained by what the customer thinks their cost of living is, and not by their income-generating capabilities. This can be
garnered by taking into account the type of life insurance that they purchase. Here, life insurance companies, who are limited by what they can
payout to the people, play an imperative role. We can conclude that the value assigned to life by a particular person can be estimated based on the
value of life insurance that he/she buys. So here, we have to price it against the insurance companies.
How exactly will you proceed with that?
So as stated there are 1,00,000 Hepatitis-C patients. To find the base cost we will do the following calculation-
Generic drug - 80,000* 1,000 = 8,00,00,000
Innovative drug -20,000 * 20,000 = 40,00,00,000
In total, 48,00,00,000 comes out to be our base cost.
Further, to get the total amount that insurance companies have to reimburse these patients annually, we will estimate the costs that are required for
liver transplantation. There are 20,000 people out of the total 1,00,000 who end up facing liver failure.
So, required amount - 20,000 * 30,000 = 60,00,00,000
The total amount comes out to be 1,08,00,00,000 (60,00,00,000 + 48,00,00,000)
Lastly, we will divide this number by 1,00,000 to get the average patient reimbursement cost - 1,08,00,00,000/ 1,00,000 = 10,800.
What can you decipher from this?
It tells us that any price below the average cost of liver transplants would be appreciated by insurance companies, as it would lower their costs. The
new drug would likely have a large market share, as 80% of patients would not want to take the risk of liver failure. 171
Right. I think that will be it.
Brownie Points-
For value-based pricing, you generally peg the cost of the product to something that is similar or has the same level of utility as it.

title: Pharmaceuticals — BCG
prompt: Your client is a pharma company growing consistently over the last 4 years. Their main product is an over-the-counter drug similar to paracetamol. Their cost of goods sold has gone up. Suggest a remedy to turn around the situation.
solution:
C: Can I ask some preliminary questions?
Yes, sure, go ahead.
Since when have we been facing this issue? What is the magnitude of the increase?
We've been facing this for the last 1.5 years. The COGS has also gone up by 15%.
Firstly, I have divided the costs as per the value chain into the following categories:
Procurement
Production
Distribution
Do we have any information regarding which of the above has increased over the last 1.5 years?
Yes, let's focus on the procurement of raw materials.
Great. Under procurement, the costs could have gone up because of the following reasons:
The access to raw materials could have been impacted
Costs of inbound logistics could have gone high
There could have been significant costs associated with research and development
Do we know which of these has been an issue?
Let's focus on access to raw materials. What do you think could have been the cause of the high cost there?
Okay sure. I have assumed that the primary raw materials required to produce the drug are oil and sugar. Should I focus on other raw materials as
well?
No, let's focus on oil and sugar only.
Great. I would like to see this issue from the supply and demand perspective. It could either be that the supply of raw materials has been impacted
or the demand has significantly gone up, leading to higher prices. So should I focus on any of these in detail?
There has been an issue on the supply end.
Okay, so when we talk of supply, any of the following could have happened:
Shortage of raw materials because of external factors
Reduced production capacities of suppliers
Negatively impacted relationships with suppliers
Increased transit cost
Materials damaged in transit
What should I focus on?
That's a good breakup. However, let's focus on the first one. What do you think has changed in the last 1.5 years that might have led to a shortage
in raw materials?
The following have occurred in the last 1.5 years:
The Covid-19 crisis negatively impacted global supply chains leading to material shortages.
The Russia-Ukraine crisis severely impacted global markets and
access to raw materials, especially oil.
A culmination of these two situations has led to surging industrial raw material prices thereby leading to higher costs of goods sold.
Excellent. So what do you think can be done to turn around the situation?
The following can be done:
Firstly, we should localize our suppliers and see if we can sign agreements with them to supply exclusively to us.
We can put some thought into backward integration and see if we can internalize the entire material acquisition process through mergers and
acquisitions or joint ventures.
And third, we could turn to commodity derivatives to hedge raw material prices in the international market.
Good job. We can end the case here. Have a nice day!

title: Terrorism — Dalberg
prompt: You are working in a premises office and must reduce terrorism in India.
solution:
Sure. Could I start by understanding what kind of terrorism we are talking about? Issues in cyber security, increased number of bomb blasts and
threat to civilian safety? Also, have there been any significant events in the past few months? Any substantial incidence?
By terrorism, we mean shootings, bomb blasts etc. And there have been a few incidents in the last few months because of which authorities are
concerned.
Got it. Is there any timeline by which we need to reduce terrorism? Also are there any budgetary constraints?
No timeline and no constraints.
All right. We can look to reduce terrorism in 2 stages:
1) Preventive measures
2) Handling the situation
Under preventive measures, we can look at segmenting based on geography and following different strategies for Indian and Overseas terrorists:
For Indian terrorists: We can ban / regulation of guns and ammunition. Improve inter-state security by checking at borders. Have military
deployment at prominent places and airports for increased security, mandatory checking at crowded places and keeping a track record of the
professions of citizens.
For International terrorists: We should prevent mode of entry at airports, land or sea by strict military frisking. Their identification, locations of
travel, incoming country everything should be monitored. Their documents consisting of Visa, tickets, stay etc should be checked.
We can also analyze patterns of attack-
Festivals - As most of the attacks happen around festival times, police must be on high alert during such times
Places - Crowded markets, malls and hotels are prone to terrorist attacks and must be well protected.
Lastly, we should also focus on prediction and Training-
Special force - A unique team must exist that works solely on monitoring and anticipating terrorist activities.
Training - The military should be well prepared in terms of training and equipment to handle the situation better
Penalties: Extremely harsh penalties must be imposed on such activities It is important to note that sometimes one attack comprises multiple
bomb blasts. Hence, it is important to look at how situations can be handled. Additionally, if there is a good response procedure in place, it will
discourage future terrorism.
Under handling the situation, we can look at:
Identification of nearby key locations to the epicentre of the attack and issuing high alerts and evacuating of those areas
Reduction in regulatory barriers and requirement of permissions for immediate action
No media coverage must be allowed in times of terrorist attacks.
In the long run, we can also identify ways to reduce the number of terrorists and cut funding to notorious organizations.
Thank you, we can close the case now.

title: Banking — BCG
prompt: A bank' s struggling MSME division faces challenges including high Non-Performing Assets (NPA) and no growth and is looking to do risk- mitigated lending. With Naturals planning to expand from 100 franchises to 500, an MSME is seeking funds from the bank to open a Naturals franchise. The bank needs help with the following: 1. Assess if this is a good lending avenue. 2. How do we evaluate this? 3. How can Naturals support us in the event of default by the MSME borrower?
solution:
C: So, to get a clarification, our client is Naturals or the Bank?
Our client is the bank from which the MSME is seeking a loan.
Out of the three questions, the second one is how do we evaluate it? May I answer the second one first?
Yes, tell me how will you evaluate this?
So, I would like to evaluate this by making three divisions of the problem- pre-lending, during-lending, and post-lending.
1.
Pre-Lending:
The pre-lending stage addresses adverse selection by evaluating the credit risk of the borrower. This involves screening to reduce the risk of wrong
selection. Two types of factors are considered: MSME-related and specific brand-related.
MSME-related factors include the borrower's history, transaction record, existing loans, status, Cibil score, and a review of their financial statements.
Naturals brand-related factors can be categorized into internal and external factors.
External factors encompass the overall industry performance, such as the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on ice cream sales and the growing
health consciousness among consumers. It also includes assessing Naturals'market share compared to competitors.
Internal factors focus on Naturals'financial position, creditworthiness, and records of past franchisees. The performance of previous franchisees,
including any fluctuations or downward trends, is also considered.
2. During-Lending
This is the issue of moral hazard which includes ensuring the parties using loans for the intended purpose. To address this, the key focus is placed on
monitoring.
This would include determining 3 things:
Nature of audit that the bank needs to undertake
Medium of audit, i.e., physically visiting the franchise or requesting the franchise to send proof of performance to the bank
Frequency of audit - monthly quarterly, etc.
3. Post-Lending
In the post-lending stage, the focus is on addressing the question of how Naturals can assist in the event of borrower default. This issue arises
primarily when there is a non-performing asset (NPA) situation. To enhance the recovery process, I suggest three ways:
Recovery Agreement: Establishing an actionable comprehensive recovery agreement with clauses that clearly define the steps and strategies for
recovering the amount in case of default.
Shared liability model: Implementing a shared liability model where Naturals also shares a portion of the liability. This can be in the form of a
predetermined percentage (x%) that Naturals agrees to bear, while the bank takes responsibility for the remaining portion (100-x%).
Collateral: SME's assets such as the premises owned by the borrower where the franchise is in making can be used as collateral against the loan.
What role can Naturals play here?
I'll take the example of education loans where the borrower is the student, and there is a guarantor of the loan, typically parents or guardians. So, I
propose that in this case, Naturals can act as a guarantor to the borrower.
You mentioned that you will assess the performance of past franchisees. Currently, there are 100 existing franchises. I would like to understand
how you plan to benchmark and evaluate the performance of all these franchises. Additionally, I'
m curious to know whether you will evaluate all
of them, considering that out of the 100 franchises, 30 are incurring losses, 10 are at breakeven, and 60 are profitable.
There are 3 factors to consider for this:
Geography: Comparing franchises within the same state or locality, or considering factors such as whether they are located in metro areas or Tier
2 or Tier 3 cities, can serve as indicators of performance.
Size of the store outlet: Franchises with seating arrangements may have different revenue streams and profitability compared to those focused
on takeaway services.
Stage of operation: New franchises typically incur losses during the initial phase until they reach breakeven and start generating profits. It would be
unwise to directly compare a newly established franchise with one that has been operating for several years.
Great answer. That will be all, thank you!

title: Tractor — BCG
prompt: Your client is a leading tractor manufacturer and has been facing a decline in market share. You have been called to identify reasons for the same and give appropriate suggestions.
solution:
C: Before I begin with the solution can I ask some preliminary questions?
Yes, sure.
The company has been facing this decline for how long?
It has been 2 years and the decline in market share is around 1-2%.
What exactly is the product?
The product is tractors and before you ask, the problem doesn't lie with the product.
Who are the customers besides farmers?
Let's assume they are farmers only.
How many units do you sell per annum?
We sell around 10,00,000 tractors.
How is the competitive scenario?
We were around 30%, four other competitors at 10% each and others at 6-7%. The problem lies in the number of units sold and not the price.
Let'
s focus on appropriate levels of supply and demand. 182
How will you know that it is a supply and demand problem?
Since the problem is a decline in the market share and it has been cleared that the problem doesn't lie with the product but in the number of units
sold, I think it'
s a safe assumption that it lies within the supply and demand chain.
Fair enough, go ahead with it.
Starting with supply and demand, let'
s start unusually. I'll be presenting it in a production-distribution manner. For production, there is raw material
procurement, processing of the raw material and storage, after which we assemble the tractor, followed by product testing and then we finally have
packaging and dispatch. Now is there any problem in the plant?
No, you can focus on the distribution.
In distribution, we can focus on distribution channels or the end customers.
You should look at the customers.
So for this, we can calculate the number of customers per order. After this, there are 2-3 different routes which you can adopt. One is to do some sort
of segmentation of customers. We can also take customers as a whole. What approach do you want me to take?
Ok, so let'
s go ahead with the segmentation method. What are the types of segmentation that you can think of?
The first one that comes to mind is geographical, the other is customer size like high, low and medium value.
The problem is with geography. The north and the west are facing a drop in demand in India. What are the reasons that you can think of?
As it is a localised problem we should focus on the differences between the 2 regions. In the north and west, there can be 3 things, firstly some sort of
external disturbance, or some sort of crop pattern problem or lastly, there is an issue on the distribution side.
Yes, there is a problem with distributors. What could it be? 183
The distributors are diplomatic or they don't have too much cash. It is also possible that some competitors are offering them better deals.
Yes, you are very close to the actual cause of the problem cause.
The problem is with the distributors. So maybe they were not able to store the stock because of financial constraints.
What would be your recommendations for the same?
The distributors need to be spoken to about what exactly the constraints are and figure out a way that the company can smoothen the process. An
example of this constraint can be that distributors have low liquidity due to deferred payments from the farmers which stops them from purchasing
new tractors. Here the client can provide tractors on a BNPL basis to the distributors. Also, exploring other distribution channels in the region is a
viable option.
Yes, this is it for the case. Thank you.
Brownie Points-
The candidate could have paused at certain areas and thought of a better structure.
This was a pressure interview, don't let it get to you.
Stick to the logic.

title: Policy Implementation — Kearney
prompt: You have been approached by the Government of India to develop a government programme and you have three objectives for this - 1. Identify three states for proper implementation. 2. Identify the unemployment rate 3. Discuss 3-4 ways through which employment can be driven up in the next 4-5 years
solution:
C: So we begin by taking into account three different funnels -
1.
Factors that influence employment,
2.
Policies that are already offered in the state and
3.
Which state had the highest impact?
The first funnel i.e. factors that influence employment are -
1.
Literacy rate, as states with higher literacy rates would have a lower unemployment rate and vice versa and would require more employment
programmes,
2.
The next one is the working-age population and a state having a higher working-age population would need more such programmes, and
3.
The last one is the skillset of the people in the state. After this, we will have a fair idea of what our top 3 states might look like.
Moving on to the second funnel i.e. policies implemented.
The sub-factors that should be taken into consideration here are the number of policies implemented, the reach of these policies and their
conversion rates; these factors would how to convey how these past policies have helped people in the past and the number of people who have
availed the benefits of these policies.
The third and final funnel is related to figuring out the population size of the states. A state with a larger population should be placed higher on the
preference list as the target population is greater. For this, we can apply a simple formula
The working population of the state = No of People willing to work at the given wage rate/Total Population of the State*100
Higher this Percentage, the Better the potential of the state for policy implementation.
How would you calculate the unemployment rate of a state?
So for this, are we looking at people who don't have jobs at all or is there some other group that I should keep in mind?
We are only looking at people who are employable and don't have jobs right now
Which age category are we looking at?
Let's look at people between the ages of 23-28
So for calculating the unemployment rate of a state we should start with the population of the state and divide it into different categories based on
age.
Through this, we get populations in categories of
1.
0-18,
2.
8-65 and
>65.
3. 186
We can later on also apply another filter to find the population in the age group of 23-28 and divide it into rural and urban groups as employability
differs in urban and rural areas. Now we can further divide this subcategory into different income groups like -
below the poverty level, middle income and higher income.
Next, we take a split for the number of people who attended school and also look for a population that has the skills we require for the
implementation of our policy. These skills can be IT skills, knowledge of mechanics etc.
Finally, we look at the people who are unemployed under the required category.
Okay, we can close the case now.

title: MSME — Dalberg
prompt: What are the ramifications of air pollution?
solution:
Trying to follow a MECE approach, the following are the ramifications of air pollution:
I. Health Effects: Respiratory problems; Cardiovascular problems etc (like, asthma, cancer, strokes etc)
II. Environmental Effects: Climate Change (Global Warming); Ecosystem Damage (Acid Rain, Deforestation) etc
III. Economic Effects: Healthcare Costs; Lost Productivity; Damage to Infrastructure; Impact on Tourism
IV. Social Effect: Disproportionate impact on marginalized groups; reduced quality of life; public perception and awareness
Let'
s particularly focus on the Economic side of the impact. What specific industries would be affected by air pollution? Devise a framework to
understand the same.
Should I focus on the negative or the positive impacts of air pollution on different industries?
Let's focus on the negative for now.
There would be some industries affected directly and some indirectly. For instance, those affected directly would be:
> Healthcare Industry: Increased Healthcare Costs, Demand for Medical Equipment/supplies
> Agriculture Industry: Reduced Crop Yield, Lower productivity
> Energy Industry: Increased costs for air pollution controls, Deceased energy efficiency
Some other industries impacted indirectly would be Manufacturing, Transportation, Tourism etc.
While trying to make a framework, we can see various metrics and bases such as maximum pressure created on all industries, which industry's
market size is reducing, which industry's market size is increasing, productivity etc.
For example, in the healthcare industry, increased levels of air pollution lead to higher instances of respiratory and cardiovascular diseases, which
will increase the demand for medical services, resources, and manpower. This industry will require more resources and manpower to tackle the
health impact of air pollution.
Moreover, when we look from the perspective of the market size we observe:
Increasing Market Size:
1. Air Purifiers and Masks: With the increase in air pollution, people are becoming more conscious of the need to protect themselves from harmful
pollutants. This trend has led to the growth of the air purifier and mask market.
2. Renewable Energy Industry: With the increasing focus on reducing air pollution, the demand for renewable energy sources such as solar and wind
power is on the rise. This trend is likely to lead to an increase in the market size of the renewable energy industry.
Decreasing Market Size:
1. Automotive Industry: With the rise in air pollution, the demand for electric vehicles, hybrid cars, and other low-emission transportation options is on
the rise. This trend will lead to a decline in demand for traditional gasoline-powered cars and trucks.
2. Fossil Fuel Industry: The increasing focus on reducing air pollution has led to the growth of renewable energy sources such as solar and wind
power. This trend is likely to reduce demand for fossil fuels, which could impact the market size of this industry.
Further, elaborate upon the healthcare industry.
The healthcare industry is particularly affected by air pollution because it puts pressure on public healthcare infrastructure, which primarily serves
underprivileged people and blue-collar workers who cannot afford private healthcare. As a result, there will be a need for more resources,
infrastructure, and personnel, such as hiring more doctors and healthcare workers. The impact of pollution on health reduces productivity and
increases
the demand for healthcare services, leading to a strain on the industry, especially the public healthcare sector.
Drawing from the insights mentioned above, we can also talk about the blue-collar workers'industry, which operates in an informal structure and is
also affected by air pollution. The workers in this industry are particularly vulnerable to the harmful effects of pollution due to their prolonged
exposure to it. The impact on their health not only reduces their productivity but also leads to increased healthcare costs for the industry.
Giving examples from several other industries:
Air pollution may not have a significant impact on the education industry, but might be hampering underprivileged children.
It can affect the retail industry. Retail businesses may experience reduced footfall due to people staying indoors to avoid exposure to pollution, which
can impact sales and revenue.
Elaborate a little bit more on the impact on tourism as well.
The Impact on tourism can be explained as follows:
The impact of air pollution on the tourism industry can be measured in terms of the decrease in tourist arrivals and tourism revenue. This can be
quantified by analysing data on tourist arrivals, hotel occupancy rates, and revenue generated by tourism-related businesses.
Causes:
Air pollution in tourist destinations can be caused by several factors, such as industrial activities, vehicular emissions, and open burning. These
sources of pollution can lead to poor air quality in the destination, which can deter tourists from visiting.
This can lead to a decrease in tourist arrivals and tourism revenue, which can have ripple effects on other businesses such as hotels, restaurants,
and tour operators.
Okay, we can close the case now.

title: BCG Oil and Gas — BCG Oil and Gas
prompt: Your client is an Oil and Gas provider who's facing a high attrition rate in their Mumbai office. Analyze the problem and give solutions to turn around the situation.
solution:
C: For how long has the company been facing this problem?
3 years
What is the degree of the churn rate?
28%
Is this problem faced industry-wide or is it specific to our client?
We don't know.
How many employees does the Mumbai office have and what does the hierarchy look like?
The office has 50 engineers, all of them are salaried people. An engineer during job hours is either supposed to be in the office or at the Rigs.
Where is the office located in Mumbai and what are the facilities for reaching the rigs?
The office is located in the Fort area and the company provides the transportation to the rigs.
How many offices do the company have across the globe and are all offices facing a similar issue?
The company has offices in 85 countries and has about 14,500 employees. No, the other offices are not facing this problem and it is only specific
to the Mumbai office.
Do we have any data on the number of employees at the rigs and in the office?
OIL AND GAS
The company owns 25 rigs and about 36% of them require the presence of at least 2 field engineers and 1 standby engineer on the rig at a given
point in time.
Got it. Given the calculation, I can infer that 9 rigs require the presence of engineers, with 3 engineers on each rig, 27 engineers are required on the
rigs and 23 would be present in the office.
Yes, that's right. An engineer at the rig also gets a bonus. It is observed that the engineers placed in the office often tend to take leaves.
Got it, I would like to analyze the reasons for the high attrition rate as per three broad parameters - Internal reasons (organization-specific factors),
External reasons (competition/ other opportunities) and Personal reasons.
Sounds good, go ahead
The Internal reasons concerned with:
Monetary aspects: Compensation and Perks Bonus
Non-monetary aspects: Management, Work atmosphere, Safety, Travel Time, Job Security, Motivation, Safety, Working Hours, Work Pressure and
Facilities
Other opportunities: work/skill enhancement and Better work/pay outside
The person factors that come into play are Personal Growth, Social factors and Cultural factors
Yes, you pointed out right, people have been leaving the firm because of other opportunities but not necessarily other jobs. Can you think of any
other opportunities?
Other opportunities can be - higher education, skill enhancement programs by the government, diploma courses, skill enhancement workshops, etc.
OIL AND GAS
Right, a lot of the employees leaving the firm are pursuing their higher education - postgraduate degrees to get better work opportunities. We
also know that about 90% of the engineers employed should be at the rigs as per industry standards. Currently, the company has 27 minimum
required engineers + 16 more at the rigs. Calculate the effective workforce utilization under the present circumstance.
Current utilization = (27+16/50)*100 = 86% Ideal utilization = 90% (at rigs) It shows us that there is a less than effective utilization of the engineers at
rigs because of which they might be going for higher education for better career development.
I: Right, can you give us solutions to this problem?
C: Sure, I would like to give my solutions in the form of two major areas to be tackled.
1. Selection
1.
Find the right talent with set criteria for educational requirements to avoid high attrition.
2.
Sign a minimum-term contract with the employees.
2. Retention
1.
Offer in-house training and skill enhancement workshops.
2.
Partner with other offices across the globe to ensure better effective utilization of the workforce.
3.
Offer higher education support; with a contract of joining back the firm.
4.
Invest in research and development to increase the capacity of the rigs for better returns and consequently better employee utilization.
Great solution. That'll be all. Thank you!

title: MSME — Dalberg
prompt: You have to analyze the problems faced by the MSME sector because of covid.
solution:
C: So MSME contributes to nearly 27% of GDP, and during the Covid times, the entire economy saw a hit, especially this sector, because it is still in a
development stage and cash flows were limited. We can divide the entire MSME sector into three parts. Agriculture, Manufacturing, and service
sector. Manufacturing can be further divided into labour and capital-intensive industries. Lastly, the service sector can be divided into two parts that
are in-person services like tailoring, fruit and vegetable vendors, etc. And online services like education institutes, call centres, etc. Before proceeding
further, is this division appropriate?
Yes, please proceed to the problems.
Starting with the agriculture industry. The crop yield and related activities did not see a decline because the food supply was essential at that time.
However, production in allied sectors had declined significantly, especially in the poultry sector, followed by fisheries and Sheep/Goat/Pig (S/G/P)
sector, primarily due to the widespread fear circulating in the wake of COVID-19 regarding the safety of non-vegetarian food. Following up the
movement of vehicles was restricted thus the supply chain for this sector was broken. Agricultural inputs like pesticides and fertilizers were limited,
marketing channels like mandis, and haats were not operational and microfinance credit saw a decline as well. Other issues were increases in the
price level of raw materials, unemployment, production level, consumer demand and disruptions in supply chains. Can I proceed to another sector?
Yes, please.
Coming to manufacturing we saw huge unemployment in labor-intensive industries because of lack of demand and lower cash flows. There was a
capital crunch, higher cost of transactions, low footfall, near zero imports, and hindrance in exports. As for capital-intensive industries working capital
was limited, delayed payments were observed, increase in default rate and low creditworthiness. For sustaining the business, the best way out was
shifting to e-commerce platforms and adapting to digital technology which required giving away past savings/assets and becoming more liquid.
Can I jump to the last sector?
Please move forward,
The online service sector saw a boom with more players joining in and turning to digital support. As for in-person services, there was a decline
because of the same reasons like low demand, disruption in the supply chain, lack of credit availability and unemployment etc. I would like to put a
special focus on the financial sector which includes banks and NBFCs. Massive changes in liquidity position and their ability to manage debt
servicing in the short term. The long-term impact of the shutdown was seen in their asset quality, business volumes and profitability levels. The loans
of retail NBFCs are granular in nature and largely cater to the self-employed borrower segment where the cash-flows are correlated to the economic
activity levels and therefore, inherently more volatile.
That's informative. Moving on we have a case study challenge. A large development bank wants to enter the market. Which sectors should it
target to maximize the impact generated? Also, mention the financial and non-financial help it can offer.
I think the development bank can focus on the manufacturing sector especially labour-intensive industries and enterprises because they were the
worst affected by covid. This can include small construction companies, factories that produce daily FMCGs, the textile industry, and the electronic
products market.
Fine. Could you guesstimate the number of MSMEs operating in India to get more clarity on funds required by such a development bank?
Okay. So, I would like to ask a few clarifying questions. Can I assume the population of India to be 140 crores and the average family size to be 5?
Although families in cities are nuclear and majorly comprise 3 people whereas in rural areas families are joint and comprise more than 5. The
effective number of households will become 140/5= 28 crore families. Is that a fair assumption?
Yes, these are fair assumptions.
Moving on I would like to divide this population based on Urban Rural categories. Urban comprises 40% which makes it equal to 11.2 families and rural
comprises 60% which makes it equal to 16.8 families. Approximately 11 and 17 crore families. Is that a fair assumption?
Yes, you can carry on.
In the rural majority of people would be engaged in agriculture which is a major part of MSME. I assume 20% have their farm and allied business, 50%
are employed in other farm or allied services and the remaining 30% can be divided among people who are employed in factories or secondary
sector activities (20%) and people who have their processing units (10%). So effectively only 30% of families will be having an MSME, which is equal to
30 times 17 crore divided by 100. Comes out to be 5.1 crore families.
Now in urban settings, we can divide based on income groups- 10% higher income slab, 50% middle-income slab and 40% lower slab. I eliminate the
lower 40% because they are people like daily wage earners, and rickshaw pullers doing petty jobs and would not be having a business of their own.
Yes, continue.
So, the middle 50% comes out to 5.5 crore families and the higher 10% comes out to 1.1 crore families. Approximately 6 crore and 1 crore families. Now
in the middle 50%, we can assume that only 10% of families would be having business whereas the remaining would be employed in the service or
tertiary sector. But in the upper segment, this trend would be reversed with 8% of families owning a business and the remaining 2% working at top
positions with very high pay levels. Another thing to be kept in mind should be that the *% of the upper segment would be established businesses
and shouldn't be considered as MSME. So effectively only 10% of the middle segment would be having an MSME business, which comes out to be
10*6/100= 0.6 crore families.
Thus, the total number of families running an MSME would be equal to the total number of MSMEs in the country, which is 5.1+0.6= 5.7 crore MSMEs.
Approximately 6 crore MSME industries in all sectors that is agriculture, manufacturing, tertiary services etc.
Great work. Thank You.

title: WILDLIFE SANCTUARY — BCG
prompt: The Government of India has decided to reintroduce the cheetah into Indian territories that got extinct in the late 1940s. The program for the same has been named by the Government of India as Project Cheetah. You need to make sure that Project Cheetah is a success.
solution:
C: Sure, I would like to begin by understanding the criteria for success for Project Cheetah first. I would also like to know if there are any living
cheetahs in India right now.
For the project to be a success, there should be around 50-70 stable populations of cheetahs in the Indian territory within 10-12 years. As for the
second question, there are no living cheetahs in India right now.
Understood. After importing, where will the cheetahs be placed?
They will be placed in a Wildlife Sanctuary and other protected zones that lie under the purview of the government.
Okay. May I also know about the life expectancy of a cheetah? And what is the reproductive age and reproductive cycle for them?
The life expectancy of a cheetah is 15 years. They come into reproductive age when they are 2 years old and only the female cheetah can bear a
child. The gestation period for a baby is 6 months, and for the next 18 months, the female cheetah cannot reproduce again.
And what is the count of cubs that a female cheetah can produce in one pregnancy?
3 cubs with a survival rate of 50%.
Okay. For this project to be a success, I would like to consider the factors of the total population of cheetahs to be sourced, their survival rates, and
their death rates. Do we also have some data about the quality of cheetahs that we are importing, and if they can habituate to the Indian climate?
How many cheetahs do we need to import? What should be the male-to-female ratio?
I would like you to try and calculate the number of cheetahs to be imported from the information that you have already received.
All right. So, 10 years are the total reproductive years for a female cheetah. For 24 months (6 months for gestation of the baby and 18 months when
she cannot get pregnant) a female cheetah cannot reproduce again.
Yes, Go on
So, there are 5 reproductive cycles in the lifetime of a female cheetah, and in one reproductive cycle, she can produce 3*0.5=1.5 cubs which come
out to be 5*1.5 = 7.5 cubs in her lifetime. For Project Cheetah to be successful there need to be 50 stable populations of cheetah in 10- 12 years. Is it
okay to assume the period available for us as 10 years and the number of cubs produced by a female cheetah in her lifecycle as 7?
Yes, you may.
Okay, so as per the given information, around 7 female cheetahs will be required.
Good, Now Calculate the number of male cheetahs required.
Yes sir. Is there a possibility of one male cheetah producing cubs with only one female cheetah or shall I consider the possibility of one male cheetah
producing cubs with many female cheetahs?
Consider the possibility of one male cheetah reproducing with many female cheetahs. Rather, let us assume the number of male cheetahs to be
3.
Okay Sir, so the total number of cheetahs that need to be imported from Africa will be 10 with 7 females and 3 males. They along with their cubs will
lead to a stable population of 50 cheetahs in 10 years in India.
Yes. Now continue to the survival aspect.
Okay, so for the number of cheetahs expected to survive, we can divide it into two categories about the survival chances of the adult cheetah and
the survival chances of the newborn cheetahs. To reach our target rate we need to increase the survival rate of newborn cheetahs from 50%
What are the measures that you will take to achieve this goal?
Since the adult cheetahs are being placed at wildlife sanctuaries, the quality of employees serving the cheetahs and the surveillance can be
improved to increase their survival rates. Government interference in the form of vaccines, medications, and annual check-ups can also go a long
way in increasing the survival rates of cheetahs.
Fair enough. What criteria will you use to increase the survival rate of newborn cheetahs?
We can keep a check on the key resources such as food, water, and shelter and ensure that they are available for the newborn cheetah along with
medical intervention in the form of vaccines.
Okay, you may proceed to the last aspect of the problem now.
C: Yes sir. The last aspect of this framework involves the death chances of the cheetah. The causes of death may be Natural or Artificial. Within
natural death, there may be internal or external causes. Internal causes may include the death of the cheetahs due to old age. External causes may
include some disease, birthing issue, malnutrition, and/or chronic illness, etc. The Artificial causes of death will include death due to poaching,
hunting, mistreatment by humans, etc.
What are the ways by which the chances of death of the cheetahs through both natural and artificial means can be reduced?
Death rates of Cheetahs can be reduced by the formation of stricter laws by the government along with their strict implementation. Natural deaths
rates may be reduced by regular check-ups and medications for the Cheetahs
Good. You have covered all the aspects of the problem statement. We can close the case now.
